courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Current topics are American Building Construction, American Architecture from a Preservation Perspective, Research Methods.

courses.title: Introduction to Historic Preservation
courses.description: History, evolution, modern concepts, and professional techniques of historic preservation.

courses.title: Transportation and Preservation
courses.description: Addresses compliance issues with preservation laws relating to transportation development. Includes understanding processes and negotiating needs of the resource with the needs of transportation authorities.

courses.title: American Architecture from a Preservation Perspective I
courses.description: American built environment from the colonial era to the federal period. Stylistic development and building analysis for preservation; cultural, historical, and physical contexts within building forms.

courses.title: American Architecture from a Preservation Perspective II
courses.description: American built environment from the federal period to 1893. Stylistic development and building analysis for preservation; cultural, historical, and physical contexts within building forms.

courses.title: American Architecture from a Preservation Perspective III
courses.description: American built environment from 1893 to the present. Stylistic development and building analysis for preservation; cultural, historical, and physical contexts within building forms.

courses.title: Vernacular Architecture
courses.description: Examines the characteristics and origins of vernacular building types and explores the cultural meanings of these sites, particularly in light of contemporary ideas about socioeconomic issues.

courses.title: National Register Nomination
courses.description: Provides an overview of the National Register of Historic Places (NRHP) and the processes for documenting, evaluating, and nominating properties to the register.

courses.title: Building Pathology: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines the scientific fundamentals of material properties in building construction and the application of that knowledge to the preservation of historic buildings.

courses.title: Legal Issues in Historic Preservation
courses.description: Examines constitutional, statutory, and common law affecting historic preservation. Covers First Amendment, eminent domain, due process, police powers, regulatory "takings," and aesthetic zoning.

courses.title: Preservation Ethics
courses.description: Examines professional ethics in the field of historic preservation based on theories of public policy.

courses.title: Preservation Economics
courses.description: Introduces economics of historic preservation projects and the financial techniques and incentives used to rehabilitate historic properties in the United States.

courses.title: Historic Survey and Inventory Methodology
courses.description: Examines how historic inventories help communities plan for wise use of historic resources. Includes complete reconnaissance and survey documentation for historic properties and development of historic context statement.

courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Accounting for Entrepreneurs
courses.description: Sources and uses of cash in the context of start-up and small firms. Emphasis on cash generated by operations and used for operations and growth. Secondary emphasis on external sources of cash.

courses.title: Intermediate Accounting I
courses.description: Concepts and principles of financial accounting, including U.S. and international financial reporting standards; analysis of alternatives for income measurement and asset and liability valuation.

courses.title: Intermediate Accounting II
courses.description: Concepts and principles of financial accounting, including U.S. and international financial reporting standards. Analysis of alternatives for income measurement and asset and liability valuation.

courses.title: Intermediate Accounting III
courses.description: Concepts and principles of financial accounting, including U.S. and international financial reporting standards; analysis of alternatives for income measurement and asset and liability valuation.

courses.title: Cost Accounting
courses.description: Development and communication of cost information to assist in planning, motivating managers, controlling costs, and evaluating performance.

courses.title: Professional Ethics for Accountants
courses.description: This course presents the ethical standards of the accounting profession with emphasis on contemporary issues confronting accountants and auditors including their social and ethical responsibilities.

courses.title: Auditing
courses.description: The audit environment, examinations of financial statements, and the audit process. Includes professional standards, audit sampling, and the audit profession; concepts underlying professional ethics for auditors.

courses.title: Advanced Financial Accounting
courses.description: Accounting for equity; financial accounting and reporting for corporate consolidation.

courses.title: Government & Not for Profit Accounting
courses.description: This course teaches differences in accounting and financial reporting for governmental and not-for-profit organizations and differences between private sector and public-sector accounting, standards-setting, meeting stakeholder needs.

courses.title: Federal Taxation
courses.description: Federal income tax law covering primarily the taxation of individuals with a focus on business invoice and property transactions. Introduction to tax planning.

courses.title: Accounting Data Analytics I
courses.description: Focuses on the increased use of data analytics within the accounting profession, including an understanding of data analytic thinking, terminology and application.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Developing the Business Professional, International Accounting, Tax Pass-Through Entities.

courses.title: Financial Accounting
courses.description: Introduces the accounting model and financial statements for external users. Emphasizes the use of accounting information in valuation and performance evaluation.

courses.title: Tax Research
courses.description: Introduces tax law, tax authority, and how to research complex tax issues. Emphasizes development of professional research memorandums and advising clients on tax strategies.

courses.title: Taxation of Business
courses.description: Taxation of business entities (C corporations, partnerships, S corporations, and limited liability companies) as they form, operate, and dissolve.

courses.title: Taxes and Business Strategy
courses.description: How to use economic analysis as a tax planning tool, thereby incorporating tax factors in economic decisions.

courses.title: Taxation of Pass Through Entities
courses.description: Designed for accountants, managers, and entrepreneurs, covers basics of taxation of partnerships, S corporations and their shareholders, and trusts and their beneficiaries. Also covers taxation of exempt entities. Students cannot receive credit for both ACTG 619 and LAW 681 .

courses.title: Accounting Measurement and Disclosure
courses.description: Recent Financial Accounting Standings Board decisions; current measurement and disclosure conflicts facing the accounting profession. Includes exposure to governmental and not-for-profit accounting issues.

courses.title: Financial Statement Analysis and Valuation
courses.description: Examines the role of accounting information in financial decisions. Highlights valuation's relationship to accounting earnings and book value.

courses.title: Advanced Assurance Services
courses.description: Knowledge and application of generally accepted accounting principles and generally accepted auditing standards systems, design and flow charting, work paper preparation and review, oral and written presentation, and application of judgment.

courses.title: Strategic Cost Management
courses.description: Theory and application of management accounting techniques to decisions made under uncertainty in complex business environments.

courses.title: Accounting Data and Analytics II
courses.description: Leveraging skills and knowledge developed in ACTG 580 , this course focuses on enabling students to successfully engage with an iterative process to more effectively perform and communicate analytics across increasingly complex scenarios, producing actionable results that consider risks related to data quality, privacy and misinterpretation.

courses.title: Accounting Data and Analytics III
courses.description: Leveraging prior coursework, accounting students are challenged to initiate and complete end-to-end data analytics projects, with more reliance on self-directed problem solving, incorporating aspects of visual perception and principles of design to tell stories with data that influence business leaders through actionable insights. Sequence with ACTG 580 , ACTG 681 .

courses.title: Developing the Accounting Professional I
courses.description: Developing the Accounting Professional I is the first course in a two course series. This course equips students with essential communication and leadership skills and is intended to be “applied” and/or “hands on.” The course fosters change agility, effective business communication, and a growth mindset for continuous learning in dynamic professional environments. Class discussion will focus on real problems and potential approaches used by accounting professionals. Sequence with ACTG 692 .

courses.title: Developing the Accounting Professional II
courses.description: Developing the Accounting Professional II is the second course in a two course series. This class equips students with essential career advancement skills and fosters change agility, effective business communication, and a growth mindset for continuous learning in dynamic professional environments. This class is intended to be “applied” and/or “hands on.” Class discussion will focus on real problems and potential approaches used by accounting professionals. Sequence with ACTG 691 .

courses.title: Introductory Academic Oral Communication
courses.description: Focuses on strategies to improve aural-oral academic communication through discussions, seminars, dialogue, videos, and lectures. Sequence with AEIS 102 .

courses.title: Advanced Academic Oral Communication
courses.description: Focuses on strategies to improve aural-oral academic communication through discussions, seminars, dialogue, and presentations. Sequence with AEIS 101 .

courses.title: Reading Academic Discourse
courses.description: Focuses on interactive reading of academic text, building reading strategies for better comprehension, speed, and confidence, and developing critical reading skills.

courses.title: Introductory Academic Writing
courses.description: Introduces conventions of expository essay writing. Emphasizes clear, effective written communication and development of editing skills. Covers grammar in context. Sequence with AEIS 111 , AEIS 112 .

courses.title: Intermediate Academic Writing
courses.description: Intermediate writing for nonnative speakers of English. Critical analysis of academic texts leading to summary, paraphrase, essay-examination responses, and expository essays. Sequence with AEIS 110 , AEIS 112 .

courses.title: Advanced Academic Writing
courses.description: Advanced writing for non-native speakers of English. Critical reading of academic texts for response in various academic modes: reporting research, critical analysis, and argumentation. Sequence with AEIS 110 , AEIS 111 .

courses.title: Introduction to Traditional Ecological Knowledge
courses.description: Traditional ecological knowledge (TEK) includes moral precepts that guide interaction with the local ecosystem, skill and knowledge sets used for resource extraction and management, and expressive media used for knowledge transmission. This course integrates Indigenous and Western perspectives to examine TEK in global perspective.

courses.title: Anthropology of Pirates and Piracy
courses.description: Examines the political and economic origins and legacies of piracy through 500 years of history in the Americas, Europe, and Africa.

courses.title: Anthropology and Aliens
courses.description: Examines how anthropology and speculative fiction have mutually constituted each other historically as each explores culture and society, and what makes us human.

courses.title: Principles of Archaeology
courses.description: Introduction to archaeology methods and interpretation.

courses.title: World Archaeology
courses.description: Introduction to prehistoric societies and cultural change through the examination of archaeological case studies from around the world. Taught once or more per academic year.

courses.title: Introduction to Cultural Anthropology
courses.description: A first look into the work of cultural anthropology and an introduction to the cultural diversity of the world.

courses.title: Origins of Storytelling
courses.description: Application of evolutionary thinking to the origins and function of literature.

courses.title: Sexuality and Culture
courses.description: Examines sexuality through the historical, cultural, economic, and political factors that contribute to the construction of sexual identities, relationships, and institutions.

courses.title: Introduction to Human Origins
courses.description: Homo sapiens as a living organism; biological evolution and genetics; fossil hominids.

courses.title: Introduction to Monkeys and Apes
courses.description: Evolutionary biology of the primates: the fossil record and ecology in the age of mammals, primate anatomy, locomotor feeding adaptations, taxonomic relations, primate ethology, primate conservation

courses.title: Evolution of Human Sexuality
courses.description: Includes basic genetics, physiology, and behavior. Evolution of sex, of the sexes, and of the role of sex in mammal, primate, and human behavior.

courses.title: Evolutionary Medicine
courses.description: Focuses on the application of evolutionary thinking to the study of human health and disease.

courses.title: Introduction to Forensic Anthropology
courses.description: Introduction to human skeletal analysis and its application in a legal context, using biological and anthropological approaches to the recovery and identificationof human remains.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Laboratory Projects: [Topic]
courses.description: An experience in a lab setting in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content master.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Business Anthropology
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive overview of business anthropology on a range of topics including entrepreneurship, finance capital, multinational corporations, and race/gender in business. Readings and discussions will focus on how to apply anthropological knowledge to solve real-world business problems.

courses.title: Introduction to Nutritional Anthropology
courses.description: Human nutrition from a biocultural anthropological perspective, including the relationship of food consumption patterns to evolution, contemporary issues relating to malnutrition, and diseases of nutrition.

courses.title: Island Archaeology
courses.description: Provides an in-depth understanding about the prehistory of various island regions in a comparative perspective as seen in the material culture and how islands help archaeologists decipher patterns of changing human behavior over time, including migration processes, settlement patterns, environmental interactions, and exchange systems.

courses.title: Atlantis, Aliens, and Archaeology
courses.description: Critically examines pseudoscientific examples of archaeology using case studies from around the world (e.g. the lost city of Atlantis, ancient aliens) and explores how proper scientific archaeological research is conducted.

courses.title: Domestic Animals
courses.description: Explores human relationships with domestic animals, examining the domestication process and the effects of animal domestication on human society.

courses.title: Introduction to Biological Anthropology
courses.description: Examines the biological aspects of the human species from comparative, ecological, and evolutionary perspectives. Explores theoretical and methodological issues in biological anthropology.

courses.title: Animals and People
courses.description: Explores contemporary and historical examples of human-animal interactions around the globe from a cross-cultural perspective. We analyze the influences of culture and biology on these interactions, explore perspectives, and engage in hypothesis testing.

courses.title: Science, Race, and Society
courses.description: Understanding past scientific attitudes on racial variation helps place modern concepts of human diversity and racial segregation in a broader anthropological and scientific context.

courses.title: Anthropology of Globalization
courses.description: Introduces students to a wide range of issues related to economic, cultural, and ideological aspects of globalization.

courses.title: Gender, Folklore, Inequality
courses.description: Cross-cultural exploration of the expressive and artistic realm of women's lives. Topics include life-cycle rituals, religion, healing, verbal arts, crafts, and music.

courses.title: Native North Americans
courses.description: Interpretive approach to accomplishments, diversity, and survival of precontact, postcontact, and present-day American Indian peoples. Impact of Euro-American stereotypes on politics and identity.

courses.title: Anthropology of the United States
courses.description: In addition to accounting for the rise of the United States as an object of anthropological inquiry, this course explores the culture and political-economy of life in the contemporary United States, with a focus on gender, race, class, faith, migration, and inequality.

courses.title: Immigration and Farmworkers
courses.description: Mexican farmworkers in the United States, their history and living and working conditions explored within the political culture of immigration. Introductory social science course recommended.

courses.title: Hunters and Gatherers
courses.description: Survey of contemporary hunter-gatherer societies. Foraging, decision-making, exchange, prestige, marriage, gender roles, parenting, history, and demography in an ecological and evolutionary perspective.

courses.title: Cultures of India and South Asia
courses.description: Survey of contemporary South Asia's religious and cultural diversity, issues of ethnic identity, gender construction, social conflict, and politics of poverty.

courses.title: Human Attraction and Mating Strategies
courses.description: Evolutionary theory, experimental and real-world data illuminate what we find attractive in others, variation in who we are attracted to, and why.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Archaeology
courses.description: Methods modern archaeology uses to reconstruct the past, including background research, field methods, laboratory analyses, and interpreting data.

courses.title: Food Origins
courses.description: Biological, ecological, and social dimensions of plant-animal domestication and the environmental impact of agriculture in the Late Pleistocene-Holocene epochs.

courses.title: Archaeology of Egypt and Near East
courses.description: The archaeology of ancient Egypt and the Near East.

courses.title: Pacific Islands Archaeology
courses.description: Archaeology and prehistoric cultural development of Pacific island peoples from earliest settlement through early Western contact. Emphasizes Southeast Asian cultural foundations and ecological adaptations.

courses.title: Oregon Archaeology
courses.description: Native American cultural history of Oregon based on archaeological evidence. Environmental and ecological factors that condition human adaptations and contemporary cultural resource protection.

courses.title: Archaeology of East Asia
courses.description: Explores the evolution of diverse cultures and ethnic identities in East Asia during prehistoric and early historical times.

courses.title: Archaeology of Southeast Asia
courses.description: Explores the evolution of the diverse cultures of Southeast Asia during the prehistoric and early historic periods.

courses.title: Origins of Art
courses.description: Examines prehistoric and recent hunter-gatherer art to understand the role that art behavior played in ancestral human life.

courses.title: Decolonizing Archaeology
courses.description: This course examines the development of decolonizing practices within the field of archaeology, otherwise known as Indigenous archaeology, collaborative archaeology, community-engaged archaeology, and community-based participatory research. The course will provide a global perspective of Indigenous archaeology case studies and outline similarities and differences in practice in Indigenous archaeology(ies).

courses.title: Human Evolution
courses.description: Fossil evidence of human evolution; Homo sapiens' place among the primates; variability of populations of fossil hominids.

courses.title: Human Biological Variation
courses.description: Genetic and biological structure of human populations; population dynamics and causes of diversity; analysis of genetically differentiated human populations and their geographic distribution.

courses.title: Food and Culture
courses.description: Anthropological approach to the role of nutrients in human development (individual and group); cultural determinants and differences among populations; world food policy; applied nutritional anthropology.

courses.title: Human Osteology Laboratory
courses.description: Introduction to the human skeleton from the perspective of biological anthropology. This course uses anatomical, developmental, and evolutionary approaches to understand fundamental aspects of human biology in general, as well as the individuals represented by skeletal remains.

courses.title: Human Growth and Development
courses.description: Examines key issues in human and nonhuman primate growth and development; addresses genetic, social and ecological determinants of variation in growth.

courses.title: Psychoactive Substances in Ancient Societies
courses.description: Global review of psychoactive substances in past human societies, including the paraphernalia, iconography, and residues of drugs found in the archaeological record.

courses.title: Decoding Your Genome
courses.description: Explores what genomic data can tell us about human variation and evolution, and discusses how genomics is currently used in our daily lives.

courses.title: Field Methods in Cultural Anthropology
courses.description: Techniques of participant observation, community definition and extension, nondirective interviewing, and establishing rapport. Provides theoretical perspectives and emphasizes investigator's ethical responsibilities.

courses.title: Culture, Illness, and Healing
courses.description: Cultural foundations of illness and healing. Attempts to analyze illness experiences, looks at therapies cross-culturally, and examines the nature of healing.

courses.title: Migrant, Citizen, Refugee
courses.description: This course introduces students to the anthropological study of migration. Students will study both early and contemporary intellectual trends in the study of migration, including “globalization,” “transnationalism,” “diaspora,” “borderlands,” “transmigration,” and “imagined community,” through relevant ethnographic case studies from the U.S., with global comparisons.

courses.title: Latino Roots I
courses.description: Documents Latino history in the racial history of what is now Oregon since 1500 and teaches students to conduct oral history interviews. Multilisted with JCOM 427M . Sequence with ANTH 428M . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Latino Roots II
courses.description: Continuation of Latino Roots I, designed for producing a short documentary using oral history as the story. Covers basic theory and practice of digital film-video documentary production. Multilisted with JCOM 428M . Sequence with ANTH 427M . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Indigenous Peoples of South America
courses.description: Contact period and contemporary ethnography of native peoples; ecological adaptation, socioeconomic organization, and culture change.

courses.title: Race and Gender in Latin America
courses.description: Examines intersecting systems of race, gender, ethnicity, and nationalism through 600 years of Latin American history, focusing on five countries in three regions.

courses.title: Recent Cultural Theory
courses.description: Survey of various cultural frameworks: Durkheimian, Marxian, feminist, transnationalism, Orientalism.

courses.title: Northwest Coast Archaeology
courses.description: This course examines the more than 12,000-year-old archaeological record of the Northwest Coast of North America. We begin by establishing the ecological and ethnographic background for the region and then study how these have shaped archaeologists' ideas about the past. Next, we study the contents of sites and consider the relationship between data, interpretation, and theory. Throughout the term, we discuss the dynamics of contact and colonialism.

courses.title: Gender and Archaeology
courses.description: Discussion of gender as a focus of archaeological theory, method, and interpretation. Examination of case studies from around the world.

courses.title: Cultural Resource Management
courses.description: Objectives, legal background, operational problems, ethical and scholarly considerations in the management of prehistoric and historic cultural resources.

courses.title: African Archaeology
courses.description: The archaeology of humans in Africa with an emphasis on the past 15,000 years.

courses.title: Peopling of the Americas
courses.description: Reviews anthropological methods of the Americas including biological, genetic, archaeological, and paleoenvironmental evidence.

courses.title: Advanced Evolutionary Medicine
courses.description: Explores current research in the field of evolutionary medicine.

courses.title: Primate Evolution
courses.description: The fossil record and theoretical implications of the Cenozoic primates with special reference to their various adaptations: locomotion, special senses, dentition.

courses.title: Primate Behavior
courses.description: Ecology and ethology of free-ranging primates. Classification, distribution, and ecological relationships of living primates; social structure and social organizations.

courses.title: Paleoecology and Human Evolution
courses.description: Relationship between ecology and comparative morphology as a basis for theories of hominid phylogeny; analysis of methods of paleoecological inference; current theories of hominid origins.

courses.title: Statistical Analysis of Biological Anthropology
courses.description: The important methods in biometry (biological statistics) and their inherent assumptions, limitations, interpretations, and common uses (and misuses) as relevant to biological anthropology.

courses.title: Zooarchaeology: [Topic]
courses.description: Analysis and interpretation of bone and shell animal remains from archaeological sites.

courses.title: Primate Conservation Biology
courses.description: Evaluates the conservation status of the order Primates. Explores biological-ecological issues and social-cultural influences on primate biodiversity, distribution, and abundance.

courses.title: Advanced Forensic Anthropology
courses.description: Teaches theory and analysis of human remains for medico-legal professionals, including estimating biological parameters from skeletons and outdoor crime scene processing and testimony.

courses.title: Regarding Remains
courses.description: This course covers the policies and regulations of human and non-human remains in biological anthropology and forensic sciences contexts. It explores the considerations important for establishing, building, maintaining and working with skeletal collections.

courses.title: Taphonomy: Bones, Bugs, and Burials
courses.description: Application of taphonomic studies in the fields of paleontology, archaeology, and forensic-medicolegal anthropology.

courses.title: Principles of Evolutionary Psychology
courses.description: Investigates how understanding of our evolutionary history is used to further understanding of the human mind.

courses.title: Bioanthropology Methods
courses.description: Laboratory-based introduction to research methods in biological anthropology, with an emphasis on research among living human populations.

courses.title: Archaeology and Anthropology
courses.description: Use by archaeologists of concepts drawn from anthropology; modifications and additions made necessary by the nature of archaeological data.

courses.title: Language and Culture I
courses.description: Provides intermediate level Arabic proficiency. Activates and augments grammar structures of modern spoken Arabic, colloquial Egyptian Arabic, and the study of Arabic culture. Sequence with ARB 302 , ARB 303 .

courses.title: Language and Culture II
courses.description: Provides intermediate Arabic proficiency and substantially adds to the vocabulary base. Activates and augments grammar structures of modern spoken Arabic, colloquial Egyptian Arabic, and the study of Arabic culture. Sequence with ARB 301 , ARB 303 .

courses.title: Reading Classical Arabic
courses.description: Improves students' abilities to work with classical Arabic texts; serves as a gateway to other classical Arabic text courses.

courses.title: Arab Cinema
courses.description: Introduction to Arab cinema and culture through an examination of the development of cinema in Arabic-speaking countries. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Introduction to Architecture
courses.description: Offers a structure of principles for making places for people. Examines places, design procedures, and the use of archtiectural principles in general.

courses.title: Design Skills
courses.description: Introduction to basic design processes, methods, and media.

courses.title: Introduction to Architectural Computer Graphics
courses.description: Introduces basic skills and literacy with the computer for architectural illustration, drafting, and design.

courses.title: Architectural Design I
courses.description: Design studio projects and exercises introducing fundamental concepts and considerations in environmental design. Teaches knowledge and skills needed in subsequent studios and professional course work. Sequence with ARCH 284 .

courses.title: Architectural Design II
courses.description: Design studio projects and exercises introducing fundamental concepts and considerations in environmental design. Teaches knowledge and skills needed in subsequent studios and professional course work. Sequence with ARCH 283 .

courses.title: Architectural Design III
courses.description: Studio projects. Integration of issues of context, activity support, spatial order, construction, structure, and environmental control. Emphasis on schematic concept formation and subsequent architectural development. Sequence with ARCH 384 .

courses.title: Architectural Design IV
courses.description: Studio projects. Integration of issues of context, activity support, spatial order, construction, structure, and environmental control. Emphasis on schematic concept formation and subsequent architectural development. Sequence with ARCH 383 .

courses.title: Context of the Architectural Profession
courses.description: Introduction to the professional practice of architecture and related careers. Examines the professional, legal, and regulatory environment; firm organization and management; marketing; contractual issues; and the construction process.

courses.title: Media for Design Development: [Topic]
courses.description: Instruction in media for design process. Techniques for problem and context analysis, generating concepts, developing form, and testing proposals. Subject emphasis varies with instructor.

courses.title: Building Information Modeling
courses.description: Introduces fundamentals of building informational modeling: conceptual design, design development, and project representation to create 3D models and drawings using BIM software and modeling tools.

courses.title: Architectural Contexts: Place and Culture
courses.description: How the design of buildings interacts with physical and cultural contexts of human traditions, landscape, settlements, cities, and suburbs. Historical and contemporary examples.

courses.title: Community Design
courses.description: Multidisciplinary examination of the history, theory, and practice in the design and development of meaningful and sustainable neighborhoods. Special focus selected by faculty. Open to all majors. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Sustainable Urbanism
courses.description: Seminar investigates contemporary trends and thinking about ‘Sustainable Urbanism’ and will evaluate these trends in light of their applicability to current design and development projects.

courses.title: Principles of Urban Design
courses.description: Introduction to theory and practice of urban design, comparative studies of neighborhood conservation, central city regeneration, growth policies and prospects for restructuring cities, metropolitan regions.

courses.title: Theory of Urban Design II
courses.description: Examines the cultural and formal ideas that underlie American and European urban design. 1700 to the present.

courses.title: Housing Prototypes
courses.description: An examination of modern housing prototypes (1920s to the present) with an emphasis on understanding the many and varied factors involved in the production of quality housing. Open to all majors.

courses.title: Minimal Dwelling
courses.description: Examination of the design of small dwelling units for a variety of users. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Human Context of Design
courses.description: Theoretical principles, case studies, and technical skills for assessing user needs, developing building programs, applying research findings to design, and evaluating performance of the built environment.

courses.title: Simulation and Visualization
courses.description: Exploration of simulation tools to build skills to evaluate the performance of buildings for daylight, electric light, thermal comfort, and energy use.

courses.title: Spatial Composition
courses.description: Architectural space as a means to measure existence and expand awareness. Focus on compositional principles in architecture and methods for analyzing and generating spatial organizations.

courses.title: Types and Typology
courses.description: Critical introduction to theory of typology that categorizes urban and architectural forms by formal characteristics and cultural meaning. Lectures cover basic concepts, historical development, and case studies.

courses.title: Structural Behavior
courses.description: Develops understanding of behavior of structural elements and systems and their implications on architectural form with reference to historical and contemporary buildings. Sequence with ARCH 462 .

courses.title: Structural Design
courses.description: Historical development of materials. Analyzes elements, connections, and systems of wood, steel, and concrete structures from the perspective of construction process, spatial and structural design. Sequence with ARCH 461 .

courses.title: Building Construction
courses.description: Provide an understanding of the basic materials and methods of architecture with emphasis on the design, construction and performance of primary structure.

courses.title: Building Enclosure
courses.description: Selection, design, detailing, and performance evaluation of building envelopes: wood, metals, glass, concrete, and masonry veneers and roofing.

courses.title: Natural Building Systems and Materials: [Topic]
courses.description: Investigations of inorganic and organic natural building materials, benefits, challenges, embodied energy and carbon, latest technological and process innovations, potential use in mainstream construction.

courses.title: Advanced Mass Timber Design
courses.description: In-depth examination of mass timber buildings, advanced wood products, timber industry, production, integrated design process involving Architectural Engineering and Design industries, structural systems, and case studies.

courses.title: Design the Unseen: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced topics in the design of the unseen element in the built environment that impact the health of occupants, communities, and the planet.

courses.title: Residential Construction
courses.description: Provides an understanding of basic materials and methods of North American residential construction with emphasis on design and construction of the wood light frame. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Supervised Design Teaching
courses.description: Supervised assistance with desk critiques and tasks related to studio teaching. Written application required.

courses.title: Architectural Design
courses.description: Design projects requiring comprehensive and integrative study over a wide range of project options. Individual criticism, group discussions, lectures and seminars by visiting specialists, public review of projects.

courses.title: Advanced Architectural Design I
courses.description: In-depth work on complex design projects and design development beyond that normally possible in intermediate studios. Sequence.

courses.title: Advanced Architectural Design II
courses.description: In-depth work on complex design projects and design development beyond that normally possible in intermediate studios.

courses.title: Environmental Control Systems I
courses.description: Influence of energy source, climate, heating, cooling, lighting, acoustics, and water and waste systems on design of buildings and sites. Architectural and mechanical means to manipulate thermal environment. Sequence.

courses.title: Environmental Control Systems II
courses.description: Influence of energy source, climate, heating, cooling, lighting, acoustics, and water and waste systems on design of buildings and sites. Implications of lighting, acoustics, and water and waste for architectural design.

courses.title: Passive Cooling
courses.description: Conceptual and quantitative investigations of passive cooling design and performance, including precedents, shading, natural ventilation, evaporative cooling, use of thermal mass, radiant cooling assisted by cold night skies, and control scheduling, supported by field investigations and introductory energy modeling. Multilisted with ENVS 493M .

courses.title: Daylighting
courses.description: "Daylighting"—increasing the energy efficiency of a building by maximizing the amount of daylight versus electric light—as an element in architectural design. Models and photography used to study behavior of light. Case studies and prediction techniques.

courses.title: Passive House Design and Details
courses.description: In-depth examination climate-specific passive house standard designing principles, building envelope detailing, mechanical systems, net zero design, dynamic modeling, and consultant certification.

courses.title: High Performance Buildings Design: [Topic]
courses.description: Integrated building design and simulation advanced architectural technology course covers the theory and history of high-performance buildings with topical applications of design strategies.

courses.description: An examination of modern housing prototypes (1920s to the present) with an emphasis on understanding the many and varied factors involved in the production of quality housing. Open to all majors.

courses.title: Minimal Dwelling
courses.description: Examination of the design of small dwelling units for a variety of users. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Human Context of Design
courses.description: Theoretical principles, case studies, and technical skills for assessing user needs, developing building programs, applying research findings to design, and evaluating performance of the built environment.

courses.title: Simulation and Visualization
courses.description: Exploration of simulation tools to build skills to evaluate the performance of buildings for daylight, electric light, thermal comfort, and energy use.

courses.title: Spatial Composition
courses.description: Architectural space as a means to measure existence and expand awareness. Focus on compositional principles in architecture and methods for analyzing and generating spatial organizations.

courses.title: Types and Typology
courses.description: Critical introduction to theory of typology that categorizes urban and architectural forms by formal characteristics and cultural meaning. Lectures cover basic concepts, historical development, and case studies.

courses.title: Structural Behavior
courses.description: Develops understanding of behavior of structural elements and systems and their implications on architectural form with reference to historical and contemporary buildings.

courses.title: Structural Design
courses.description: Historical development of materials. Analyzes elements, connections, and systems of wood, steel, and concrete structures from the perspective of construction process, spatial and structural design. Sequence with 561.

courses.title: Building Construction
courses.description: Provide an understanding of the basic materials and methods of architecture with emphasis on the design, construction and performance of primary structure.

courses.title: Building Enclosure
courses.description: Selection, design, detailing, and performance evaluation of building envelopes: wood, metals, glass, concrete, and masonry veneers and roofing.

courses.title: Natural Building Systems and Materials: [Topic]
courses.description: Investigations of inorganic and organic natural building materials, benefits, challenges, embodied energy and carbon, latest technological and process innovations, potential use in mainstream construction.

courses.title: Advanced Mass Timber Design
courses.description: In-depth examination of mass timber buildings, advanced wood products, timber industry, production, integrated design process involving Architectural Engineering and Design industries, structural systems, and case studies.

courses.title: Design the Unseen: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced topics in the design of the unseen element in the built environment that impact the health of occupants, communities, and the planet.

courses.title: Residential Construction
courses.description: Provides an understanding of basic materials and methods of North American residential construction with emphasis on design and construction of the wood light frame. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Supervised Design Teaching
courses.description: Supervised assistance with desk critiques and tasks related to studio teaching. Written application required.

courses.title: Architectural Design
courses.description: Repeatable. Design projects requiring comprehensive and integrative study over a wide range of project options. Individual criticism, group discussions, lectures and seminars by visiting specialists, public review of projects.

courses.title: Advanced Architectural Design I
courses.description: In-depth work on complex design projects and design development beyond that normally possible in intermediate studios. Sequence.

courses.title: Advanced Architectural Design II
courses.description: In-depth work on complex design projects and design development beyond that normally possible in intermediate studios.

courses.title: Environmental Control Systems I
courses.description: Influence of energy source, climate, heating, cooling, lighting, acoustics, and water and waste systems on design of buildings and sites. Architectural and mechanical means to manipulate thermal environment. Sequence.

courses.title: Environmental Control Systems II
courses.description: Influence of energy source, climate, heating, cooling, lighting, acoustics, and water and waste systems on design of buildings and sites. Implications of lighting, acoustics, and water and waste for architectural design.

courses.title: Passive Cooling
courses.description: Conceptual and quantitative investigations of passive cooling design and performance, including precedents, shading, natural ventilation, evaporative cooling, use of thermal mass, radiant cooling assisted by cold night skies, and control scheduling, supported by field investigations and introductory energy modeling. Multilisted with ENVS 593M .

courses.title: Passive House Design and Details
courses.description: In-depth examination climate-specific passive house standard designing principles, building envelope detailing, mechanical systems, net zero design, dynamic modeling, and consultant certification.

courses.title: High Performance Buildings Design: [Topic]
courses.description: Integrated building design and simulation advanced architectural technology course covers the theory and history of high-performance buildings with topical applications of design strategies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Dissertation
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Special Problems: [Topic]
courses.description: Specialized inquiry into a particular topic by a student and discussed with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Graduate Design Process
courses.description: Foundation knowledge, concepts, and skills fundamental to design process and media subject areas.

courses.title: Research Methods in Sustainable Design
courses.description: Research methods to assess the design of buildings and communities; discussions include defining research themes, hypotheses, and developing thesis proposals.

courses.title: Computational Design
courses.description: This course will introduce computational design methods to support individual design intent through: Diagrams and 3D modeling; Analog Parametric Design and Lighting; Digital Parametric Design.

courses.title: Global Modern Architecture
courses.description: Graduate introduction to architectural history. Buildings and landscapes of the twentieth and twenty-first centuries, emphasizing interactions between Western and non-Western cultures.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Sustainable Design
courses.description: Fundamentals of sustainable design practices; codes and standards related to building design; perspectives and implementation strategies by leading practitioners.

courses.title: Teaching Technical Subjects in Architecture
courses.description: Covers techniques for effective teaching. Focuses on one or more standard building-technology courses in architecture and interior architecture.

courses.title: Introductory Graduate Design I
courses.description: Design projects and exercises intended to familiarize the student with fundamental concepts of environmental design. Emphasis on developing graphic skills and the capability for visual thinking that are essential to advanced studios. Sequence.

courses.title: Introductory Graduate Design II
courses.description: Design projects and exercises intended to familiarize the student with fundamental concepts of environmental design. Emphasis on developing graphic skills and the capability for visual thinking that are essential to advanced studios. Sequence.

courses.title: Introductory Graduate Design III
courses.description: Design projects and exercises intended to familiarize the student with fundamental concepts of environmental design. Emphasis on developing graphic skills and the capability for visual thinking that are essential to advanced studios.

courses.title: Introduction Graduate Design IV
courses.description: Design to expand perception and response to issues in architectural design. Design as exploration of fundamental theoretical ideas. Studio projects require comprehensiveness and integrative study.

courses.title: Advanced Dissertation Proposal Development
courses.description: Directed study for the development of dissertation proposals. Approval of faculty advisor required. Repeatable twice for a maximum of 18 credits based on development of proposal for dissertation.

courses.title: Introduction to Visual Culture
courses.description: This course introduces to students to a wide variety of methods for looking at and analyzing images and objects of visual culture, drawing on concepts and methods from art history, anthropology, archaeology, and media studies.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic}
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Ancient Mediterranean Art
courses.description: This course is a survey of the art and architecture of Africans, Christians, Jews, Greeks, Persians, Romans, and others in the ancient Mediterranean world.

courses.title: Medieval Renaissance European Art
courses.description: Historical survey of visual arts. Selected works of painting, sculpture, architecture, and other arts studied in relation to the medieval to early Renaissance cultures producing them.

courses.title: History of Western Art III
courses.description: Historical survey of visual arts. Selected works of painting, sculpture, architecture, and other arts studied in relation to the Renaissance to modern cultures producing them.

courses.title: History of Indian Art
courses.description: Historical Survey of the visual art of India. Selected works of painting, sculpture, architecture, and other arts studied in relation to the culture in which they were created.

courses.title: History of Chinese Art
courses.description: Historical survey of the visual arts of China. Selected works of painting, sculpture, architecture, and other arts will be studied in relation to the culture in which they were produced.

courses.title: History of Japanese Art
courses.description: Historical survey of the visual arts of Japan. Selected works of painting, sculpture, architecture, and other arts studied in relation to the culture in which they were produced.

courses.title: Survey of Latin American Arts
courses.description: Overview of the rich tradition of visual arts within Latin America from the pre-Columbian period, through the art of the Spanish viceroyalties, to the contemporary artistic landscape.

courses.title: History of World Architecture I
courses.description: Survey of global architectural developments from prehistory to the Middle Ages.

courses.title: History of World Architecture II
courses.description: Survey of global architectural developments from c. 1400 to the present.

courses.title: Gothic Architecture
courses.description: A specialized survey of Gothic architecture in western Europe (c. 1140 to c. 1500). This course gives special consideration to the constructional and structural developments of the Gothic period, but will also consider architecture in relationship to social, religious, and economic practices.

courses.title: Romanesque Architecture
courses.description: Examines Romanesque architecture through the lens of pilgrimage, a major cultural force in the Middle Ages.

courses.title: Classical Greek Art and Architecture
courses.description: This course is a survey of ancient Greek art and architecture in the Classical Period, encompassing works throughout the ancient Mediterranean.

courses.title: Ancient Jewish Art
courses.description: A survey of Jewish art and architecture throughout the ancient Mediterranean (e.g., Aegina, Beth Alpha, Corinth, Dura Europos, Jerusalem, Masada, Ostia, Rome, Sardis, Sepphoris, Qumran)

courses.title: Hellenistic Art and Architecture
courses.description: This course is a survey of ancient Greek art and architecture in the Hellenistic period, encompassing works throughout the ancient Mediterranean and Near East.

courses.title: Roman Art & Architecture
courses.description: This course is a survey of the art and architecture of Republican and Imperial Rome throughout the Mediterranean and Near East. Considering the Roman world as a multicultural space, it explores works at locations such as Alexandria, Athens, Dura Europos, Jerusalem, Petra, Pompeii, and Rome.

courses.title: Islamic Art & Architecture
courses.description: This course is a survey of visual culture from the Islamic world, beginning with its origins in the seventh century CE.

courses.title: Medieval Art
courses.description: Introduction to the most important monuments of the Middle Ages, spanning the fourth through the 15th centuries with the primary focus on Western Europe and Byzantium.

courses.title: Italian Renaissance Art
courses.description: Painting and sculpture of the Renaissance and mannerist periods analyzed in terms of style, iconography, theory, patronage, and social context.

courses.title: Northern Renaissance Art
courses.description: Painting and graphic arts in the Netherlands, Germany, and France in the 15th and 16th centuries. Van Eyck, Durer, Holbein, other leading artists.

courses.title: History of Manga
courses.description: Survey of the history of Manga (Japanese comics) from the 19th Century to the present.

courses.title: 19th Century Art
courses.description: Introduction to artistic movements in Europe from 1780 to the 1880s including neoclassicism, romanticism, realism, and impressionism.

courses.title: Art of the Enlightenment
courses.description: Explores European art of the 18th century. Lectures cover major movements—rococo, chinoiserie, neoclassicism, early romanticism—in the context of changing social values, the scientific revolution, secularization, and globalization.

courses.title: Modern Art 1880 to 1950
courses.description: History of modern art from the end of Impressionism to Abstract-Expressionism in relation to social, political, intellectual and cultural contexts.

courses.title: Contemporary Art
courses.description: Survey of contemporary art in the West from 1945 to the present in relation to historical, social, cultural, and political concerns.

courses.title: History of Design
courses.description: Survey of the history of modern design from the 18th-century to the present day. The course explores the intersection of the historical development of design with its technological, social, commercial and aesthetic dimensions.

courses.title: History of Photography
courses.description: History of photography from the early 19th-century to the present, aesthetics of the medium, its relationship to art, and its social role.

courses.title: Arts and Visual Cultures of Climate Change
courses.description: Critical exploration of climate change and its representation in contemporary art and visual culture (e.g., satellite imagery, activism, science fiction)

courses.title: Arts of Colonial Latin America
courses.description: This course examines the role of art and architecture in colonial Latin America (ca. 1492- 19th century).

courses.title: 20th Century Latin American Art
courses.description: Examines art and architecture across 20th-century Latin America, with special emphasis on the formation and expression of ethnic, cultural, regional, and national identity through them.

courses.title: Arts of the Silk Road
courses.description: This course examines cross-cultural and artistic encounters between Asia, Africa, Europe, and the Americas by studying, diasporas and migrations, religions, urban settlements, trade, and slavery.

courses.title: Chinese Buddhist Art
courses.description: Introduction to selective aspects of the history of Buddhist art in China. Emphasis on sculpture and painting.

courses.description: Offerings vary from year to year and reflect the interests of faculty members.

courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.description: Offerings vary from year to year and reflect the interests of faculty members.

courses.title: Art History Critical Methods
courses.description: Methods used to study art history (formalist, iconographical, social history of art, etc). Materials are drawn from art historical scholarship on art from a variety of regions and periods.

courses.title: Ancient Mediterranean Art: [Topic]
courses.description: This course examines art and architecture from the ancient Mediterranean world, exploring material from a particular region/period or through a particular critical/thematic lens.

courses.title: Medieval Art and Architecture: [Topic]
courses.description: An in-depth study of a discrete theme within the field of medieval art and architecture. Themes will vary from year to year and will reflect the interests of faculty members.

courses.title: Museology
courses.description: This course covers the history and theory of museums. Through readings, written assignments, lecture and classroom discussion we engage issues of museum ethics, the role of museums in society, and curatorial practice. The scope includes art museums as well as other types of museums.

courses.title: Renaissance and Baroque: [Topic]
courses.description: In-depth examination of art, architecture, and material culture of the Renaissance and Baroque eras as situated within their historical, social, religious, and economic contexts. Topics vary.

courses.title: Romanticism in the Visual Arts
courses.description: The course offers an overview of the diverse artists and interests of the Romantic Movement in European art, while critically examining Romanticism as a period term.

courses.title: Studies in 19th Century Art: [Topic]
courses.description: Focuses on a specific area, movement, period or issue in visual art of the long 19th century (c. 1770–1914).

courses.title: Modern Art: [Topic]
courses.description: This course focuses on a specific movement, sub-period, geographical region, or theoretical issue in the history of global modern art and the avant-gardes roughly between 1880 to the postwar period.

courses.title: World Architecture Since 1960
courses.description: Examines key themes, designs and structures, conversations, events, and people in architectural history, theory, and practice from around the world, from the early 1960s up to the present.

courses.title: Contemporary Art: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics are unified by their emphasis on the historical, socio-cultural, political, and theoretical conditions associated with art since 1945. Offerings vary from year to year and reflect the interests of faculty members.

courses.title: Art Theory and Criticism: [Topic]
courses.description: This is an upper-division course focusing on concepts and problems in art theory, criticism, or methods, ideally for advanced undergraduate and graduate students.

courses.title: Modern Architecture
courses.description: Examines modern architecture from 1885 to 1965, and the discourse surrounding it. Emphasis on the meanings and manifestations of modernity as these evolved in Europe, the US, and around the world.

courses.title: American Architecture II
courses.description: Major developments in American architecture, 1800–1900. Includes the rediscovery of national symbols, the impact of industry, and the national focus on the single-family residence.

courses.title: American Architecture III
courses.description: Major developments in American architecture, 1885 to the present. Emphasizes academicism, the impact of international modernism, and the rediscovery of eclectic symbolism.

courses.title: Latin American Art History: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics related to arts of Latin America from the Pre-Columbian period to the present. Offerings vary from year to year and reflect the interests of faculty members.

courses.title: Chinese Art: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics are unified by their emphasis on the historical, socio-cultural, political, and theoretical conditions associated with Chinese art. Offerings vary from year to year and reflect the interests of faculty members.

courses.title: Japanese Art: [Topic]
courses.description: Explores the issues surrounding material cultures of Japan from prehistoric to contemporary periods.

courses.title: Japanese Prints
courses.description: The woodblock print in Japan as part of the cultural, social, and political conditions.

courses.title: Understanding Contemporary Art
courses.description: Examine contemporary art concepts, approaches and themes. Understand how to interpret contemporary artwork and contextualize arts’ relationship to culture, society and life today.

courses.title: The Artist Experience
courses.description: Explore concepts defining contemporary art through the individual studio practice of art faculty. Artist presentations will examine their creative process, influences, professional experiences and career.

courses.title: Surface, Space, and Time
courses.description: Introduces interdisciplinary media processes, critical theory, formal communication design, color theory, skills in objective evaluation and critique, and how materials, processes, and context establish meaning.

courses.title: Core Interdisciplinary Laboratory
courses.description: Rigorous studio projects in the core studio sequence stressing interdisciplinary media transitions and the interrelatedness of conceptual and formal concerns.

courses.title: Drawing II
courses.description: Emphasizes synthesis of ideas and approaches, complex subjects, investigation, and expression while building on previous drawing skills. Sequence with ART 233 .

courses.title: Letterpress
courses.description: Experiments with lead and wooden type as related to graphic composition and communication.

courses.title: Print Media Digital Arts
courses.description: Examines application of print media in contemporary visual culture; explores its use in a fine art context. Introduces digital drawing, digital photo editing, and typographic layout to visually communicate expressive concepts. Laboratories, lectures.

courses.title: Time-Based Digital Arts
courses.description: Explores the notion of time as a medium in relation to contemporary art through which concepts of sequence, narration, scoring, and motion are expressed. Laboratories, lectures.

courses.title: Interactive Digital Arts
courses.description: Introduces resources that the computer offers the artist. Concentrates on animation, interaction, and the web as expressive mediums. Laboratories, lectures.

courses.title: Introduction to Production
courses.description: Traditional camera, sound, and lighting techniques in production; nonlinear editing; and key theoretical, historical, and aesthetic approaches to video art.

courses.title: Digital Drawing
courses.description: Applies technology as a drawing medium to communicate concepts visually. The entire creative process is researched in an experimental studio environment.

courses.title: Digital Imaging
courses.description: Intermediate-level focus on the proper preparation and presentation of digital images for use in print and on screen. Covers color theory.

courses.title: Introduction to Animation
courses.description: Introduction to principles of animation, timing, sequence; key frames, in-betweens, and metamorphosis. Uses various methods to record and edit animation tests.

courses.title: Digital Letterform
courses.description: Concepts in the history, use, and appreciation of digital typography. Considers issues in communicative power of type and situations where it functions as message.

courses.title: Digital Interactivity
courses.description: Students will learn programming to interactive artworks. This class will include how to program microcontrollers and use electronics to create responsive and expressive interactive systems.

courses.title: Computational Aesthetics
courses.description: Introduces screen-based programming and data navigation; use of motion, duration, and time-based interaction as a means of artistic expression. Students build navigational structures and explore stochastic principles in developing an individual approach to interactivity.

courses.title: Introduction to Video Art
courses.description: Intermediate video-audio production and nonlinear editing, including camera, sound, and lighting techniques. Key theoretical, historical, and aesthetic approaches to time-based art in video and sound are surveyed.

courses.title: Experimental Animation
courses.description: Intermediate to advanced students explore personal creative practice and experiment with film, video, and computer animation techniques. Integrates readings, screening, and discussion with production.

courses.title: Emerging Technologies
courses.description: Explores use of emerging technologies in art. Create works using emerging technologies and techniques and explore contemporary artworks, philosophies, and cultural trends.

courses.title: Video Art: Experimental Film
courses.description: Video and sound art practices, from conceptual deconstructions of the film-video apparatus to self-reflexive socio-political and/or cultural critique, are examined through short format and video installation.

courses.title: Communication Design
courses.description: Explores the communication of ideas and information through visual means. Introduces design process and principles, visual language, and the art of problem solving in visual communication.

courses.title: 3-D Computer Imaging
courses.description: Introduces 3-D computer graphic arts: 3-D digital space and form, model building, scene composition, surface properties, lighting, and rendering 3-D images.

courses.title: 3-D Computer Animation
courses.description: Introduces 3-D computer animation arts. Includes time and space in the digital 3-D environment, animation concepts and techniques in 3-D space, production techniques for various multimedia applications.

courses.title: Issues and Practices in Digital Arts
courses.description: Intensive critique, discussion, readings, and presentations.

courses.title: Introduction to Photography
courses.description: Covers basic elements of photography using a DSLR camera and provides a broad-based understanding of photographic practice in a fine art context. Sequence with ARTO 350 .

courses.title: Film and Darkroom
courses.description: Technical and aesthetic consideration of black-and-white analog photography. Basics of camera, film development, and photographic print controls in the darkroom. Sequence with ARTO 250 .

courses.title: Constructed Image Photography
courses.description: Explores processes and concepts extending beyond the single photographic image. Narrative and formal strategies in manipulation and sequencing, exploring the materiality of the photograph. Studio course.

courses.title: Creative Large-Format Photography
courses.description: Introduces large-format cameras and their aesthetic possibilities. Four-by-five and eight-by-ten view cameras provided. Includes camera use, film and development, printing skills.

courses.title: Introduction to Printmaking
courses.description: Introduction to printmaking methodologies. Explores the unique print, multiples, and variable edition; techniques may include monotype, linocut, collagraph, etching, and screen print.

courses.title: Relief
courses.description: Creative exploration of graphic and conceptual possibilities through woodcut and related relief printing methods; techniques include block cutting, registration, single and multicolor printing.

courses.description: Techniques of etching, drypoint, engraving, aquatint, soft ground, lift ground, white ground, embossment, relief plate printing. Emphasizes personal imagery development.

courses.title: Screen Printing
courses.description: Creative exploration of graphic and conceptual possibilities through screen printing; techniques include registration, color, edition printing, and stencil-making using hand-drawn, photographic, and digital methods.

courses.title: First-Year American Sign Language
courses.description: Study of basic grammatical structure and vocabulary of American Sign Language, expressive and receptive finger-spelling, and introduction to American deaf culture.

courses.title: First-Year American Sign Language
courses.description: Increased receptive and expressive communication skills in ASL, and study of cultural values and behavioral rules of the deaf community.

courses.title: First-Year American Sign Language
courses.description: Concentration on understanding and acquiring advanced conversational proficiency. Emphasis on ASL classifiers. Continued study of deaf culture as a linguistic minority.

courses.title: Second-Year American Sign Language
courses.description: Applied conversational use of ASL through literature, narratives, poetry, and plays. Explores various underlying metaphors found in ASL literature.

courses.title: Second-Year American Sign Language
courses.description: Emphasis on more abstract and challenging conversational and narrative ranges. Lab and readings cover historical aspects of deaf community and culture.

courses.title: Second-Year American Sign Language
courses.description: Further emphasis on more abstract and challenging conversational and narrative ranges. Explores broader political and social activities of international deaf community.

courses.title: American Deaf Culture
courses.description: Study of the relationship between small groups and dominant culture in the United States. Explore issues of language, culture, self-representation, identity, and social structure.

courses.title: The Solar System
courses.description: Naked-eye astronomy, development of astronomical concepts, and the solar system.

courses.title: Birth and Death of Stars
courses.description: The structure and evolution of stars.

courses.title: Galaxies and the Expanding Universe
courses.description: Galaxies and the universe.

courses.title: Topics in Astrophysics
courses.description: Problem solving of the orbits, kinematics, and dynamics of astronomical systems, structure and evolution of stars and galaxies.

courses.title: Introduction to Business
courses.description: Presents an integrated view of both established and entrepreneurial businesses by studying their common characteristics and processes in a global context. Introduces theory and develops basic skills in the areas of accounting, finance, management, and marketing, with an emphasis on social responsibility and ethical practices. Explores how businesses can create value for themselves and society by addressing environmental and social challenges.

courses.title: Data Analysis Using Microsoft Excel
courses.description: Covers Microsoft Excel software skills necessary for evidence-based problem-solving, including workbook editing, formula creation, charting, and pivot tables. Emphasizes hands-on learning using Excel functions to perform data analysis to enhance decision-making. Previously taught as BA 240.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Principles of Financial Accounting
courses.description: Imparts an understanding of the purpose of accounting, common financial statement items, and the principles of internal controls. Focuses on recording the impact of economic events on account balances using U.S. Generally Accepted Accounting Principles, and the creation and analysis of financial statements to aid in external decision making.

courses.title: Principles of Managerial Accounting
courses.description: Builds an understanding of the role of managerial accounting in a business, focusing on the development and use of information to evaluate production costs and operational performance in support of short- and long-term organizational decision-making.

courses.title: Accounting: Language of Business Decisions
courses.description: How the accounting model reflects business transactions or events. Interpretation and analysis of financial statements. Understanding cost and revenue information, organization, and decisions.

courses.title: Global Perspectives in Business
courses.description: An interdisciplinary introduction to what it means to participate in a global economy, critically reflecting on globalization, the impacts of business activity, human rights, global finance, marketing, and management. Also explores concepts and skills in intercultural communication and working with others from around the world.

courses.title: Leadership and Communication
courses.description: Personal leadership and communication skills. Focuses on self-awareness for leading, persuading, and working with others; effective business writing and speaking; and team development. Students may not receive credit both BA 308 and BA 308H . Sophomore standing required.

courses.title: Leadership and Communication
courses.description: Personal leadership and communication skills. Focuses on self-awareness for leading, persuading, and working with others; effective business writing and speaking; and team development. Students may not receive credit both BA 308 and BA 308H . Open only to students in the LCB Honors Program.

courses.title: Economy, Industry, and Competitive Analysis
courses.description: Free enterprise capitalism and market competition. Economic value added, product cost, and product pricing. Organizational arrangements and the control of economic activity.

courses.title: Management: Creating Value through People
courses.description: Management systems for planning, controlling, organizing, and leading; how they influence human behavior in organizations. Selecting, training, retaining, and motivating the human resource in organization.

courses.title: Marketing: Creating Value for Customers
courses.description: Market analysis, target customer identification, and development of marketing-mix strategies to deliver superior customer value and contribute to the performance of the organization.

courses.title: Finance: Creating Value through Capital
courses.description: Financial statement analysis, pro forma statements and capital budgeting, time value of money, net present-value analysis, risk and cost of capital.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Sustainable Business
courses.description: Survey of the ideas, tools, and skills used by businesses to address environmental and social challenges, including new business attitudes, models, ventures, and ecosystems.

courses.title: Business Law and Ethics
courses.description: Legal and ethical environments of business, including U.S. legal concepts, social and environmental impacts of business, and ethical decision making.

courses.title: Business Law and Ethics
courses.description: BA 325H offers an intensive exploration of legal and ethical environments of business, including U.S. legal concepts, social and environmental impacts of business, and ethical decision making. Students cannot receive credit for both BA 325 and BA 325H .

courses.title: Cross-Cultural Business Communication
courses.description: Theoretical and practical approach to value dimensions across cultures and their impact on communication in business and professional contexts. Develops intercultural business communication skills.

courses.title: Cross-Cultural Negotiation
courses.description: Theory and practice of negotiating effectively across cultures. Research and analysis of culturally specific models for negotiating and experience using those models in cross-cultural simulations.

courses.title: Business Strategy and Planning
courses.description: Capstone course focusing on strategy formulation and decisional processes. Includes writing a business plan that applies knowledge and develops course of action to accomplish organizational objectives. Students cannot receive credit for both BA 453 and BA 453H .

courses.title: Business Strategy and Planning
courses.description: Provides conceptual tools for in-depth strategic analysis and interactive discussions from sources relevant to the challenge of developing and implementing strategy. Students cannot receive credit for both BA 453 and BA 453H .
