courses.title: Literature of Modern Taiwan
courses.description: Surveys the literature of Taiwan from the postwar era to the present. Discussion focuses on national identity, gender, class, modernization, and globalization. Taught in English.

courses.title: Professional Chinese
courses.description: This course is designed for students with intermediate Chinese language proficiency, particularly those who have completed two years of Chinese language studies at the college level. It aims to enhance students' Chinese language skills and cross-cultural awareness in the context of Chinese business and professional communications. The course explores common Chinese business cultural practices, social etiquette, and polite speech acts through discussions of a variety of relevant course materials.

courses.title: Gender and Sexuality in Traditional Chinese Literature
courses.description: Examines the changing constructions of gender and sexuality in premodern China. Topics include arranged marriage and concubinage, attitudes toward the body and transgender identities. No background in Chinese necessary; readings in English.

courses.title: Gender and Sexuality in Modern Chinese Literature
courses.description: Primary and secondary works about women, sexuality, and changing gender roles in republican, socialist, and post-Mao China. Readings in English.

courses.description: Readings in various styles and genres of classical Chinese literature; stress on major works of different periods. Preparation for research.

courses.title: History of the Chinese Language

courses.title: How to Watch TV

courses.title: How to Watch Movies
courses.description: Introduction to analysis, theory, and history of movies. Includes viewing and discussing examples with scholarly rigor and reading academic scholarship on movies.

courses.title: Storytelling for Screens
courses.description: Learn to be a storyteller: a beginner’s guide to screenwriting. Includes analyzing existing screenplays, developing your own screenplays, engaging in reading and editing original screenplays. No previous experience required.

courses.title: History of the Motion Picture I
courses.description: Studies the technological, artistic, and cultural histories of motion pictures in various national contexts, from precinema through the silent era.

courses.title: History of the Motion Picture II
courses.description: Studies the technological, artistic, and cultural histories of motion pictures in various national contexts, from the transition to sound through the early 1960s.

courses.title: History of the Motion Picture III
courses.description: Studies the technological, artistic, and cultural histories of motion pictures in various national contexts, from the 1960s through the present.

courses.title: Television History
courses.description: Analyzes the history of television, from its roots in radio broadcasting to the latest developments in digital television.

courses.title: Narrative Production I
courses.description: Focuses on basic theory and practice of digital video for narrative production.

courses.title: Introductory Media Industry Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Explores various introductory contexts of films, video, and television through the practices of the media industries.

courses.title: Theory and Criticism: [Topic]
courses.description: Aesthetic, historical, and theoretical analysis of media texts, genres, filmmakers, and the theories and criticisms related to them.

courses.title: National and Regional Cinema: [Topic]
courses.description: Explores cinematic traditions, artistic styles and industrial practices in specific national and regional contexts as well as cinema’s global development. Also examines issues of transnationalism, globalization, and diaspora.

courses.title: Beginning Screenwriting
courses.description: Introduction to the basics of writing for the screen. Provides students with an organized strategy for writing a feature film.

courses.title: Exhibition and Audiences
courses.description: Explores the exhibition and reception of film and other media by audiences in various contexts.

courses.title: Production Studies
courses.description: Introduction to the development of production practices and the lived realities of film and television production workers.

courses.title: Stars
courses.description: An examination of how and why stars and celebrities are produced and marketed by entertainment industries.

courses.title: Film Theory
courses.description: Introduction to theoretical debates about film as a medium of artistic expression in a transhistorical and global framework.

courses.title: Contemporary Korean Film
courses.description: Introduction to contemporary South Korean film. Explores changes in film culture, practice, and industry in relation to social changes since the early 1990s. Offered alternate years. Multilisted with KRN 362M .

courses.title: Digital Cinema
courses.description: Examines the impact of digital media technologies on diverse dimensions of cinematic experience encompassing the production, delivery, and reception.

courses.title: Narrative Production II
courses.description: Focuses on the creative choices and intermediate skills of narrative production.

courses.title: Film, Media, and Culture
courses.description: Study of film and media as aesthetic objects that engage with communities identified by class, gender, race, ethnicity, and sexuality. Multilisted with ENG 381M .

courses.description: Explores different approaches to directing by investigating a filmmaker’s use of narrative and aesthetic tools. Students will learn to work with actors, visualize stories, and build narratives through exercises based on particular directing techniques.

courses.description: Introduction to database systems, emphasis on database design and access. Database concepts, data modeling, SQL, connecting database to web.

courses.description: Server- and client-side technologies and their interaction for database-driven web applications: application frameworks, single-page applications, cloud platforms, and open-source software stacks—MEAN (MongoDB, ExpressJS, AngularJS, Node.js) versus LAMP (Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP).

courses.title: Networking Fundamentals

courses.title: Classical Mythology
courses.description: Introduction to the world of Greek and Roman mythology with an emphasis on the issues of personal and social identity.

courses.title: Ancient Athletics in Greece and Rome
courses.description: This course explores the role of sports, games and athletics in the lives of the ancient Greeks and Romans. Beginning with the disciplines of the ancient Olympic festivals in Greece, we consider the way these were interwoven with the development of Greek thought, society, warfare and politics, especially the democratic polis. We also consider the role of spectacular entertainments such as chariot racing and gladiatorial combat in imperial Rome.

courses.title: Introduction to Classical Archaeology
courses.description: Introduction to the archaeology and material culture of the Ancient Greeks and Romans.

courses.title: Greek Life and Culture
courses.description: Introduces students to the literature, history, and art of ancient Greece. Students develop an appreciation for Greek culture and its similarities to and differences from American culture.

courses.title: Roman Life and Culture
courses.description: Examines a thousand years of Roman life and culture from the founding of the city Rome in the 8th century BCE to the founding of the "New Rome, Constantinople, in the fourth century CE.

courses.title: Greek and Roman Epic
courses.description: Analysis of the heroic tradition and epic themes in the Homeric poems, the works of Hesiod, and the Aeneid. Emphasis on literary criticism and intellectual history and the reception of these works by later writers and artists.

courses.title: Greek and Roman Tragedy
courses.description: Examination of the major Greek tragedians, Aeschylus, Sophocles, Euripides, and the Roman, Seneca, from the viewpoint of literary criticism and cultural and intellectual history.

courses.title: Classical Greek Philosophers
courses.description: Introduction to the philosophies of Plato and/or Aristotle from the viewpoint of Greek intellectual history.

courses.title: Early China, Ancient Greece
courses.description: Examines the relationship between knowledge and wisdom in literature produced by two different ancient civilizations, Greece and China, from c. 1000 BCE to 86 CE. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Death and Rebirth in Greece and India
courses.description: Explores Greco-Roman and Indian conceptions of the soul and beliefs concerning the afterlife, particularly those of reincarnation and soul transmigration. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Gender and Sexuality in Antiquity
courses.description: Introduction to construction of the categories of norms of Western sexuality through study of Greek and Roman attitudes toward gender roles, homo- and heterosexuality, the family, and privacy.

courses.description: Explores literature from a global standpoint. Examines movement of literary forms (e.g., genres, motifs, rhetorical modes) across cultures, regions, historical epochs.

courses.description: Introduces the principles of comparative analysis, exploring the aesthetic, ideological, and socio-economic exchanges between national cinematic traditions. Themes vary by instructor. Recent themes include Melodrama, Zombies, Queer Cinema.

courses.title: United States Culture in Comparative Contexts
courses.description: Introduction to comparative analysis of literature and culture with emphasis on multilingual US-based writers reflecting on history, memory, identity, and place. Emphasis on the representation of race, gender, power, identity, difference. Introduces core methodologies: close-reading, analysis, comparison, and critique.

courses.title: Literature and Society
courses.description: Introduction to the interdisciplinary study of literature in relation to society and politics. Draws on perspectives from political science, law, sociology, and related fields.

courses.title: Literature and Film
courses.description: Introduction to the interdisciplinary study of literature and film. Draws on perspectives from cinema studies, media aesthetics, and related fields.

courses.description: Introduction to theory and methods in comparative literature, with some attention to the history and problems of the discipline.

courses.title: Theories of Poetry
courses.description: Introduction to the study of poetry and poetic form from a world perspective. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Theories of the Novel

courses.title: Cultural Studies
courses.description: Introduction to the interdisciplinary study of cultural discourses and practices.

courses.title: Gender and Identity in Literature
courses.description: Introduction to the study of gender in literature, from Asia to Europe to the Americas, and from the classics to the late 20th century.

courses.title: Comparative Comics
courses.description: Examines genre of narrative from a comparative and global standpoint, reviewing the impact of comics and other visual media on questions of national, regional, and ethnic identity. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Comparative Media: [Topic]
courses.description: Critical analysis of a range of media from a transnational and/or intercultural perspective. Taught as a hybrid course blending face-to-face and online learning. Recent topics include Tokyo Cyberpunk, Asian Horror, Listening to Cinema.

courses.title: Comparing Identities, Agencies and Differences
courses.description: A critical reflection on contemporary US power imbalances and systems of race, ethnic, gender and religious privilege, as viewed through the lens of poetry. courses.title: Counseling Skills
courses.description: Emphasizes experiential learning of a broad range of communication skills needed to form effective helping relationships. Covers client intake procedures and interviewing strategies. Includes laboratory. Prereq for nonmajors; instructor's consent.

courses.title: Professional Ethics
courses.description: Ethical and legal concerns in the professional practice of psychology. Ethical theory and decision-making processes; legal aspects of client-psychologist relationships.

courses.title: Introduction to Counseling Psychology
courses.description: Historical foundations of counseling psychology. Major theories and theorists. Counseling as an ecological and context-sensitive interactive process. Settings and roles of the profession.

courses.title: Theories of Counseling
courses.description: Overview of selected historical and current counseling theories.

courses.title: Counseling Diverse Populations
courses.description: Influence of gender, race, ethnicity, and other factors related to diverse populations on the identity-formation process in contemporary society. Applications to counseling psychology.

courses.title: Theories of Career Development
courses.description: Addresses life-span career development including issues, concepts, and definitions; theories of career development and choice; intervention in strategies; and career resources in the context of a multicultural society.

courses.title: Group Dynamics and Counseling
courses.description: Presents basic elements of general group counseling theory and group dynamics; includes introduction to group-based interventions, guidelines for multicultural practice, ethical and professional issues in group practice, and group leadership.

courses.title: Lifespan Developmental Psychology
courses.description: Understanding continuity and change in human development and the ways in which the development of children, adolescents, and adults can be enhanced.

courses.title: Psychological Assessment II
courses.description: This course develops skills related to psychological assessment and provision of assessment findings to clients and other professionals, focused on personality structure and its relationship to psychopathology. Topics include (a) psychometric properties of instruments, (b) utility of assessment in clinical practice, (c) consideration of context when conducting assessments, (d) potential influences on test results/interpretations, and (e) practicing ethically, with attention to relevant human diversity considerations.

courses.title: Child and Family Interventions
courses.description: Empirically oriented interventions with children and families, ranging from early childhood through adolescence. Integrates developmental and intervention sciences.

courses.title: Psychological Services for Latinos
courses.description: Provide graduate students with content specific to carrying out human services work and research with those who are Latino and/or Spanish-speaking.

courses.title: Latinx Mental Health: [Topic]
courses.description: This course is designed to enhance understanding of Spanish-speaking and Latinx populations in the US with attention to strengths, resilience, social injustice and inequity, and how these influence the health and well-being of Latinx communities as applied in clinical mental health and school settings.

courses.title: Social Aspects of Behavior
courses.description: This course introduces research and concepts related to social influences on human behavior, including prejudice, conformity, aggression, prosocial behavior, internalized social norms, and social cognition.

courses.title: Health Promotion and Equity
courses.description: This course introduces theoretical and empirical work in prevention-focused health psychology, integrating cultural, developmental, and community psychology concepts as they pertain to health related behaviors.

courses.title: Supervision and Consultation
courses.description: Focuses on didactic knowledge of supervision theory, research, models of practice, and evidence-based practices in supervision and consultation and acquisition of supervision and consultation skills. Students refine their knowledge of supervision theory, research, and techniques and integrate these into supervision and consultation activities.

courses.title: Adult Practicum
courses.description: Adult Practicum is a three-term experience over one academic year in which counseling psychology doctoral students work in a clinical setting and provide therapy services to adult clients. Students receive individual and group supervision and training in evidence-based interventions and theoretical models of practice.

courses.title: Child and Family Practicum
courses.description: Child & Family Practicum is a three-term experience over one academic year in which counseling psychology doctoral students work in a clinical setting and provide therapy services to children and families. Students receive individual and group supervision and training in evidence-based interventions and theoretical models of practice.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Introduction to Conflict Resolution
courses.description: Explores up-to-date conflict management theories and practical steps to communicate effectively in sensitive situations.

courses.title: Restorative Justice
courses.description: Provides a critical introduction to the principles and practices of restorative justice.

courses.title: Israel and Palestine
courses.description: Examination of the Palestinian and Israeli conflict. Evolution of the political struggle with a broad look at the human side of conflict, and examination of critical negotiation issues.

courses.title: Dialogue across Differences
courses.description: Introduction to processes and facilitation of discourse and dialogue, with special emphasis on participation. Sequence with CRES 441 .

courses.title: Dialogue Across Differences II
courses.description: Advanced course in dialogic processes and facilitation, with special emphasis on context. Sequence with CRES 440 .

courses.title: Conflicts of Incarceration
courses.description: Issues of crime, incarceration, and justice within the Western context.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: Repeatable.

courses.title: Philosophy of Conflict Resolution
courses.description: Study of how philosophical and theoretical frameworks influence current views and practices of conflict resolution.

courses.title: Negotiation, Bargaining and Persuasion
courses.description: Examines issues that pervade negotiations, including framing arguments, analyzing bargaining conditions, and crafting deals. Basic skills in negotiation, bargaining and persuasion developed through simulated negotiations.

courses.title: Mediation Skills
courses.description: Develop mediation skills such as problem framing, listening, and issue identification and sequencing. Learn to diagnose problems, clarify facts and craft interventions.

courses.title: Facilitation
courses.description: Fundamentals of facilitating group discussions and decision-making.

courses.title: Culture, Power, and Conflict Resolution I
courses.description: This course introduces multiple approaches to conflict drawing from distinct cultural traditions. It asks students to consider how cultural differences and power dynamics impact how people approach conflict and conflict resolution.

courses.title: Culture, Power, and Conflict Resolution II
courses.description: Students will explore creative ways to engage cultural difference and power as central assumptions in conflict resolution.

courses.title: Culture, Power, and Conflict Resolution III
courses.description: In this course students theorize how they would like to engage cross cultural dynamics and existing power structures in their own practice.

courses.title: Psychology of Conflict
courses.description: Examines the psychological sources, nature, and functions of conflict, covering multiple levels of analysis relevant to intrapersonal, interpersonal, intragroup, and intergroup conflict.

courses.title: Managing Conflict in Organizations
courses.description: Prepares students to assist in managing disputes within organization. Students will receive a basic introduction to organizational context, and structure, leadership and communication styles, and sources of workplace disputes. The course also provides an overview of the processes by which organizations typically resolve disputes.

courses.title: Research Methods
courses.description: Explores questions that research may encounter or raise, and how to resolve them. Considers both qualitative and quantitative research methods.

courses.title: Capstone Seminar
courses.description: Provides student with opportunities to systemically consider lessons from their practicum experiences. Class sessions based on student fieldwork.

courses.title: Academic Capstone: Course Concentration
courses.description: Fulfills the course component of the course concentration final project for conflict resolution students.

courses.title: Environmental Conflict Resolution
courses.description: Students learn and critically examine methods of environmental conflict resolution, including conflict assessment, negotiation, decision-making, adaptive management, collaboration, and public participation.

courses.title: Family Mediation
courses.description: Preparation for work-related experiences in family mediation, specifically domestic relations involving custody and parenting time.

courses.title: Adversarial Processes
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the primary adversarial or adjudicative processes used to resolve civil disputes: Litigation and Arbitration.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Kidd Workshop I
courses.description: Introduction to an intensive, yearlong sequence devoted to the study and practice of poetry, fiction, and nonfiction.

courses.title: Introduction to Poetry Writing
courses.description: Introduction to forms and techniques of writing poetry.

courses.title: Kidd Workshop II Poetry
courses.description: Second in a yearlong sequence devoted to the studying and practicing poetry and fiction, with an emphasis on poetry. Projects include beginning to develop an individual line of inquiry as well as workshopping original poetry.

courses.title: Introduction to Fiction Writing
courses.description: Introduction to forms and techniques of writing fiction.

courses.title: Kidd Workshop II: Fiction
courses.description: Second class in a yearlong sequence devoted to studying and practicing poetry and fiction, with an emphasis on fiction. Projects include beginning to develop an individual line of inquiry as well as workshopping original fiction.

courses.title: Intermediate Poetry Writing
courses.description: Intermediate-level study of poetry writing.

courses.title: Kidd Workshop III: Poetry
courses.description: Third class in a yearlong sequence devoted to studying and practicing poetry and fiction, with a special emphasis on poetry. Projects include completing the individual line of inquiry, an original portfolio, and the Kidd Workshops student anthology.

courses.title: Intermediate Creative Nonfiction
courses.description: Intermediate-level study of literary nonfiction writing.

courses.title: Intermediate Fiction Writing
courses.description: Intermediate-level study of fiction writing.

courses.description: Third class in a yearlong sequence devoted to studying and practicing poetry and fiction, with a special emphasis on fiction. Projects include completing the individual line of inquiry, an original portfolio, and the Kidd Workshops student anthology.

courses.title: MFA Poetry Workshop
courses.description: Concentration on student writing in a workshop setting. Open only to students admitted to creative writing master of fine arts program in poetry.

courses.title: MFA Fiction Workshop
courses.description: Concentration on student writing in a workshop setting. Open only to students admitted to creative writing master of fine arts program in fiction.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Computer and Information Security
courses.description: This course introduces fundamental concepts, terminologies, principles, methods, and scenarios of computer and information security.

courses.title: Fluency with Information Technology
courses.description: Introduction to the study of computer-based information systems (information technology). Overview of computing, including basics of the internet and world wide web and issues such as security and privacy. Students create websites using HTML and CSS.

courses.title: Introduction to Web Programming
courses.description: Project-based approach to learning computer programming by building interactive web pages using JavaScript and HTML. Programming concepts including structured and object-oriented program design. CS 110 recommended preparation.

courses.title: Introduction to Programming and Problem Solving
courses.description: Computational problem solving, algorithm design, data structures, and programming using a multi-paradigm programming language. Introduces techniques for program design, testing, and debugging.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Special Studies in Computer Science: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Computer Science I
courses.description: Basic concepts and practices of computer science. Topics include algorithmic problem solving, levels of abstraction, object-oriented design and programming, software organization, analysis of algorithm and data structures. Sequence with CS 211 , CS 212 .

courses.title: Computer Science II
courses.description: Basic concepts and practices of computer science. Topics include algorithmic problem solving, levels of abstraction, object-oriented design and programming, software organization, analysis of algorithm and data structures. Sequence with CS 210 , CS 212 .

courses.title: Computer Science III
courses.description: Basic concepts and practices of computer science. Topics include algorithmic problem solving, levels of abstraction, object-oriented design and programming, software organization, analysis of algorithm and data structures. Sequence with CS 210 , CS 211 .

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Repeatable.

courses.title: Intermediate Data Structures
courses.description: Design and analysis of data structures as means of engineering efficient software; attention to data abstraction and encapsulation. Lists, trees, heaps, stacks, queues, dictionaries, priority queues.

courses.title: Computer Organization
courses.description: Introduction to computer organization and instruction-set architecture -- digital logic design, binary arithmetic, design of central processing unit and memory, machine-level programming.

courses.title: Intermediate Algorithms
courses.description: Algorithm design, worst-case and average-behavior analysis, correctness, computational complexity.

courses.title: Introduction to Software Engineering
courses.description: A project-intensive introduction to software engineering intended to build skills, knowledge, and habits of mind that prepare students for 400-level computer science courses, internships, and other software.

courses.title: C/C++ and Unix
courses.description: Practical software design and programming activities in a C/C++ and Unix environment, with emphasis on the details of C/C++ and good programming style and practices.

courses.title: System and Security Administration Lab
courses.description: This lab course is designed to help students develop hands-on skills in system and security administration, based on the following relevant Cyber Defense knowledge units defined by the National Security Agency (NSA) National Centers of Academic Excellence in Cybersecurity (NCAE-C): Operating System Administration (OSA), which provides students with skill to perform basic operations involved in system administration of operating systems; and Operating Systems Hardening (OSH), which provides students with the ability to apply methods such as managing applications, services, and network ports to improve the security and robustness of operating systems.

courses.title: Applied Cryptography
courses.description: This course provides a systematic study of cryptography and its application. It covers cryptographic algorithms, including symmetric-key cryptography, public-key cryptography, cryptanalysis, cryptographic hash functions, and their usage toward message authentication codes, digital signatures, key management and distribution, and user authentication protocols.

courses.title: Machine Learning for Data Science
courses.description: Introduction to Machine Learning, with an emphasis on topics relevant for data science. Multilisted with DSCI 372M .

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Supervised College Teaching
courses.description: A student, under faculty supervision and sponsorship, accepts responsibility for teaching a university course.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: Supervised consulting. Students provide learning assistance in computer science courses.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Opportunity to study in greater depth specific topics arising out of other courses.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Advanced Data Structures
courses.description: Complex structures, storage management, sorting and searching, hashing, storage of texts, and information compression.

courses.title: Operating Systems
courses.description: Principles of operating system design. Process and memory management, concurrency, scheduling, input-output and file systems, security.

courses.title: Automata Theory
courses.description: Provides a mathematical basis for computability and complexity. Models of computation, formal languages, Turing machines, solvability. Nondeterminism and complexity classes.

courses.title: Software Methodology I
courses.description: Technical and nontechnical aspects of software development, including specification, planning, design, development, management and maintenance of software projects. Student teams complete projects.

courses.title: Software Methodology II
courses.description: Application of concepts and methodologies covered in CS 422 . Student teams complete a large system design and programming project. Final system specification, test plan, user documentation, and system walk throughs.

courses.title: Principles of Programming Languages
courses.description: Syntax and semantics. Scope rules, environments, stores, denoted and expressed values, procedures, and parameters. Definitional interpreters. Types, overloading, parametric polymorphism, and inheritance. Varieties of abstraction.

courses.title: Computer Architecture
courses.description: RISC (reduced instruction-set computer) and CISC (complex instruction-set computer) design, storage hierarchies, high-performance processor design, pipelining, vector processing, networks, performance analysis.

courses.title: Introduction to Parallel Computing
courses.description: Parallel architecture, theory, algorithms, and programming with emphasis on parallel programming, focusing on models, languages, libraries, and runtime systems.

courses.title: Introduction to Networks
courses.description: Principles of computer network design. Link technologies, packet switching, routing, inter-networking, reliability. Internet protocols. Programming assignments focus on protocol design.

courses.title: Computer and Network Security
courses.description: Security for various aspects of computers and networks. Elementary cryptography, program security, trusted operating systems, network security, privacy, and legal and ethical issues.

courses.title: Computer and Network Security II
courses.description: This course covers security threats and solutions for distributed systems and networks, particularly the Internet, the Internet of Things, and distributed systems based on them.

courses.title: Secure Software Development
courses.description: This course establishes a foundation for applying security principles to the lifecycle of software development in order to minimize software vulnerabilities and counter cyber threats.

courses.title: Computer and Network Security Practicum
courses.description: This course is designed to help students solidify knowledge and develop hands-on skills in computer network security. Students will practice various attack and defense techniques. Specifically, we will systematically study each layer of the Internet, discuss the design and implementation vulnerabilities at each layer, and demonstrate how attacks may exploit the vulnerabilities. This course will cover many classic attacks in great technical detail in addition to high-level concepts and theories, to help students deeply understand why and how each attack works. We'll also discuss how each attack may be handled, and students will learn defense techniques such as firewalls, etc.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Graphics
courses.description: Introduction to the hardware, geometrical transforms, interaction techniques, and shape representation schemes that are important in interactive computer graphics. Programming assignments using contemporary graphics hardware and software systems.

courses.title: User Interfaces
courses.description: Introduction to user interface software engineering. Emphasis on theory of interface design, understanding the behavior of the user, and implementing programs on advanced systems.

courses.title: Modeling and Simulation
courses.description: Theoretical foundations and practical problems for the modeling and computer simulation of discrete and continuous systems. Simulation languages, empirical validation, applications in computer science.

courses.title: Database Processing
courses.description: Fundamental concepts of DBMS. Data modeling, relational models and normal forms. File organization and index structures. SQL, embedded SQL, and concurrency control. Students cannot receive credit for both CS 451 and DSCI 431 .

courses.title: Data Mining
courses.description: Databases, machine learning, artificial intelligence, statistics, and data visualization. Examines data warehouses, data preprocessing, association and classification rule mining, and cluster analysis. Students cannot receive credit for both CS 453 and DSCI 435 .

courses.title: Introduction to Compilers
courses.description: Lexical analysis, parsing, attribution, code generation.

courses.title: Introduction to Artificial Intelligence
courses.description: Basic themes, issues, and techniques of artificial intelligence, including agent architecture, knowledge representation and reasoning, problem solving and planning, game playing, and learning.

courses.title: Machine Learning
courses.description: A broad introduction to machine learning and its established algorithms. Topics include concept learning, decision trees, neural network.

courses.title: Probabilistic Methods for Artificial Intelligence
courses.description: Fundamental techniques for representing problems as probability distributions, performing inference, and learning from data. Topics include Bayesian and Markov networks, variable elimination, loopy belief propagation, and parameter.

courses.title: Game Programming
courses.description: Introduction to theory topics related to game programming, including linear algebra, 3D graphics, lighting, audio, physics, and artificial intelligence. This course provides an opportunity to build games using a commercial game engine, reinforced by best practices in terms of implementation and project management.

courses.title: Distributed Systems
courses.description: Principles of distributed computer systems: interprocess communication, distributed file systems, distributed timing and synchronization, distributed programming, transactions, process scheduling, distributed shared memory.

courses.title: Advanced Parallel Computing
courses.description: Advanced topics in parallel processing including massively parallel computer architecture, supercomputers, parallelizing compiler technology, performance evaluation, parallel programming languages, parallel applications.

courses.title: Computer Networks
courses.description: Advanced issues in computer networks, focusing on research to extend the services offered by the Internet.

courses.title: Advanced Network Security
courses.description: Classic and state-of-the-art research topics in network security; threats and attacks, defense algorithms and mechanisms, measurement and evaluation of both security problems and solutions. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Writing in Computer Research
courses.description: Students learn to provide and accept constructive criticism of writing samples in a workshop format.

courses.title: Data Science
courses.description: Data science is the development of methods to study large and complex data sets. Methods that scale to very large data sets are of particular interest. This course introduces state-of-art data science methods focused on processing very large data sets of real-world data.

courses.title: First Year Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: DAN 125 provides a general introduction to dance as a field of study in higher education with a specific focus on opportunities in Dance and allied studies at UO.

courses.title: Dance Improvisation: [Topic]
courses.description: Dance Improvisation covers a range of solo and group improvisational techniques to develop improvisational skill and heighten creative instincts. Attention will be given to developing safe practices to heighten movement exploration. Topics vary based on the chosen focus of the teacher of record.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Performance, Production Experience, Repertory.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Screendance: History and Theory
courses.description: This Arts and Letters group satisfying course explores the historical, theoretical, and cultural contexts of screendance, an interdisciplinary art form at the intersection of dance, film, and new media technologies. It focuses on critical inquiries into screendance while locating that subject in a broader context.

courses.title: Looking at Dance
courses.description: Overview of dance as a cultural and aesthetic experience. Examines its meaning and impact on contemporary United States society.

courses.title: Dance Composition I
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the elements of dance composition. Students will create studies that concentrate on details of formal compositional structure as well as some more contemporary models

courses.title: Dance Production I
courses.description: Introduction to production planning, management, lighting, design, costuming, and publicity for dance. Practical experience in Dougherty Dance Theatre. Students cannot receive credit for both DAN 255 and any Theater Arts production courses ( TA 211 , TA 212 , TA 321 , TA 322 , TA 323 , TA 324 , TA 467 ). Sequence with DAN 355 .

courses.title: Dance Somatics
courses.description: Exploration of patterning in movement. Various body therapies—Bartenieff Fundamentals, ideokinesis, and body-mind centering—provide a framework for experiential investigations.

courses.title: Musical Elements of Dance
courses.description: Explores musical elements of dance and dancing; especially music/dance culture, rhythm, melody, phrasing and form. Sequence with DAN 458 .

courses.title: Anatomy of Human Movement
courses.description: An introduction to scientific reasoning focusing on methodology and current research in dance anatomy and kinesiology.

courses.title: Contact Improvisation: [Topic]
courses.description: This course is designed to develop skills, experience and awareness of contact improvisation. Sequence with DAN 171 .

courses.title: Dance Loft Performance
courses.description: Dance Loft Performance is a beginning level repertory dance course modeled after a professional choreographic rehearsal process and performance experience. Participants in this course will perform the work of a student choreographer, under the supervision of a faculty mentor, in a single informal performance.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: African Dance Aesthetics
courses.description: Using the field of dance studies to examine African dance aesthetics in popular culture, daily media landscapes, and expressive cultures in Africa and the diaspora.

courses.title: Dance Composition II
courses.description: This course is a continuation of skill development from Dance Composition I. Students will create studies that concentrate on details of formal compositional structure as well as some more contemporary models. The goal of this class is not to make finished dances, but to examine and refine elements of the craft that contribute to dance-making, to further develop and deepen an individual movement awareness and vocabulary, and to develop a shared creative dialogue from which to begin the process of training one’s critical eye for movement form. Sequence with DAN 252 .

courses.title: Dance Production II
courses.description: Extended application of skills and procedures used in producing a concert. Practical backstage work; pre- and postconcert sessions.

courses.title: Dance Kinesiology
courses.description: This course explores advanced movement concepts begun in DAN 260-Anatomy of Movement. We will investigate the relationships between different anatomical structures and various body systems. Concepts of Anatomy, Kinesiology, Motor Control and Somatics will be explored through readings, discussions, and physical embodiment practices. Included is analysis and direct application to dance technique and expression. Sequence with DAN 260 .

courses.title: Improvisation II
courses.description: A continuation of study of Dance Improvisation including structured improvisation geared towards facility with movement invention, some aspects of contact improvisation and site-specific exploration. Students will enhance and deepen their fundamental skills and learn to problem solve “in the moment” at a more sophisticated level of immediate movement discourse. Sequence with DAN 171 .

courses.title: Student Dance Concert Performance
courses.description: Student Dance Concert Performance is an intermediate level practical studio course. Participants in this course will perform the work of a student choreographer, under the supervision of a faculty mentor, performed in a run of fully produced performances.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Apprenticeship under the guidance of a supervising teacher in areas such as teaching, arts management, administration, and dance production.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Choreographic Analysis, Contemporary Issues.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics include rehearsal and performance for department-sponsored events.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: Current topics are Choreography, Production Design, and Management.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Current topics include Neuromuscular Bases of Dance, Topics in Technique, Composition III.

courses.title: Student Dance Concert
courses.description: Students apply ideas learned about concert choreography, production, and management. In a cooperative venture, students produce dance works in Dougherty Dance Theatre.

courses.title: Senior Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: This multi term topics course focuses on professional development within dance for senior dance majors.

courses.title: Dema African Performance Ensemble: [Topic]
courses.description: Dema is the concept of total performance experience through dance, music, singing, storytelling, and costume-making through education, teaching, and to entertaining. Dema is a means of encouraging independence, critical thinking, self-worth, and freedom of the mind for students to discover their voices.

courses.title: Dance and Power
courses.description: This course uses identity as a lens through which we examine structures of power in dance, including, but not limited to, intersections of race, gender, religion, sexual orientation, ethnicity, nationality, socioeconomic status, language, (dis)ability, age, religious commitment, and political perspective. Sequence with DAN 444 .

courses.title: Contemporary Issues in Dance
courses.description: This course engages critical conversations about dance in contemporary society with emphasis on understanding various ways that dance functions within communities, how dance can participate and promote social change, and ways that dance makers are expanding possibility in dance.

courses.title: Dance Composition III
courses.description: Advanced skills in dance composition to engage critical creative processes for the development of substantial choreographies. Sequence with DAN 351, DAN 352 .

courses.title: Music for Dancers
courses.description: Surveys musical form, style, and expressive content as it relates to dance. Examines the interrelationship of elements of music and dance in significant works from a range of cultures.

courses.title: Faculty Dance Concert Performance
courses.description: Participants in this course will perform the work of a faculty choreographer, or guest artist, in a run of fully produced dance concerts.

courses.title: Student Dance Collective: [Topic]
courses.description: Student Dance Collective is an advanced dance performance ensemble modeled after a professional repertory dance company, which includes a collaborative rehearsal process and multiple live performances.

courses.title: Teaching Dance
courses.description: Application of teaching theories, course planning methods, teaching resources and techniques. Emphasis on teaching in university situation.

courses.title: Advanced Movement Practice
courses.description: Advanced Movement Practice is a synthesis of physicality, creative practice, and theory whereby students will investigate the relationships between form, expression, technique, style and identity through embodiment, discussion, readings, writing, video and performance.

courses.description: Current topics include Neuromuscular Bases of Dance, Topics in Technique, Composition III.

courses.description: Dema is the concept of total performance experience through dance, music, singing, storytelling, and costume-making through education, teaching, and to entertaining. courses.description: DANC 170 Contemporary I is the study of basic principles of dance movement in the contemporary idiom with an emphasis on the elements of space, time and energy.

courses.title: Ballet I
courses.description: Ballet I explores basic movement vocabulary in the ballet idiom.

courses.title: Hip Hop I
courses.description: Hip hop I is a beginning level hip hop technique class incorporating guided warm-up exercises, choreographed sequences, improvisation, and performance.

courses.title: African I
courses.description: DANC 185 is a beginning level studio course in dance vocabulary from the African diaspora.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Tango, Hip-Hop, Salsa, Drumming, and Swing.

courses.title: Contemporary II
courses.description: DANC 270 Contemporary II is intermediate dance training designed to build upon previously developed technical skills and artistic expression, with a focus on contemporary aesthetics.

courses.title: Ballet II
courses.description: Ballet II is an intermediate studio technique course in the ballet idiom.

courses.title: Hip Hop II First Term
courses.description: Hip hop II is an intermediate level hip hop technique class incorporating guided warm-up exercises, choreographed sequences, improvisation, and performance. Sequence with DANC 175 .

courses.title: Hip Hop II Second Term
courses.description: This course advances intermediate Hip Hop skills by integrating the instructor’s specialty style (e.g., commercial choreography, Krump, popping, locking, breaking) into groove-based choreography. Students will also explore the process of producing choreography for performance and film. Sequence with DANC 175 , DANC 275 .

courses.title: African II
courses.description: African II offers students an intermediate level integration of African movement, traditions, culture, history, music, and storytelling.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Contemporary III
courses.description: This studio technique course is designed for advanced students to enhance skills in modern/contemporary concert dance. Emphasis is on technical and expressive aspects, including stability, mobility, alignment, musicality, and dynamic range. Students will also engage in readings, writing, viewing, and discussions to deepen their understanding of this art form. Sequence with DANC 170 , DANC 270 .

courses.title: Ballet III
courses.description: This class Infuses the movement practice of ballet with aesthetic, historic, cultural, social, and political learning and seeks to enhance physical and artistic abilities in ballet for expressive movement, moving towards precision, presence, and artistic awareness. Ballet III is an advanced technique studio course in the ballet idiom. Sequence with DANC 172 , DANC 272 .

courses.title: Hip Hop III
courses.description: This advanced Hip Hop course focuses on developing freestyle techniques and battle culture by building on previous training. Students will enhance their skills through weekly freestyle video journals, tracking their progress, and providing peer feedback within their crew. The course explores "open style" battling, where individuality and movement history fuse for originality, alongside research into giants of the freestyle battle scene to analyze their strategies and success. The culmination will be a freestyle exhibition or battle.

courses.title: African III
courses.description: African Dance III offers students an advanced exploration of tradition, culture, history, music, storytelling, improvisation, and choreography through the study of dances from the African continent. These neo-traditional forms will also be explored in reference to the application of African Aesthetics to American Diasporic dance forms. Sequence with DANC 185 , DANC 285 .

courses.title: Foundations of Data Science I
courses.description: This course utilizes a quantitative approach to explore fundamental concepts in data science. Students will develop key skills in programming and statistical inference as they interact with real-world data sets across a variety of domains. Ethical and privacy concerns are explored. Sequence with DSCI 102 .

courses.title: Foundations of Data Science II
courses.description: This course expands upon critical concepts and skills introduced in DSCI 101 . Topics include the normal distribution, confidence intervals, regression, and classifiers. Sequence with DSCI 101 .

courses.title: Principles and Techniques of Data Science
courses.description: Intermediate and advanced techniques in data science. Topics include managing data using software programs, data cleaning, handling text, dimensionality, principle component analysis, regression, classification and inference.

courses.title: Probability and Statistics for Data Science
courses.description: Introduction to probability and statistics, with an emphasis upon topics relevant for data science. Students cannot get credit for both MATH 343 and DSCI 345M / MATH 345M .

courses.title: Humanities Research Data Management
courses.description: This course provides students with theoretical and practical experience in collecting, processing, archiving, and publishing humanities data (images, video, sound, text, maps, etc.) gathered from galleries, libraries, archives, and museums (GLAMs). Multilisted with LIB 350M .

courses.title: Machine Learning for Data Science
courses.description: Introduction to Machine Learning, with an emphasis on topics relevant for data science. Multilisted with CS 372M .

courses.title: Capstone Project
courses.description: This course for Data Science majors provides a student the opportunity to apply the theoretical knowledge and techniques acquired during the Data Science degree curriculum to a project involving real data from the student’s domain of specialization. Requires an average 3.75 GPA in courses required.

courses.title: Data Access and Management
courses.description: Data management using structured and unstructured databases. Students learn to interface with common database systems. Working with structured databases, students learn the fundamentals of relational data and query languages. Students also learn to work with unstructured databases. Students cannot receive credit for both DSCI 431 and CS 451 .

courses.title: Data Mining, Exploration, and Visualization
courses.description: Effective data summarization and description. This course will teach students how to efficiently develop intuition for a dataset. Best practices in data manipulation, including key concepts in data cleaning and tidying will be covered. Students will learn how to summarize data with summary statistics and ordination techniques, as well as visualize data using a range of transformations and plotting approaches. Students cannot receive credit for both DSCI 435 and CS 453 .

courses.description: Data management using structured and unstructured databases. Students learn to interface with common database systems. Working with structured databases, students learn the fundamentals of relational data and query languages. Students also learn to work with unstructured databases. Students cannot receive credit for both DSCI 531 and CS 551 .

courses.title: Data Mining, Exploration, and Visualization
courses.description: Effective data summarization and description. This course will teach students how to efficiently develop intuition for a dataset. Best practices in data manipulation, including key concepts in data cleaning and tidying will be covered. Students will learn how to summarize data with summary statistics and ordination techniques, as well as visualize data using a range of transformations and plotting approaches. Students cannot receive credit for both DSCI 535 and CS 553 .

courses.title: Foundational Mathematics for Data Science
courses.description: Key math for data science models, algorithms, and processes. Taught at a level accessible to those with minimal college-level math. Topics include differential calculus including higher-order and partial derivatives, linear algebra including linear transformations and eigenanalysis, and select topics in discrete mathematics.

courses.title: Foundational Statistics for Data Science
courses.description: An introduction to data visualization and statistical inference. Taught at a level accessible to those with no background in statistics. Topics include basic simulation, publication-quality plot production, parameter estimation, hypothesis testing and p-values, linear models, and non-parametric statistical analysis.

courses.title: Foundational Programming for Data Science
courses.description: An introduction to computer programming for data science. Taught at a level accessible for those with no prior programming experience. Topics include an introduction to programming, debugging, reproducible code and documentation, data cleaning and visualization, libraries, and a basic introduction to simple machine-learning approaches.

courses.title: Statistics for Data Science
courses.description: Probability for classical and Bayesian inference. This course will cover statistical uncertainty as applied to hypothesis testing, experimental design, and parameter estimation. Students will gain exposure to common probability distributions in the Sciences, and their application in statistical models, validation of model assumptions, and hypothesis formulation and testing. Basic maximum likelihood methods using linear models (regression, ANOVA) will be covered. Will include an introduction to Bayesian statistics.

courses.title: Machine Learning I
courses.description: Applied concepts and methods in machine learning. This course will cover fundamental concepts, algorithms, and techniques. High-level conceptual and statistical tradeoffs in machine learning will feature prominently, as will key processes in model optimization, including resampling methods and an introduction to gradient descent. Both supervised and unsupervised learning techniques covered.

courses.title: Machine Learning II
courses.description: Advanced methods in machine learning. This course will feature a diverse range of techniques to round out student mastery of cutting-edge predictive modeling. Includes clustering and multivariate ordination redux, Bayesian model fitting procedures with Hamiltonian Monte Carlo, and deep learning with various classes of artificial neural network. Students will also gain exposure to the art of feature engineering and learn how to define effective, tailored loss functions. Sequence with DSCI 633 .

courses.title: Synthetic Project Capstone
courses.description: Project-driven application of data science knowledge and skills. Students will gather, tidy, visualize, and analyze a large data set conducive to a predictive modeling problem. Curated data sets from biological and materials sciences will be provided by program faculty and colleagues. Key learning outcomes include a reproducible workflow published in a code repository, original design and implementation of a machine learning model, and communications of finding in a written report.

courses.description: Introduction to distinctive features of Japan's linguistic, literary, artistic, and religio-philosophical heritage. Includes guest lectures, films.

courses.title: Chinese, Japanese, and Korean Phonetics
courses.description: This course introduces students to the articulatory and acoustic analyses of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean sound systems. Students acquire knowledge of the sound systems of the three languages and to engage in the examination of current issues involving Chinese, Japanese and Korean phonetics.

courses.title: Second-Language Acquisition
courses.description: Analyzes how important theories and concepts in second-language acquisition apply specifically to the learning of Chinese, Japanese, and Korean.

courses.title: Chinese, Japanese, and Korean Pedagogy
courses.description: Advanced language pedagogy; includes investigation of issues pertinent to the teaching of East Asian languages.

courses.title: East Asian Psycholinguistics
courses.description: Language is a fundamental aspect of human beings and is central to social life. In this course, we will consider a range of topics relevant to our ability to comprehend and produce language, focusing on East Asian languages. We will discuss various topics ranging from the nature of abstract linguistic representation to the relationships between language, cognition, and culture.

courses.description: Training in Chinese and Japanese language instruction through lectures, observations, and teaching practicums.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Dissertation
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Critical Approaches
courses.description: Introduces recent research and methodologies in the fields of Chinese, Japanese and Korean traditional and modern literary, cultural, film, and linguistic studies.

courses.title: Linguistics Research and Bibliography
courses.description: Provides critical training in quantitative and qualitative methods and bibliography research in linguistics and language pedagogy.

courses.title: Contemporary Economic Issues
courses.description: Examines contemporary public policy using economic principles. Topics may include balanced budgets and tax reform, unemployment, health care, poverty and income redistribution, environmental policy, and international trade policy.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Principles of Microeconomics
courses.description: Examines how consumers and firms make choices when facing scarce resources, and how those choices are related to government policy and market outcomes, such as prices and output.

courses.title: Principles of Macroeconomics
courses.description: Examines the aggregate activity of a market economy, economic growth, inflation, unemployment, and the use of fiscal and monetary policy to address macroeconomic problems.

courses.title: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
courses.description: Consumer and firm behavior, market structures. General equilibrium theory, welfare economics, collective choice, rules for evaluating economic policy. Students cannot receive credit for both EC 311 and FIN 311 .

courses.title: Intermediate Macroeconomic Theory
courses.description: Determination of aggregate income, employment, and unemployment; evaluation of macroeconomic policies.

courses.title: Introduction to Econometrics I
courses.description: Application of classical statistical techniques of estimation, hypothesis testing, and regression to economic models. Includes laboratory section.

courses.title: Introduction to Game Theory
courses.description: Introductory course in game theory. Develops game-theoretic methods of rational decision making and equilibriums, using many in-class active games.

courses.title: Behavioral Economics and Its Applications
courses.description: This is an introductory course on behavioral economics, which brings together economics and psychology to understand human decision making in a wide range of real life applications.

courses.title: Urban and Regional Economic Problems
courses.description: Topics may include urban and metropolitan growth, land use, race and poverty, education systems, slums and urban renewal, transportation, crime, and pollution and environmental quality.

courses.title: Resource and Environmental Economic Issues
courses.description: Economic analysis of replenishable and nonreplenishable natural resources; environmental issues and policies.

courses.title: Issues in Public Economics
courses.description: Principles and problems of government financing. Expenditures, revenues, debt, and financial administration. Production by government versus production by the private sector. Tax measures to control externalities.

courses.title: Labor Market Issues
courses.description: Topics may include the changing structure of employment, the minimum wage, the dual labor market hypothesis, collective bargaining, discrimination, and health and safety regulation.

courses.title: Issues in Industrial Organization
courses.description: Topics may include analysis of market power, trends in industrial structure, the role of advertising, pricing policies and inflation, impact of social regulation (e.g., OSHA, EPA), and international comparisons.

courses.title: Money and Banking
courses.description: Operations of commercial banks, the Federal Reserve System, and the Treasury that affect the United States monetary system.

courses.title: International Economic Issues
courses.description: Exchange across international boundaries, theory of comparative advantage, balance of payments and adjustments, international financial movements, exchange rates and international financial institutions, trade restrictions and policy.

courses.title: Problems and Issues in the Developing Economies
courses.description: Topics may include the role of central planning, capital formation, population growth, agriculture, health and education, interaction between economic and cultural change, and the "North-South debate."

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Internship
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Yearly offerings vary depending on interests and needs of students and on availability of faculty members.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Advanced Microeconomic Theory
courses.description: Advanced theory of consumer and firm behavior, market structures.

courses.title: Foundations of Economic Policy Analysis
courses.description: Advanced theoretical foundations of economic policy design and analysis.

courses.title: Advanced Macroeconomic Theory
courses.description: Advanced theory about the determination of aggregate income, employment, unemployment; evaluation of macroeconomic policies.

courses.title: Economic Analysis of Community Issues I
courses.description: Hands-on experience applying economic analysis and econometrics to problems that face local community nonprofits and government agencies.

courses.title: Economic Analysis of Community Issues II
courses.description: Hands-on experience applying economic analysis and econometrics to problems that face local community nonprofits and government agencies.

courses.title: Introduction to Econometrics II
courses.description: Analysis of departures from classical regression assumptions, corrections, and other related topics. Includes laboratory section.

courses.title: Economic Forecasting
courses.description: Basic techniques of economic forecasting that are typically used in a business environment.

courses.title: Econometrics
courses.description: Introductory topics in probability theory and statistical inference; regression problems of autocorrelation, heteroskedasticity, multicollinearity, and lagged dependent variables; special single-equation estimating techniques; the identification problem in a simultaneous equation setting; development of simultaneous equation estimating procedures.

courses.title: Econometrics
courses.description: Introductory topics in probability theory and statistical inference; regression problems of autocorrelation, heteroskedasticity, multicollinearity, and lagged dependent variables; special single-equation estimating techniques; the identification problem in a simultaneous equation setting; development of simultaneous equation estimating procedures.

courses.title: Econometrics
courses.description: Introductory topics in probability theory and statistical inference; regression problems of autocorrelation, heteroskedasticity, multicollinearity, and lagged dependent variables; special single-equation estimating techniques; the identification problem in a simultaneous equation setting; development of simultaneous equation estimating procedures.

courses.title: Games and Decisions
courses.description: Game-theoretic methods of decision-making. Topics may include extensive-form games, noncredible threats, subgame perfect equilibrium, strategic-form games, undominated strategies, Nash equilibrium, coalitional games, and the core.

courses.title: Behavioral and Experimental Economics
courses.description: Investigates the "rational choice" model and behavioral alternatives, using laboratory experiments. Topics may include altruism, auctions, bargaining, behavioral finance, hyperbolic discounting, and decision-making under uncertainty.

courses.title: Urban and Regional Economics
courses.description: Location theory; urbanization and metropolitan growth; regional analysis; intraurban rent, location and land use, size distribution of urban areas; welfare economics, political economy, and urban problems.

courses.title: Environmental Economics
courses.description: Introduction to the field that includes theoretical environmental policy, issues in environmental regulation, and empirical techniques used by practitioners.

courses.title: Public Economics
courses.description: Theory of public goods and their optimal provision. Collective choice versus private choice and implications for resource allocation and efficiency.

courses.title: Health Economics
courses.description: Includes moral hazard and adverse selection; incentives faced by health care providers through reimbursement, managed care, and malpractice; rationale for government intervention in the health care sector.

courses.title: Political Economy
courses.description: Covers the economic problems that arise when the government is a self-interested actor in the economy. We study political agency, voting, the economic origins of political institutions and the size and number of nations.

courses.title: Computational Economics
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to modern computational economics. Computational economics allows us to tackle problems that would be impossible or too time-consuming to solve by hand.

courses.title: Labor Economics
courses.description: Supply and demand for labor, wage determination under various market structures, minimum wage and worker exploitation, human capital investments, labor market signaling and sorting, discrimination, uncertainty, and job matching.

courses.title: Issues in Labor Economics
courses.description: Topics may include the determination of wages, employment, and unemployment; globalization and immigration; income inequality; internal labor markets; the role of unions; human capital, education, and schools.

courses.title: Theories of Industrial Organization
courses.description: Theories, quantitative measures, and institutional descriptions of the structure, conduct, and results that characterize American industry. Emphasis is on the determinants and consequences of market power.

courses.title: Monetary Theory
courses.description: Money creation, deficit finance, and taxation in monetary economies. Topics may include the government budget constraint, causes and consequences of inflation, Richardian equivalence, and seigniorage.

courses.title: International Finance
courses.description: Foreign exchange markets, interaction between spot and forward markets, speculation and interest arbitrage, balance-of-payments accounting, measures of deficits and surpluses, "open-economy" macroeconomic issues.

courses.title: International Trade
courses.description: Theories of international trade, direction of trade flows, determination of prices and volumes in international trade, tariffs, quotas, customs unions, free versus restricted trade.

courses.title: Economics of Globalization
courses.description: Applications of economic theories and empirical methods to globalization issues: market integration of goods and factors, international labor and environmental standards, economic growth and income inequality, financial stability, global governance.

courses.title: Multinational Corporations
courses.description: Economist's perspective of multinational corporations. Explores the policies governments use to influence corporate behavior and patterns of investment; taxation as a tool for implementing public policy.

courses.title: Economic Growth and Development
courses.description: Experience of developed countries and theories of development. Analysis of specific development programs, role of agriculture, sources of investment, techniques and strategies of investment planning.

courses.title: Terminal Project
