courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: K-12 Education Policy for School Leaders
courses.description: Introduction to the education policy process at the state and local levels, focusing on school leaders. Emphasis placed on examining policies that further marginalize students and staff that have been historically underserved and underrepresented.

courses.title: Communications and Community Relations
courses.description: Principal Licensure candidates learn proactive systems, strategies, and protocols for communicating with staff, students, parents and community members. Students develop an overall communication plan for their school or district location for ensuring the effective two-way flow of communication.

courses.title: Ethical Leadership
courses.description: Prepare Principal Licensure candidates to become ethical leaders that lead with an equity lens at the forefront of the leadership decisions they make. Examine the ethical implications of school board policies and how to apply an equity lens to these policies.

courses.title: School Improvement Through Curriculum and Assessment
courses.description: Develop the knowledge and skills related to leading curriculum, assessment and instruction at the building level. Analyze curriculum frameworks, state and national standards, effective strategies and assessment practices and leading professional learning will be examined. Create a school improvement plan.

courses.title: Leading Educational Change Initiatives
courses.description: Overview of current thought on how to lead successful organizational change initiatives. Readings come from the fields of business as well as education, and the focus is on leading change initiatives in educational settings.

courses.title: Supervision of Teaching and Learning
courses.description: Explores the theory, research and practical applications of supervision and evaluation. Analysis of supervisory behaviors that lead to improvement of teaching and learning, that in turn lead to improved outcomes for all students, particularly those students who have been underserved or historically marginalized.

courses.title: Oregon School Management and Budget
courses.description: Prepare future administrators for designing a system for developing, allocating and managing a school-based budget, including opportunities for staff input into the budget cycle and specific strategies for engaging the community, particularly those parts of the community that are underrepresented.

courses.title: Leading for Equity
courses.description: This course is designed to provide advanced exposure to current research and practice in leading for equity and inclusion within professional educational settings and a strong conceptual foundation in leadership.

courses.title: Survey and Questionnaire Design
courses.description: Students gain practical experience in the collection and analysis of social science information through the design of surveys and questionnaires.

courses.title: Leaders Serving Special Populations
courses.description: School and district leaders' preparation for future work serving students and families of students receiving special education services, as well as dually-identified students (English language learners receiving special education services). Eligibility, placement, Individual Education Plans, and meaningful inclusion of student and family voice and choice.

courses.title: Hierarchical Linear Models I
courses.description: Introduction to multilevel modeling and hierarchical data structures, random and fixed effects, intercepts and slopes as outcomes models, estimation, centering, and the use and interpretation of HLM statistical software. Sequence with EDLD 629 .

courses.title: Hierarchical Linear Models II
courses.description: This course will include advanced topics in multilevel modeling and hierarchical data structures, including longitudinal and categorical data analysis, estimation methods, missing data and multiple imputation, and the use and interpretation of different HLM statistical software packages, including HLM, R, and Mplus. Sequence with EDLD 628 .

courses.title: Structural Equation Modeling I
courses.description: Theory, application, and interpretation of structural equation modeling techniques. Includes covariance structures, path diagrams, path analysis, model identification, estimation, and testing. Sequence with EDLD 634 .

courses.title: Structural Equation Modeling II
courses.description: Emphasis on structural and latent variable models, including cross-validation, mean structures, comparing groups and models, latent growth-curve analyses. Sequence with EDLD 633 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Executive Leadership for Principals
courses.description: Investigation of leadership theory and styles as they relate to equitable, ethical and instructionally inclusive criteria. Creating vision and execution for strategic systems and structures that lead to a positive culture infused with high expectations and accountability.

courses.title: School Ethics and Governance
courses.description: Examination of how intentional school and district governance serves system transformation. Viewing ethics through an equity lens, leaders develop policies and practices that eliminate systems of advantage and disadvantage thereby closing the opportunity and achievement gaps present in their schools and system.

courses.title: Advanced Public School Law
courses.description: Overview of legal issues in school board–superintendent relations, media relations, personnel evaluation practices, student and employee rights, collective bargaining, contract management, and official complaints.

courses.title: Educational Data Science Capstone Project
courses.description: The final course of the Educational Data Science specialization, this course is an applied capstone where students tackle an applied data problem.

courses.title: Evidence-Based Decision Making in Schools
courses.description: Introduces basic concepts of evidence-based decision making applied to school settings. Focuses on the application of research-based practices for the improvement of academic and behavioral performance in schools.

courses.title: Learning Organization
courses.description: Four theories of organizational learning are explored: structural frame, human resource, political, and symbolic.

courses.title: Ethical Governance for School District Leaders
courses.description: Intentional school and district governance serves system transformation. Viewing ethics through an equity lens, leaders will develop policies and practices that eliminate systems of advantage and disadvantage thereby closing the opportunity and achievement gaps present in their public schools and system.

courses.title: Advanced Seminar Educational Research Methods
courses.description: Examines special issues in the use and application of educational statistics and research design in a discussion-seminar format.

courses.title: Introductory Educational Data Science
courses.description: Introduces students to the fundamentals of statistical computing for data science. Introductory programming, data wrangling, data visualization, reproducible research.

courses.title: Data Visualization for Educational Data Science
courses.description: Best practices in data visualization for social data science communication. Visual perception, color, uncertainty, and communication mediums.

courses.title: Functional Programming for Educational Data Science
courses.description: Foundations of functional programming for data science. Function writing and iteration emphasized.

courses.title: Machine Learning for Educational Data Science
courses.description: Statistical models for prediction. Bias-variance tradeoff, cross-validation methods, model evaluation, and a variety of models used in data science.

courses.title: Item Response Theory I
courses.description: Theory and application of item response measurement models. Participation outcomes include knowledge of IRT models, terminology, and resources. Emphasis on popular models and underlying assumptions.

courses.title: Oregon School and District Finance
courses.description: Overview of school finance concepts, Oregon's school financing system, political and legal considerations, taxation, state distribution formulas, school finance reform, the federal role in education.

courses.title: PhD Research Seminar
courses.description: The primary purpose of this course is to induct doctoral students into the practice of educational research and provide them a base for a career as a faculty member at an institution of higher education or a member of a research institute.

courses.title: Oregon Legal Issues in Public Education
courses.description: Gain knowledge of the legal foundations and understanding of the legal responsibilities of Oregon Administrators in relationship to students and families served, their access to educational opportunities and to educational staff who support students, families, and programs.

courses.title: Supervisory Practices for School Administrators
courses.description: School building and district professionals examine National Educational Leadership Preparation Program (NELP) and Oregon standards. Students explore frameworks for principal support, four dimensions of school leadership, and principal supervisors with the goal of improving student learning and instruction, with emphasis on historically marginalized students.

courses.title: Research Writing
courses.description: Provides structure and guidance to complete the methods section of a dissertation proposal. The nature and scope of the dissertation methods will be determined by a range of factors, including the advisor’s guidance, the data to be used, and the scope of the study.

courses.title: Dissertation Preparation
courses.description: This course is designed to support students in preparing their a dissertation using the APA guidelines for publications in education/social sciences, the standards for measurement systems used in dissertations, and the standard four chapter format: introduction, methods, results, conclusions. Sequence with EDLD 699 .

courses.title: Professional Writing I: Foundations in Professional Writing
courses.description: Covers foundational knowledge of the American Psychological Association’s (APA) writing style as well as how to synthesize research for academic purposes.

courses.title: Professional Writing II
courses.description: Students co-author and submit an article to a peer-reviewed journal or to ERIC. In addition, students will fill out the forms to apply for IRB Exempt research and IRB Review.

courses.title: Professional Writing III: Literature Review
courses.description: Students write a review of research manuscripts on a professional topic and continue to learn the nuances of APA writing style. Sequence with EDLD 696 .

courses.title: Dissertation Methods Apprenticeship
courses.description: Provides doctoral students in the DEd program a structured opportunity to complete their dissertation proposal and a PowerPoint presentation on their respective proposals.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Education and Social Change
courses.description: Students will evaluate historical, philosophical, and social viewpoints related to the purposes and goals of American education. Additionally, students will compare/contrast multiple approaches to address a range of challenges and opportunities in American schools.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A recent topic is Exploring Educational Studies.

courses.title: Childhood Studies
courses.description: Examines child development from within the context of specific development and ecological theories.

courses.title: School and Representation in Media
courses.description: Examines popular culture’s influence on schools and teachers along with the various mediating factors such as race, ethnicity, social class, gender, sexuality, religion, and nationality.

courses.title: Autobiography of Schooling
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to engage students in a critical reflection on life in classrooms through recognizing and questioning the assumptions underlying the routine of school. Includes analysis of critical autobiographies, case studies, readings along with application activities. Sequence with EDST 332 , EDST 333 .

courses.title: Learning, Teaching, and Assessment I
courses.description: Students move beyond their own critical autobiographies of life in classrooms into various disciplinary literatures on learning, teaching, and assessment.

courses.title: Learning, Teaching, and Assessment II
courses.description: Focus on specific school subjects that provide a context for examining the basic assumptions underlying teaching, learning, and assessment.

courses.title: Observation: Learning, Teaching, Assessment I
courses.description: Students focus on listening to children to better understand how they make sense of school subjects.

courses.title: Observation: Learning, Teaching, Assessment II
courses.description: Focuses on developing skills in observation of learning, teaching, and assessments.

courses.title: Curriculum Studies I
courses.description: This course will examine the development of curriculum theory through analysis of a collection of pivotal readings authored by influential scholars from the 18th and 19th centuries paired with contemporary writings that link historical developments with current policy and practice.Sequence with EDST 333 .

courses.title: Curriculum Studies II
courses.description: Examines basic assumptions underlying curriculum development in K-12 schools.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Exploring Educational Studies, IDEA Reading.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Supervised College Teaching
courses.description: A student, under faculty supervision and sponsorship, accepts responsibility for teaching a university course.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Educational Foundations.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Professional Practices, Education for Minority Students, Reading in the Upper Elementary Grades.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: Topics include Integrated Licensure I, II, III.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics include Foundations of Education, Science and Health Methods, Social Studies and Language Arts Methods.

courses.title: Living in a Stratified Society
courses.description: Examines the role of public education in the social context of the United States. The purpose of the course is to develop critical ways of thinking about schools as institutions that both reproduce and challenge social inequality.

courses.title: Technology Education
courses.description: Examines educational technology, including the theoretical, methodological, practical, and policy issues that influence the field.

courses.title: Physical Education for Diverse Learners
courses.description: Provides a variety of physical education and fitness activities appropriate for children with diverse abilities.

courses.title: Integrating the Arts
courses.description: This course introduces students to the foundational conceptions of art, learning, curriculum, culture and society that inform the integration of the arts across subjects in elementary classrooms.

courses.title: Introduction to Adaptive Physical Education
courses.description: This course is designed to introduce students to the knowledge, skills, and strategies necessary to teach adaptive physical education.

courses.title: Food and Schools
courses.description: This course focuses on a topic that has typically been omitted from education courses and programs: food. Through this course, we will be thinking about the ways that food intersects with schools, and the implications of this for formal and informal educators.

courses.title: Teaching for Climate Activism
courses.description: Future teachers survey current research and synthesize best practices for teaching about climate change. The course will draw from research across the fields of climate change education and communication as well as current curricula, books, and materials to explore how best to teach climate change.

courses.title: Poverty and Education
courses.description: Examines the way poverty structures and mediates educational experiences and influences the educational achievement of students.

courses.title: Racism and Education
courses.description: Examines multiple ways institutionalized racism structures and mediates educational experience and multiple visions of anti-racist teaching.

courses.title: Patriarchy and Education
courses.description: Examines the way gender affects educational experiences and influences the educational achievement of students.

courses.title: Heteronormativity and Education
courses.description: Examines the way sexuality and sexual identity influence the educational experiences of students.

courses.title: Decolonization and Education
courses.description: Examines educational institutions and their continuing part in larger social processes of colonization and cultural genocide.

courses.title: Immigration, Diaspora and Education
courses.description: Examines the way educational institutions have responded to human migration generally and to immigrant students specifically.

courses.title: Foundations of Literacy
courses.description: Introduction to the various theoretical dimensions of reading and writing that form the foundation for understanding and teaching the complex texts children encounter today.

courses.title: Multicultural Literacy
courses.description: Introduces preservice teachers to literature written from ethnic, linguistic, social, and cultural perspectives and draws connections to broader cultural, social, historical, economic, and political contexts.

courses.title: Foundations of Algebra Learning
courses.description: Focuses on the principles underlying the teaching and learning of algebra. Sequence with EDST 472 .

courses.title: Foundations of Geometry Learning
courses.description: Focuses on the principles underlying the teaching and learning of geometry. Sequence with EDST 471 .

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Professional Practices, Education for Minority Students, Reading in the Upper Elementary Grades.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]

courses.description: his course introduces students to the foundational conceptions of art, learning, curriculum, culture and society that inform the integration of the arts across subjects in elementary classrooms.

courses.title: Introduction to Adaptive Physical Education
courses.description: This course is designed to introduce students to the knowledge, skills, and strategies necessary to teach adaptive physical education.

courses.title: Food and Schools
courses.description: This course focuses on a topic that has typically been omitted from education courses and programs: food. Through this course, we will be thinking about the ways that food intersects with schools, and the implications of this for formal and informal educators.

courses.description: Future teachers survey current research and synthesize best practices for teaching about climate change. The course will draw from research across the fields of climate change education and communication as well as current curricula, books, and materials to explore how best to teach climate change.

courses.title: Poverty and Education
courses.description: Examines the way poverty structures and mediates educational experiences and influences the educational achievement of students.

courses.title: Racism and Education
courses.description: Examines multiple ways institutionalized racism structures and mediates educational experience and multiple visions of anti-racist teaching.

courses.title: Heteronormativity and Education
courses.description: Examines the way sexuality and sexual identity influence the educational experiences of students.

courses.title: Decolonization and Education
courses.description: Examines educational institutions and their continuing part in larger social processes of colonization and cultural genocide.

courses.title: Immigration, Diaspora and Education
courses.description: Examines the way educational institutions have responded to human migration generally and to immigrant students specifically.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Dissertation
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Foundations of Teaching and Learning
courses.description: Provides students with the psychological foundations of teaching and learning.

courses.title: Cultural Context of Education
courses.description: Examines the cultural foundations of educational practice through a critical review of four decades of ethnographic research on school and student culture.

courses.title: Language, Power, and Education
courses.description: Examines the politics, policies, and practical realities associated with language and literacy in educational settings and how these issues affect all students to some degree.

courses.title: Sapsik'walá Indigenous Education Seminar I
courses.description: Students in the Sapsik'ʷałá (Teacher) Education Program study Indigenous teaching methods, including decolonizing, Indigenous feminist, and Elder pedagogies, and reflect on and analyze the importance of their own work as Indigenous educators who are contributing to Indigenous self-determination in education and Tribal Nation building.

courses.title: Methods ESOL: Teaching English Language Development, K–12
courses.description: Examines best practices of delivering English-language development in light of federal and state standards, including teaching methods, technology, and parental involvement.

courses.title: Curricular Controversies: Math and Literacy
courses.description: Focuses on the debates that influence, and in some cases overshadow, the teaching of mathematics and literacy from kindergarten to grade 12. Sequence with EDST 621 , EDST 640 .

courses.title: Methods: Representing Mathematical Concepts
courses.description: Students deepen their content knowledge, widen their understanding of student conceptualizations of mathematics, and reflect on their own mathematics instructional practices. Sequence with EDST 620 , EDST 622 .

courses.title: Methods: Mathematical Problem-Solving Curriculum
courses.description: Prepares students to view mathematics as a problem-solving field rather than a set of discrete skills and operational rules. Sequence with EDST 620 , EDST 621 .

courses.title: Methods: Representing Science Concepts
courses.description: Examines why science is taught, what science subjects need to be taught, and how science is learned. Sequence with EDST 620 , EDST 624 .

courses.title: Methods: Scientific Problem-Solving Curriculum
courses.description: Presents science as a problem-solving field rather than a set of discrete facts and concepts. Introduces scientific literacy as the aim of science teaching. Sequence with EDST 620 , EDST 623 .

courses.title: Mgmt: Introduction to Supportive Learning Communities
courses.description: Introduces the teacher candidate to the necessary components for creating supportive and successful classroom communities, including interaction between motivation, “classroom management,” and teacher-student relationships. Sequence with EDST 628 .

courses.title: Mgmt: Creating Supportive Classroom Communities
courses.description: Builds on EDST 627 by providing specific research, experience, and strategies for developing classroom environments where student behaviors are focused on learning. Sequence with EDST 627 .

courses.title: Curriculum Studies and the Profession of Teaching
courses.description: Examines a variety of ways curriculum is conceptualized at the secondary level.Sequence with EDST 631 , EDST 632 (or EDST 634 or EDST 635 , EDST 636 ); EDST 638 .

courses.title: Methods ELA: Representing Literature to Young People
courses.description: Examines why literature is taught and the way teachers represent literary works to students. Sequence with EDST 630 , EDST 632 , EDST 638 .

courses.title: Methods: Engaging Students in Writing
courses.description: Overview of strategies and tools for engaging students in the writing process. Emphasis on genres of writing and use of technology to enhance student writing. Sequence with EDST 630 , EDST 631 , EDST 638 .

courses.title: Methods: Representing Second-Language Concepts
courses.description: Provides a research-based foundation for planning, teaching, assessing, and managing second-language learning for the great diversity of students encountered in middle and high school. Sequence with EDST 630 , 634 , 637, 638.

courses.title: Methods: Second-Language Conversation and Composition
courses.description: Advanced teaching methodologies, techniques, and skills to effectively promote proficiency and fluency in second languages. Sequence with EDST 630 , EDST 638 .

courses.title: Methods: Representing Social Studies Concepts
courses.description: Examines why social studies is taught and the way teachers represent social studies concepts to students. Sequence with EDST 630 , EDST 636 , EDST 638 .

courses.title: Methods: Social Studies Inquiry and Analysis
courses.description: Explores the theory and practice of teaching social studies as a specialized form of inquiry. Sequence with EDST 630 , EDST 635 , EDST 638 .

courses.title: Methods ESOL: English Language Learners Pedagogy Humanities
courses.description: Examines a variety of research-based instructional and assessment strategies that support English language learners in meeting the curricular mandates of mainstream language arts and social studies courses. Sequence with EDST 630 ; EDST 631 , EDST 632 (or EDST 634 or EDST 635 , EDST 636 ).

courses.title: Methods: Constructing Meaning through Literacy
courses.description: Provides concepts and strategies used in teaching children to read. Focuses in particular on instruction for beginning and intermediate readers and writers.

courses.title: Methods: Humanities Pedagogy
courses.description: Explores the application of language arts and social studies methods and strategies for future elementary school practitioners.

courses.title: Methods: Teaching Mathematics-Facts and Inquiry
courses.description: Focuses on four areas of instruction crucial to becoming a skillful beginning teacher of mathematics.

courses.title: Methods: Teaching Science-Detail and Discovery
courses.description: Emphasizes science as a process of contemplating, exploring, and raising questions about the world in elementary classrooms.

courses.title: Methods ESOL: Eng Lang Learners Pedagogy Elementary Classrm
courses.description: Examines a variety of research-based instructional and assessment strategies that support English language learners in meeting the mandates of elementary-level curriculum.

courses.title: English Language Learner Methods
courses.description: Application-based course to provide English for Speakers of Other Languages (ESOL) endorsement candidates with opportunities to apply research-based instructional and assessment strategies that support English language learners. Method course has strong connection to planning for and assessing emerging bilingual English learner strengths and needs.

courses.title: Elementary Physical Education Methods
courses.description: This course is designed to prepare future educators with the knowledge, skills, and strategies necessary to teach physical education effectively in elementary schools.

courses.title: Secondary Physical Education Methods
courses.description: This course is designed to prepare future educators with the knowledge, skills, and strategies necessary to teach physical education effectively in secondary schools.

courses.title: Learning and Motivational Sciences
courses.description: Survey of the learning and motivational sciences for advanced graduate students. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Sociology: From Reproduction to Resistance
courses.description: Focuses on the ways schools reproduce, reinforce, and challenge prevailing social, economic, and political relationships. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Curriculum Theory: Contesting Educational Content
courses.description: Survey of the history of curriculum theory, the subfield that asks the fundamental question, what is worth teaching? Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Education and Immigration
courses.description: Examines the way educational institutions have responded to human migration generally and to immigrant students, with an emphasis on bilingual education policy. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Thesis Writing
courses.description: Seminar for doctoral students who have advanced to candidacy. Emphasis is on support through the dissertation proposal writing process.

courses.title: Grant Writing: Finding Funders
courses.description: Provides graduate students with the knowledge and skills needed to write successful grant proposals for research, professional development, and curriculum development projects.

courses.title: Advanced Qualitative Methodology: Arts-Based Approaches
courses.description: Examines contemporary reflexive social science research writing, focusing on experimentations with the form used by researchers to communicate insights about human affairs.

courses.title: Indigenous Methods
courses.description: This seminar examines foundational questions and literatures that guide Indigenous methodologies, the purpose of which is to foster students' abilities and commitments to enact inquiries grounded in relationality, respect, and responsibility toward Indigenous peoples and Indigenous struggles for self-determination, sovereignty, and decolonization.

courses.title: Elementary Part-Time Teaching
courses.description: Provides scope, structure, supervision oversight, and guidance to support students during the part-time student teaching practicum in an Elementary school setting. Is first of two terms of clinical placement that together satisfy the Oregon Teacher Standards and Practices Commission intensive and extensive field-based requirements.

courses.title: Elementary Teaching Performance Assessment
courses.description: Provides scope, structure, supervision oversight, and guidance to support pre-service teachers during their fulltime student teaching ( EDST 683 ) and together are the last of two terms of clinical placement that together satisfy the Oregon Teacher Standards and Practices Commission (TSPC) intensive and extensive field-based requirement.

courses.title: Elementary Full-Time Student Teaching
courses.description: The Elementary fulltime student teaching is designed to give candidates the opportunity to gradually take the lead in preparation, instruction, and assessment for the classroom. This experience helps develop knowledge, skills, and professional dispositions to make a positive impact on student learning.

courses.title: Secondary Part-Time Teaching
courses.description: Provides scope, structure, supervision oversight, and guidance to support students in part-time pre-service student teaching practicum in an Mid-High School setting. The first of two terms of clinical placement that together satisfy the Oregon Teacher Standards and Practices Commission intensive and extensive field-based experience.

courses.title: Secondary Teaching Performance Assessment
courses.description: Provides scope, structure, supervision oversight, and guidance to support students during the full-time student teaching practicum in Mid/High School settings. Is the second of two terms of clinical placement that together satisfy the Oregon Teacher Standards and Practices Commission intensive and extensive field-based requirements.

courses.title: Secondary Full-Time Student Teaching
courses.description: The Secondary full-time student teaching is designed to give candidate the opportunity to gradually take the lead in preparation, instruction, and assessment for the classroom. This experience helps develop knowledge, skills, and professional dispositions to make a positive impact on student learning.

courses.title: Writing for Education Policy Leaders
courses.description: This course is designed to familiarize graduate students with the essential elements of effective, academically rigorous, and policy-relevant professional writing. Students will be challenged to demonstrate critical thinking by anticipating the needs of various education policy actors and understanding the perspectives of key stakeholders—including teachers, school and district leaders, policymakers, the public, and education policy researchers—all of whom play a role in shaping education policy.

courses.title: Education Policy for Multilingual Students
courses.description: This course critically interrogates historical and current approaches to meeting the needs of multilingual and immigrant origin students in the US. The focus is how schools structure education for these students, and they ways in which this enables or constrains educational equity and opportunity. We examine federal, state, and local policies related to core content access, labeling and classification, assessment, and language development, among others.

courses.title: Educational Policy Analysis
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to introduce graduate students to the craft of education policy analysis. In the course, students will learn frameworks and employ tools to conduct education policy analysis, as well as conduct an analysis of an education policy issue of their choosing. Further, students will develop policy analysis writing skills for key education policy stakeholders.

courses.title: Educational Governance and Policy Process
courses.description: This graduate level course introduces students to the education governance structures and policy- and law-making processes at the federal, state and local levels. The course is designed for students who seek to become more sophisticated in their ability to read critically about, understand, and interpret the roles of various educational bodies and actors as well as the policy process and policy environment.

courses.title: Survey of United States Education Policy
courses.description: This is a graduate-level class designed to facilitate students’ understanding of the major policy areas and debates in contemporary U.S. public K-12 education systems. It is designed for graduate students who seek to become education policymakers, school and system leaders, policy analysts and researchers.

courses.title: Foundations of Education Policy and Leadership
courses.description: Required course for the Master of Science in Education Policy and Leadership (MS-EPL) to introduce students into the program, build community within the cohort, develop a shared sense of purpose and direction in the program, and learn foundational core concepts of education policy and leadership.

courses.title: Educational Policy and Leadership Master's Capstone
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to scaffold students through the development and completion of their Educational Policy and Leadership master's capstone project. The capstone project is a discrete empirical examination of a problem of practice. The course also covers career planning and presentation skills.

courses.title: Higher Education Policy
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the realm of public policy in higher education. It delves into relevant research, theoretical frameworks, and areas of debate within the field.

courses.title: Education Law
courses.description: Designed to increase students’ understanding of the legal framework that governs schools and educational policy, focused primarily on US K-12 issues, though we will touch on some higher education issues as well. Examines the basics of the US legal system; the legal rights of students to a high quality education; educational equity (including desegregation and affirmative action); students’ and faculty’s rights of free expression; student discipline and school choice.

courses.title: Higher Education Leadership
courses.description: Analyze the structures and systems-level composition of higher education in the United States; deconstruct the concentric systems of higher education administration and the interrelated leadership roles within those systems; and, examine and classify the subdivisions of possible career paths in higher education administration.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Exploring Careers in Education.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Peer Advising Experience.

courses.description: Recent topics include Advanced Professional Practices.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Survey of Educational Research Methods
courses.description: Survey of qualitative, quantitative, and single-subject research methods. Students develop competence in using published research to inform decision-making in various settings.

courses.title: Social Science and Education Research Design
courses.description: Overview of qualitative, quantitative, and single-subject research methods. Emphasis on introducing students to considerations, issues, and techniques of social science research design.

courses.title: Introductory Statistics for Practitioners I
courses.description: Introduction to statistics for practitioners and research consumers. Includes descriptive statistics of samples and inferential statistics of populations, including chi-square tests, correlation and simple regression, t-tests, and one-way ANOVA. Sequence with EDUC 640 .

courses.title: Qualitative Data and Coding Methods
courses.description: Introduces basic methods of qualitative research, including data collection and thematic coding of interview and observation data.

courses.title: Philosophical Foundations of Social Science
courses.description: This course examines the philosophical assumptions that underlie various research methodologies in the human and social sciences. It additionally introduces beginning doctoral students to the work of a variety of professors in the College of Education.

courses.title: Program Evaluation I
courses.description: Focuses on small-scale evaluations, particularly in the field of education and human services. Students plan and design an evaluation.

courses.title: Program Evaluation II
courses.description: Implementation and completion of the evaluation design defined in Program Evaluation I.

courses.title: Qualitative Methodology I: Interpretivist Inquiry
courses.description: Examines the history of qualitative research in the study of human experience, emphasizing interpretive approaches to qualitative research that retain the regulative ideal of objectivity.

courses.title: Qualitative Methodology II: Postcritical Inquiry
courses.description: Explores the epistemic limits of representing human experience, and the political and ethical implications for researchers beginning with Marx.

courses.title: Qualitative Methodology III: Posthumanist Inquiry
courses.description: Examines theoretical influences on qualitative research beginning with those associated with the linguistic turn, then critiquing the linguistic turn, and ending with the ontological turn.

courses.title: Advanced Qualitative Methodology: New Materialisms
courses.description: Examines contemporary theoretical explorations prompted by “the new materialisms” and how questions of ontology and materiality produce considerations of agency, data, subjectivity, voice, and analysis.

courses.title: Introductory Statistics for Practitioners II
courses.description: Intermediate statistics for practitioners and research consumers. Includes multiple regression with continuous and categorical predictors and factorial ANOVA. Sequence with EDUC 614 .

courses.title: Applied Statistics in Education and Human Services I
courses.description: First course in a PhD and advanced master’s degree three-course sequence intended to provide a toolkit of statistical concepts, methods and their implementation to producers of applied research in education and other social sciences. Course is organized around principles of research design. Sequence with EDUC 614 , EDUC 640 , EDUC 643 .

courses.title: Applied Statistics in Education and Human Service II
courses.description: Second course in a PhD and advanced master’s degree level three-course sequence intended to provide a toolkit of statistical concepts, methods and their implementation to producers of applied research in education and other social sciences. Course is organized around the principle that research design. Sequence with EDUC 614 , EDUC 640 , EDUC 641 .

courses.title: Applied Statistics in Education and Human Services III
courses.description: Third course in a PhD and advanced master's degree level, three-course sequence to provide a toolkit of statistical concepts, methods, and their implementation to producers of applied research in education, human services, and other social sciences. Sequence with EDUC 641 , EDUC 643 .

courses.title: Advanced Research Design
courses.description: Provides a deeper understanding of educational research with an emphasis on principles of research designs and their use in applied research. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Single-Subject Research Methods I
courses.description: Basic single-subject design strategies and general procedures as well as issues related to conducting and analyzing single-subject research in applied settings. Sequence with EDUC 652 .

courses.title: Single-Subject Research Methods II
courses.description: Critical evaluation of single-subject and group-analysis research designs; elaboration on critical topics in single-subject methodology. Sequence with EDUC 650 .

courses.title: Advanced Applied Behavior Analysis
courses.description: Doctoral-level seminar designed to provide skills, practice, and knowledge in advanced methods and theory of applied behavior analysis.

courses.title: Advanced Analysis of Single-Case Research
courses.description: Focuses on application of statistical and meta-analytic strategies for analyzing single-case research. Sequence with EDUC 650 , EDUC 652 , EDUC 654 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Life-Changing Books
courses.description: Aims to improve students' ability to become more attentive and thoughtful readers by emphasizing the cognitive benefits of reading literature and by introducing them to books that reward careful reading -- and have the possibility to be life changing.

courses.title: Introduction to Fiction
courses.description: The study of fiction invites us to enter imaginative narratives and confront the challenges of being human. English 104Z provides opportunities for the appreciation of fiction, including deeper awareness of craft and insight into how reading fiction can lead to self-enrichment. Students read a variety of types of fiction, from diverse perspectives and eras, and develop their skills in discussion, literary analysis, and critical thinking.

courses.title: Introduction to Drama
courses.description: The study of plays exposes us to texts with the power to shock, inspire, enlighten, and delight; this course in drama can be an empowering and transformative journey toward keener engagement with the world, local community, and your intended path. English 105Z provides opportunities for the appreciation of drama, including deeper awareness of craft and insight into how reading plays can lead to self-enrichment. Students read a variety of types of drama, from diverse perspectives and eras, and develop their skills in discussion, literary analysis, and critical thinking.

courses.title: Introduction to Poetry
courses.description: The study of poetry invites us to delve into the biggest questions about life and culture alongside the seemingly smallest issues of words and sounds. English 106Z provides opportunities for the appreciation of poetry, including deeper awareness of craft and insight into how reading poetry can lead to self-enrichment. Students read a variety of types of poetry and poetic forms, from diverse perspectives and eras, and develop their skills in discussion, literary analysis, and critical thinking.

courses.title: World Literature I
courses.description: Reading and analysis of selected works in a global survey of ancient literatures, 2500 BCE–1500 CE.

courses.title: World Literature II
courses.description: Reading and analysis of selected works in a global survey of literature from the Early Modern period to the Industrial Revolution, 1500 CE–1789 CE.

courses.title: Introduction to Film and Media
courses.description: Introduction to film and media studies and various methods of critical analysis. Multilisted with CINE 110M .

courses.title: Introduction to Young Adult Literature
courses.description: Focuses on young adult (YA) literature. The course examines how authors develop common themes or use particular genres to depict the lives of younger characters.

courses.title: Introduction to Science-Fiction and Fantasy Literature
courses.description: Analyzes classic and contemporary science fiction and/or fantasy literature and media and considers the origins and nature of the genres and their conventions.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Public Speaking as a Liberal Art
courses.description: Study and practice of public speaking as grounded in the five rhetorical canons of invention, arrangement, style, delivery, and memory.

courses.title: Genre: [Topic]
courses.description: Traces the historical development and transformations of key genres for the study of English and American literature and culture, including canonical and popular literary as well as media forms.

courses.title: Early Shakespeare
courses.description: The major plays in chronological order with emphasis on the early and middle plays through "Hamlet."

courses.title: Late Shakespeare
courses.description: The major plays in chronological order with emphasis on the later plays beginning with "Twelfth Night."

courses.title: The Craft of the Sentence
courses.description: Study of basic sentence mechanics, grammatical terminology, and the conventions of punctuation. Includes some historical background on the development of English grammar. Students cannot receive credit for both ENG 209 and LING 494 .

courses.title: Age of King Arthur
courses.description: Introduction to the literature of the Middle Ages set against the backdrop of medieval culture.

courses.title: Introduction to Environmental Literature
courses.description: Introduction to literature that examines the human place in the natural world. Consideration of how writers understand environmental crises and scientific ideas of their generation.

courses.title: Introduction to Disability Studies
courses.description: Introduces students to central concepts and essential texts in disability studies and applies them to literary and cultural texts, with a focus on racial diversity and learning directly from writers and scholars who experience a wide spectrum of bodymind variabilities.

courses.title: Introduction to African American Literature
courses.description: African American literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts.

courses.title: Introduction to Asian American Literature
courses.description: Asian American literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts.

courses.title: Introduction to Chicano and Latino Literature
courses.description: Chicano and Latino literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts.

courses.title: Introduction to Native American Literature
courses.description: Native American literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts.

courses.title: Literature and Digital Culture
courses.description: This course will focus on the intersection of digital culture and literary studies. Students will learn how to use digital tools to study literature. Simultnaeously, they will use literary analysis approaches to study contemporary digital culture.

courses.title: Media Aesthetics
courses.description: Introduction to the analysis of form and style in cinema and related media, focusing on narrative, mise-en-scène, cinematography, editing, and sound. Multilisted with CINE 260M .

courses.title: Introduction to Comic Studies
courses.description: Introduction to the art of comics and the methodologies of comics studies.

courses.title: Foundations of the English Major: Text
courses.description: Chronological study of literary and media works in English, beginnings to the present, emphasizing analytic reading and writing skills. Offered in fall terms.

courses.title: English Major Foundations: Context
courses.description: Chronological study of literary and media works in English, from beginnings to the present, emphasizing their cultural and historical contexts. Offered every other winter term, alternating with ENG 305 .

courses.title: English Major Foundations: Theory
courses.description: Chronological study of literary and media works in English, beginnings to the present, emphasizing analytic reading and writing skills. Offered every other winter term, alternating with ENG 304 .

courses.title: Introduction to Literary Criticism
courses.description: Various techniques and approaches to literary criticism (e.g., historical, feminist, formalist, deconstructionist, Freudian, Marxist, semiotic) and their applications.

courses.title: Teen and Children's Literature
courses.description: Books for young readers, their social implications and historical context, from the 19th century to the present.

courses.title: Women Writers' Cultures: [Topic]
courses.description: Women's writing in a particular cultural matrix (race, ethnicity, class, sexual orientation, region, religion) examined in the context of feminist literary theories.

courses.title: Women Writers' Forms: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines women's writing in a particular genre or form (prose, fiction, drama, poetry, autobiography, folksong) in the context of current feminist literary theories.

courses.title: English Novel
courses.description: Covers the early development of the English novel from the 18th to the early 19th century.

courses.title: English Novel
courses.description: Rise of the novel from Scott to Hardy.

courses.title: English Novel Conrad to Present
courses.description: The novel in British Literature from Conrad to the present.

courses.title: Literature of the Northwest
courses.description: Survey of significant Pacific Northwest literature as set against the principles of literary regionalism. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Oral Controversy and Advocacy
courses.description: In-depth study of the habits of research, reasoning, selection, and presentation necessary for ethical and effective oral advocacy on contested topics. Not open to freshmen.

courses.title: Inventing Arguments
courses.description: Analysis and use of patterns of reasoning derived from the disciplines of rhetoric, informal logic, cognitive science, and the theory of argumentation.

courses.title: Jewish Writers
courses.description: Forms and varieties of fiction, poetry, and drama by Jewish writers from the 19th century to the present.

courses.title: African American Writers
courses.description: Examines the origins and development of African American literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts.

courses.title: Native American Writers
courses.description: Examines the origins and development of Native American literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts. Course will be taught once or more per academic year.

courses.title: Chicano and Latino Writers
courses.description: Examines the origins and development of Chicano and Latino literature and culture in relevant intellectual, social, and historical contexts.

courses.title: Global Literatures in English
courses.description: Examination of non-U.S. and non-British authors writing in English in relation to the historical, cultural, and intellectual contexts of their native countries.

courses.title: Film, Media, and History
courses.description: Study of the history of institutions and industries that shape production and reception of film and media.

courses.title: Film, Media, and Culture
courses.description: Study of film and media as aesthetic objects that engage with communities identified by class, gender, race, ethnicity, and sexuality. Multilisted with CINE 381M .

courses.title: Graphic Narratives and Cultural Theory
courses.description: Survey of 20th- and 21st- century graphic novels in the context of cultural theory. Sophomore standing required. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Bodies in Comics
courses.description: Examines questions and representations of bodily identity in comics through the lenses of disability studies and gender theory.

courses.title: American Novel
courses.description: Development of the American novel from its beginnings to 1900.

courses.title: American Novel
courses.description: Development of the American novel from 1900 to present.

courses.title: 20th-Century Literature
courses.description: Modern literature from American, British, and European cultures, 1890 to 1945. Significant works of poetry, fiction, drama, and nonfiction in relation to intellectual and historical developments.

courses.title: 20th-Century Literature
courses.description: Modern literature from American, British, and European cultures, 1945 to present. Significant works of poetry, fiction, drama, and nonfiction in relation to intellectual and historical developments.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: On- or off-campus internship in a variety of writing or literacy-related settings in connection with designated courses.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Selected seminars offered each year.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Selected topics offered each year.

courses.title: Contemporary Literary Theory
courses.description: Developments in critical thinking after the New Criticism.

courses.title: The Art of the Sentence
courses.description: Analysis of English grammar and style in literary and academic contexts. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: The Age of Beowulf
courses.description: A reading of Anglo-Saxon literature and culture as the intersection of Germanic, Celtic, and Christian traditions. Readings include Irish epic, Welsh romance, Norse mythology, and Icelandic saga.

courses.title: Medieval Romance
courses.description: Study of selected romances in the context of European intellectual and social history. May include elementary linguistic introduction to Middle English.

courses.title: Chaucer
courses.description: Close textual study of selected Canterbury Tales in Middle English; instruction in the grammar and pronunciation of Chaucer's language.

courses.title: Old English I
courses.description: Introduction to Old English language. Sequence with ENG 429 , ENG 430 .

courses.title: Old English II: [Topic]
courses.description: Study of Old English prose or poetry in the original language. Sequence with ENG 428 , ENG 430 .

courses.title: Old English III: [Topic]
courses.description: Study of Beowulf or works by other major Old English authors in the original language. Sequence with ENG 428 , ENG 429 .

courses.title: Advanced Shakespeare
courses.description: Detailed study of selected plays, poetry, or both.

courses.title: Restoration and 18th-Century Literature
courses.description: Johnson and his circle; classic to romantic; relations between England and the Enlightenment in France.

courses.title: English Romantic Writers
courses.description: Romantic thought and expression. The second generation including Byron, Keats, Mary and Percy Shelley. Junior standing required.

courses.title: Victorian Literature and Culture: [Topic]
courses.description: Exploration of major works, figures, controversies, social and cultural issues. Readings in Victorian fiction, poetry, drama, and nonfictional prose; study of examples of the visual arts and popular culture.

courses.title: American Literature to 1800
courses.description: Readings in American poetry, nonfiction prose, drama, and fiction.

courses.title: Ethnic Literature: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced study of one or more authors or literary genres related to ethnic literature including African, Native, Asian, or Chicano American.

courses.title: Literature and the Environment: [Topic]
courses.description: In-depth study of various topics related to literature and the environment including Bioart/Bioethics, Biosemiotics, Critical Animal Studies, Food Culture, Ideas of Wilderness, Rhetoric of Nature Writing, Virtual Ecologies.

courses.title: Technologies and Texts Capstone
courses.description: This course examines the way humanities disciplines use digital technologies to forge a new role in the public sphere, exploring how digital and print cultures (re)shape forms of cultural expression and knowledge production. Students will create their own digital projects in this course.

courses.title: Modern Poetry
courses.description: 20th-century British and American poetry with emphasis on the modernist period, 1910–45. Representative authors include Yeats, Stein, Pound, Eliot, H. D., Williams, and Stevens.

courses.title: Major Authors: [Topic]
courses.description: In-depth study of one to three major authors from medieval through modern periods.

courses.title: Modern American Superhero
courses.description: Examination of the path of the American comic book superhero and an exploration of the ways in which that journey reflects large processes of social change.

courses.title: Television Studies
courses.description: Study of television's institutional contents and representational practices, including such television genres as serials, news, and reality TV. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: New Media and Digital Culture: [Topic]
courses.description: Study of emerging media forms and techniques, such as digital cinema, video games, viral videos, and interactive media. Offered alternate years. Multilisted with CINE 486M .

courses.title: Race and Representation in Film: [Topic]
courses.description: Screening, interpretation, and analysis of films from developing non-European cultures and by people of color. Mechanisms of racism in dominant U.S. media.

courses.title: Rhetoric and Ethics
courses.description: Investigation of historical and contemporary theories of ethical rhetoric in both written and oral arguments.

courses.title: History of Rhetoric and Composition
courses.description: History of rhetoric as related to the theory and practice of writing, relations between rhetoric and poetics, and rhetorical criticism through the 19th century.

courses.title: Modern Rhetorical Criticism
courses.description: Theoretical topics addressed by 20th-century rhetorical critics. Varieties of rhetorical interpretation, from neo-Aristotelian to reader-response, postmodernist views of metaphor.

courses.title: Reasoning, Speaking, Writing
courses.description: Application of advanced study in argumentation theory, particularly procedural standards of rationality developed in recent argumentation studies, to selected public policy controversies.

courses.title: Feminist Film Criticism: [Topic]
courses.description: Analysis of US film and television texts from a feminist perspective.

courses.description: Supervised practical experience in all aspects of teaching WR 121Z , WR 122Z .

courses.title: Introduction to Literary and Cultural Theory
courses.description: Introduces students to a number of the most important and influential developments in 20th-century literary and cultural theory. Graduate seminar.

courses.title: Advanced Studies in Literary Theory: [Topic]
courses.description: Intensive study of one to three major theorists or a significant theoretical problem.

courses.title: Medieval Literature: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent offerings include Chaucer's Troilus and Criseyde, Humor and Vulgarity in Medieval Literature.

courses.title: Renaissance Literature: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent offerings include Hamlet, Jacobean Potboilers, Renaissance Irrationalities.

courses.title: 18th-Century Literature: [Topic]
courses.description: Intensive study of one to three major authors or selected topics from the 18th century. Recent offerings include Enlightenment and Revolution.

courses.title: American Literature: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent offerings include African American Women Writers, Evolutionary Theories and Narrative, Sentimental Novel, V. Deloria and Native American Cultural Values.

courses.title: Modern Literature: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent offerings include H. James, Modernist Politics, Environmental Humanities, Postmodernism.

courses.title: Introduction to Graduate Studies in English
courses.description: Examination of selected professional, methodological, and theoretical issues.

courses.title: Composition Theory: [Topic]
courses.description: Intensive study of topics related to rhetorical theory and the teaching of writing.

courses.title: Film Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Intensive study of selected topics related to film studies and literature. Recent topics include Introduction to Film Theory; Feminism, Comedy, and the Carnivalesque; Melodrama.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Environmental Design Careers
courses.description: Developing skills in strategizing career options and mapping to degree plan. Overview of professional communication, resume and cover letter writing, interviewing and networking skills. Previously taught as LA 301,

courses.title: Foundations in Environmental Design
courses.description: Foundations of Environmental Design introduces students to the breadth and different paths of the Environmental Design major. The course will explore Environmental Design as a multidisciplinary topic which focuses on visual and spatial design skills applied to the context of environmental sustainability. Concurrent registration in ENVD 289 required.

courses.title: Environmental Design Studio
courses.description: Study of places, their use, and how they evolve. Fundamentals of environmental awareness, social factors, and small-scale site design; abstract design and elementary graphic techniques. Concurrent registration in ENVD 210 required.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Environmental Design Media
courses.description: Environmental Design Media is the first course in a yearlong foundational environmental media sequence. The course focuses on fundamental concepts of digital and environmental design. Students cannot receive credit for both ENVD 451 and LA 451 . Sequence with LA 452 , LA 453 .

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Laboratory Projects: [Topic]

courses.title: Introduction to Environmental Studies: Social Sciences
courses.description: Contributions of the social sciences to analysis of environmental problems. Topics include human population, the relationship between social institutions and environmental problems, and appropriate political, policy, and economic processes.

courses.title: Introduction to Environmental Studies: Natural Sciences
courses.description: Contributions of the natural sciences to analysis of environmental problems. Topics include biological processes, ecological principles, chemical cycling, ecosystem characteristics, and natural system vulnerability and recovery.

courses.title: Introduction to Environmental Studies: Humanities
courses.description: Contributions of the humanities and arts to understandings of the environment. Emphasis on diverse ways of thinking, writing, creating, and engaging in environmental discourse.

courses.title: Introduction to Food Studies
courses.description: An exploration of the field of "food studies" and examination of the role of food in historical and contemporary life in the US and around the world.

courses.title: Allocating Scarce Environmental Resources
courses.description: Considerations for the design of environmental and natural resources policies and regulations: balancing society's preferences and the costs of environmental protection and resource conservation.

courses.title: Environmental Ethics
courses.description: Key concepts and various moral views surveyed; includes anthropocentrism, individualism, ecocentrism, deep ecology, and ecofeminism. Exploration includes case studies and theory.

courses.title: Ecological Footprint of Energy Generation
courses.description: Detailed study of the ecological consequences of all forms of energy generation including fossil fuels and alternative energy sources. Open to environmental science, environmental studies, and planning, public policy and management majors only.

courses.title: Environmental Issues: [Topic]
courses.description: In depth examination of a particular environmental topic such as global warming, ecosystem restoration, energy alternatives, geothermal development, public lands management, or environmental literature.

courses.title: Environmental Education Theory and Practice
courses.description: Learning theories, environmental literacy, and the planning, implementation, and evaluation of environmental education programs. Development of teaching materials in collaboration with a community partner for group project.

courses.title: Environmental and Ecological Monitoring
courses.description: Theory, design, and practice of monitoring sampling mapping, field techniques, data collection, management, analysis and presentation methods, local case studies.

courses.title: Environmental Leadership: [Topic]
courses.description: Partnering with governmental agencies, nonprofit organizations, public schools and local businesses, students develop service learning projects.

courses.title: Nature in Popular Culture
courses.description: This environmental humanities course examines the various ways that nature is represented in U.S. popular culture. What can advertisements, films, television, and video games teach us about the ways we imagine nature, the environment, and environmentalism?

courses.title: Environmental Justice
courses.description: Environmental justice and its impact on current decisions. Focus on civil rights law, perception of risk, and relation of sustainability and equity.

courses.title: Political Ecology
courses.description: Examines how social relations and economic, social, and cultural control of natural resources shape human interactions with the environment. Theory and case studies.

courses.title: Sustainability
courses.description: Examines the evolution of the concept of sustainability and its complex and sometimes problematic uses among scholars, policymakers, environmentalists, and businesses.

courses.title: Water, Public Health, and the Environment
courses.description: Water, Public Health, and the Environment examines the provision of water and sanitation services around the world with a particular focus on its impacts for public health and the environment in low- and middle-income communities.

courses.title: Wetland Ecology and Management
courses.description: Examines management, law, and policies related to wetlands in an ecological framework; includes wetland type definitions, classification, distribution, formation and development, and restoration.

courses.title: Sustainable Agriculture
courses.description: Examines sustainability issues in agricultural production and current food systems. Focuses on environmental aspects of seed, water, soil, energy, and pest management.

courses.title: Soil Science
courses.description: Chemical and physical characteristics and classification of soils, field soil identification, soil degradation.

courses.title: Passive Cooling
courses.description: Conceptual and quantitative investigations of passive cooling design and performance, including precedents, shading, natural ventilation, evaporative cooling, use of thermal mass, radiant cooling assisted by cold night skies, and control scheduling, supported by field investigations and introductory energy modeling. Multilisted with ARCH 493M .
