courses.title: Environmental Studies Theory and Practice
courses.description: Introduction to various disciplinary perspectives that contribute to environmental studies, including their research methods, vocabularies, and core concepts.

courses.title: Environmental Studies Research Methodology
courses.description: Identifying a clear and concise research problem, developing methodology to address that problem, and the process of developing a thorough knowledge of relevant literature.

courses.title: Environmental Studies Thesis Development
courses.description: Interdisciplinary readings in environmental studies focused on topics chosen by each student in consultation with instructor. Preparation for presentations at the Joint Campus Conference.

courses.title: Exploring Planet Earth
courses.description: Plate tectonics, mantle flow, and magmatism. Volcanoes, earthquakes, mountain building, generation of Earth's crust; rocks and minerals; Earth's internal structure. Comparison with other planets. Laboratory, lecture.

courses.title: Exploring Earth’s Environment
courses.description: Landforms, surface processes, and interactions between humans and the environment. Weathering, erosion, sedimentation, ground water, streams, glaciers, deserts, oceans, and coastlines; geologic hazards. Laboratory, lecture. Roering.

courses.title: Exploring Earth History
courses.description: History of the Earth. Geologic time, sedimentary environments; oceans, mountains, and climate through time; stratigraphic history of North America; evolution of plants and animals; interactions between humans, science, and the environment.

courses.title: Mountains and Glaciers
courses.description: Survey of the geological processes that create mountain ranges around the world, and the glaciers that both adorn and erode them; an introduction to these select topics in geological science.

courses.title: Scientific Revolutions
courses.description: Surveys several major revolutions in our views of the natural and technological world, focusing on scientific concepts and methodological aspects. For nonscience majors. Multilisted with PHYS 156M .

courses.title: Laboratory Projects: [Topic]
courses.description: An experience in a lab setting in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content master.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Studies of geologic topics combine background lectures with guided field trips to areas of geologic interest.

courses.title: Dynamic Planet Earth
courses.description: Processes that cause earthquakes, volcanism, mountain building, and plate tectonics. Includes Earth's origin and internal structure, rocks and minerals, gravity and magnetics. Weekly lectures, two-hour laboratory.

courses.title: Earth's Surface and Environment
courses.description: Earth materials, the rock record, human interactions with surface environment. Sedimentary rocks and environments, chemical and physical weathering, hydrogeology, ground-water contamination, surface processes, human impacts. Weekly lectures, two-hour laboratory.

courses.title: History of Life
courses.description: Origin, history, and physical evolution of the Earth; geologic time scales, development of the global stratigraphic section. Weekly lectures, two-hour laboratory.

courses.title: Geology of National Parks
courses.description: Examines selected geologic features in United States national parks/monuments and the processes that form them within a historical approach to the development of the North American continent. Focuses on parks and monuments throughout the conterminous 48 states.

courses.title: The Fossil Record
courses.description: This course will cover the history of life on Earth through time as found in the fossil record, as well as how scientists go about studying the fossil record. The curriculum also includes the processes of speciation and the disciplines of functional morphology and paleoecology.

courses.title: Dinosaurs
courses.description: This course covers the evolution, occurrence, and variety of animals classified within the Dinosauria and the changing climate and vegetation during their time. Students will discuss dinosaur video depiction in popular culture and will assess the accuracy of those representations.

courses.title: Volcanoes and Earthquakes
courses.description: Mechanisms that cause earthquakes and volcanoes, relation to plate tectonics, associated hazards, examples in Oregon and the western United States.

courses.title: Oceanography
courses.description: Characteristics and physical, chemical, and biological processes of the world's oceans. Includes sections on origin of the oceans, plate tectonics, and human use and misuse of oceans. Students cannot receive credit for both BI 150 and ERTH 307 .

courses.title: Geology of Oregon and the Pacific Northwest
courses.description: The region's geologic and tectonic history and the plate tectonic processes responsible for its evolution.

courses.title: Earth Resources and the Environment
courses.description: Geology of energy, mineral, and water resources and environmental issues related to their use. Topics include fossil fuels, metals, nuclear waste disposal, and water pollution.

courses.title: Earth Materials
courses.description: Chemical and mineralogical composition of rocks, sediment, and soil. Properties of common minerals; origin of rocks; microscopic study of rock textures; environmental issues.

courses.title: Earth Physics
courses.description: Physics of basic Earth processes; application of physics to plate tectonics and lithospheric deformation. Topics include forces, deformation, gravity, and seismology. Taught once or more per academic year.

courses.title: Introduction to Hydrogeology
courses.description: Examines the role of water in geologic and environmental processes. Topics include the water cycle, groundwater flow, and contaminant transport.

courses.title: Introduction to Field Methods
courses.description: Introduction to geologic mapping and related field skills, rock descriptions, cross sections, and structures. Lectures, laboratories, mandatory field trips.

courses.title: Field Studies Oregon Geology
courses.description: Two-week summer course. Introduction to Oregon geology in the field with emphasis on processes and materials that shape the Earth’s surface. Students use field data and maps to investigate volcanic activity, erosion, landslides, tectonic faults, and/or geologic hazards.

courses.title: Mineralogy
courses.description: Crystal chemistry, systematic study of rock-forming silicate, and selected other minerals, mineral optics, and x-ray diffraction. Lab work with hand samples and petrographic microscopes.

courses.title: Introduction to Petrology
courses.description: Origin and classification of igneous, metamorphic, and sedimentary rocks. Microscopic study of rocks in thin section.

courses.title: Sedimentology and Stratigraphy
courses.description: Sedimentary processes; characteristic properties of sedimentary rocks and their use in interpreting depositional environments; principles of lithostratigraphy and sequence stratigraphy.

courses.title: Introduction to Physical Oceanography
courses.description: Introduction to the physical processes that occur in the ocean. These processes control the movement of sediment, pollution, nutrients, and biota, as well as heat and freshwater. Topics might include waves, global ocean circulation, sediment transport, estuarine circulation, and biological oceanography.

courses.title: Structural Geology
courses.description: Description, analysis, and origin of geologic structures including faults, folds, and tectonites. Focus on kinematic and dynamic analysis of deformation of earth materials.

courses.title: Structural Geology Problems
courses.description: Exercises in solving structural geology problems using orthographic and stereographic projection techniques. Problems emphasize calculating stress and strain from structural markers.

courses.title: Structural Geology Laboratory and Field
courses.description: Collection and interpretation of field and map data for structural analysis. Includes field trips, map and cross-section generation, and some computer-based exercises.

courses.title: Geologic Hazards
courses.description: A hands-on study of natural hazards, their physical processes and geography, and their impact on societies worldwide including recent occurrences of natural disasters . This course is accessible and to individuals interested in Geosciences, Geography, and Environmental Science.

courses.title: Computational Tools for Earth Sciences
courses.description: Introduction to computational tools vital to the work of Earth scientists, including data management and analysis, algorithms, basic programming, computational environments, and visualization.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Laboratory Projects: [Topic]
courses.description: An experience in a lab setting in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content master.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Geophysical and Environmental Sensors
courses.description: This experiential course will provide students an introduction to sensors, microcontrollers, automation, data collection and programming from the perspective of sensing the Earth and the environment.

courses.title: Earth and Environmental Data Analysis
courses.description: Tools-based instruction in data analysis for earth and environmental scientists. Topics include descriptive statistics, visualization, uncertainty analysis, hypothesis testing, regression, time series, and directional data.

courses.title: Geocommunication
courses.description: Scientific writing and presentations for the geological sciences. Focus on writing scientific papers and proposals, preparing oral and visual presentations.

courses.title: Introduction to Space Physics
courses.description: Course explores the interaction of the solar wind with the Earth's magnetosphere using fundamental plasma physics supported and motivated by spacecraft observations. Students will gain an understanding of the physics governing the interaction building from single particle plasma motion to specific observation supported examples.

courses.title: Vertebrate Paleontology
courses.description: Evolution of vertebrates, including ourselves, based on fossil evidence. Physical and other evolutionary constraints are addressed, and lab exercises provide practical experience.

courses.title: Paleoecology and Functional Morphology
courses.description: Ecological methods for the study of fossil organisms, both terrestrial and marine. Covers a range of methods from those that reconstruct the ecology of individual species to those that deal with whole communities and ecosystems. Laboratory offers practical and analytical experience in the methods.

courses.title: Hillslope Geomorphology
courses.description: Hillslope processes and landforms; includes hillslope hydrology, overland flow erosion, weathering and soil formation, soil creep, landslides and related hazards, glacial and periglacial processes, effects of land-use practices and fire, and landscape evolution.

courses.title: Hydrogeology
courses.description: Study of the origin, motion, and physical and chemical properties of ground water. Emphasizes quantitative analysis of flow and interaction with geologic materials.

courses.title: Tectonics
courses.description: Tectonic processes and examples. Global kinematics of plates and the forces that drive them. Continental deformation in compressional, shear, and extensional settings.

courses.title: Fluid Dynamics
courses.description: Introduction to the continuum theory of fluid dynamics, focusing on the Navier-Stokes equations of motion including common simplified limits and extensions. Applications are drawn from Earth and Planetary Science, Biology, and Physics.

courses.title: Mechanical Earth
courses.description: Introduction to continuum mechanics. Includes stress and strain, friction, elasticity, viscous fluids, constitutive laws, equations of motion, and deformation of the Earth.

courses.title: Signal Processing
courses.description: A theoretical and hands-on introduction to signal processing techniques that are widely used in geophysical, geological, and related fields.

courses.title: Introduction to Seismology
courses.description: Introduction to observational, theoretical, and computational seismology. Includes review of earth structure, source representation, ray theory, and seismic wave phenomena.

courses.title: Thermodynamic Geochemistry
courses.description: Introduction to geologic application of classical chemical thermodynamics. Gibbs free energy and its temperature, pressure, and composition derivatives; fugacity, activity, and chemical potential. Solutions, ideal and nonideal.

courses.title: Isotope Geochemistry
courses.description: Introduction to nuclear physics and isotope systematics; techniques of isotope analysis; applications of stable and radioactive isotopes in geochronology and as tracers of geological processes.

courses.title: Soil and Environmental Chemistry
courses.description: Understanding the flow and cycling of chemicals in soils is vital for addressing many pressing societal issues, including mitigating climate change, growing abundant and safe food, and protecting water quality. This class will describe fundamental soil chemical principles and consider their broader applications.

courses.title: Volcanology
courses.description: Products and processes of volcanism, transport of magma in the conduit and into the atmosphere, eruptive mechanisms, volcanic hazards.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Advanced Igneous Petrology
courses.description: Ingeous rocks of the ocean basins, continental margins, and stable continental interior including basalts, calcalkaline series, and granites. Content varies according to research interests.

courses.title: Advanced Volcanology
courses.description: This course introduces students to concepts of heat and mass transfer to understand the transport of magma in the subsurface and the physical processes involved in volcanic eruptions.

courses.title: Introduction to Ethnic Studies
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the academic field of Ethnic Studies, the interdisciplinary, comparative and relational study of race, ethnicity and indigeneity in the United States. Special attention paid to how systems of domination and acts of resistance (re)create racial subjects.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Introduction to Black Studies
courses.description: This course introduces students to the theoretical models used in the interdisciplinary study of African-America. Using a thematic approach, students will learn to critically engage the development of and dynamics between race, racism and blackness in the United States.

courses.title: Introduction to Asian American Studies
courses.description: Focuses on historical, cultural, and social issues in Asian America and surveys scholarship in Asian American studies.

courses.title: Introduction to Chicanx and Latinx Studies
courses.description: Focuses on historical, social, and cultural issues in Chicanx and Latinx communities and surveys scholarship in Chicanx and Latinx studies.

courses.title: Introduction to Native American Studies
courses.description: Interdisciplinary approaches to understanding Native American lives, examining Native American identities, practices, histories, creative works, cultures, and political status in context.

courses.title: Introduction to Pacific Islander Studies
courses.description: Focuses on historical, social, and cultural issues in Pacific Islander communities and surveys scholarship in Pacific Island Studies.

courses.title: Theoretical Perspectives in Ethnic Studies
courses.description: Introduction to contemporary theoretical frameworks in the discipline of ethnic studies. Intended to be taken by junior year to introduce students to the second half of the Ethnic Studies major. Required for Ethnic Studies majors. Offered once per year.

courses.title: Race and Popular Culture: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines the interface between race and popular culture, surveying the historical development, political significance, and social influence ofpopular culture in the United States. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Indigenous Peoples of Oregon
courses.description: This course examines the history, culture, social and political issues of the Indigenous peoples of Oregon through oral traditions, primary sources, and secondary sources, focused on the major themes of sovereignty, traditional knowledge, and decolonization.

courses.title: Women of Color Issues and Concerns
courses.description: Contemporary social issues and feminism among women of color in the United States.

courses.title: Native Americans and the Environment
courses.description: Critical issues in Native American environmentalism.

courses.title: Social Equity and Criminal Justice
courses.description: Critical issues related to police, prisons, criminal justice, and racial and gender inequalities.

courses.title: Environmental Racism
courses.description: Explores environmental justice as both a field of scholarship and organizing framework that links power, justice, and inequality to environmental issues. Special attention is given to the specific forms of racism which produce environmental injustice.

courses.title: Black Sexual Politics
courses.description: Explores the gender and sexuality politics that influence the social, political, economic and cultural development of black communities in the diaspora, including the United States.

courses.title: Race, Ethnicity, and Cinema: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines the history and politics of race, ethnicity, and indigeneity in relation to film, including questions of production, distribution, and reception, in the United States. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Race, Migration, and Rights
courses.description: Examines historical and contemporary politics in race, immigration, and migration.

courses.title: Critical Whiteness Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: This course explores the social construction of race by investigating and historicizing “whiteness” as a racial category in the U.S.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Caribbean Migrations; Asian American Women; Critical Whiteness Studies; Native Americans and Film; Asian Diasporas; Race and Resistance in United States History.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Race, Literature, and Culture: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines race, literature, and culture from an interdisciplinary perspective. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Race and Incarceration
courses.description: Introduces several key questions necessary for understanding the crisis of prisons and incarceration in the United States, with an emphasis on race, gender, and class.

courses.title: Race and Ethnicity and the Law: [Topic]
courses.description: Addresses issues of social justice and the participation of Asian Americans, African Americans, Chicanos and Latinos, and Native Americans in the legal system.

courses.title: History of Native American Education
courses.description: Examines the historical conflict between traditional culture and knowledge transmission among Native Americans and the assimilationist educational system and practices of Euro-American culture. Offered alternative years.

courses.title: Race, Culture, Empire: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines how racial discourses have informed United States domestic and foreign policy, with special attention on cultural representations of U.S. colonialism and imperialism. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Relational Studies of Indigeneity, Race and Culture: [Topic]
courses.description: Seminar centers relation between Indigenous peoples and peoples of color instead of focusing on white/non-white line. Called comparative or relational studies, the approach yields unique insights into how race and settler-colonialism work in the United States and beyond.

courses.title: Feminist Theories of Race: [Topic]
courses.description: This course focuses on the political and theoretical trajectory of feminist and race theories associated with particular groups, such as Asian American feminisms, or comparatively in relation to Women of Color and queer politics.

courses.title: Native American Ethnohistory
courses.description: In this seminar, we read, evaluate, analyze, and critique major texts in Native American ethnohistory (a combination of anthropological and historical methods) with a focus on perspectives and concerns of contemporary Indigenous peoples.

courses.title: Indigenous Research Methods and Ethics
courses.description: This course addresses methodological and ethical issues related to conducting research about and with Indigenous peoples. The course introduces students to the principles of Indigenous research, including practices, collaboration, ethics, and Indigenous approaches to the construction of knowledge.

courses.title: Native American and Indigenous Feminisms
courses.description: This class will acquaint students with critical issues in Native American and Indigenous feminisms. We will review past and present struggles, including gendered violence, cultural systems of gender and sexuality, successes and failures in efforts towards alliance, life stories, film, and literature, in comparative perspective.

courses.title: Colloquium: Professional Development
courses.description: Professional development, teaching skills, and mentorship for first year Ethnic Studies PhD students.

courses.title: Genealogies of Ethnic Studies
courses.description: Examines the emergence and evolution of the discipline of Ethnic Studies, including major intellectual shifts in the field, particularly as they relate to changes in the social science and humanities; and the state of the discipline today.

courses.title: Theoretical Foundations in Ethnic Studies
courses.description: Introduction to the theoretical foundations and debates in Ethnic Studies scholarship.

courses.title: Interdisciplinary Research Methods in Ethnic Studies
courses.description: Introduction to interdisciplinary methodologies in the various fields of ethnic studies, with an eye towards developing interdisciplinary competence and students applying it to their own work.

courses.title: Race, Space, and Power: [Topic]
courses.description: This course questions the variety of ways that social constructions of race and space are inextricable from one another and constitute, as much as they are constituted by, modern power relations.

courses.title: Cultural Production: [Topic]
courses.description: Graduate introduction to the theories and methods utilized within Cultural Studies scholarship with attention to race, gender, nation, sexuality and indigeneity.

courses.title: Resistance and Dissent: [Topic]
courses.description: Surveys historical and contemporary methods people of color have used to subvert and challenge white power and privilege in the United States.

courses.title: Race, Gender and Sexuality Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: This course closely examines the ways in which race is deeply intertwined with gender and sexuality in the production of racial, gender, and sexual violence since the inception of European settler colonialism in the Americas.

courses.title: Issues for Children and Families
courses.description: Examines issues and problems confronting children and families in the United States. Issues such as disability, poverty, health care, addictions, racism, and violence are addressed.

courses.title: Exploring Family and Human Services
courses.description: Explores the historic basis and current design of family and human services. Emphasizes services to children, youth, adults, and families.

courses.title: Diversity in Human Services
courses.description: This course is designed to provide the foundational knowledge, awareness, and skills needed for working with diverse populations in the human services. This course will explore issues of equity and inclusion across the levels of the ecological model.

courses.title: Human Development in the Family Context
courses.description: Examines human development within the context of the family from an evidence-based perspective. Integration of contemporary family issues experienced across the lifespan within the context of the human service profession. Previously taught as FHS 328.

courses.title: Writing for Human Services Professionals
courses.description: This course prepares students with the knowledge and skills necessary for writing competently for professional/scientific audiences through a series of assigned readings, in- and out-of-class assignments, and quizzes. Students will practice iterative writing by giving and receiving peer reviews and incorporating peer and instructor feedback.

courses.title: Youth Psychopathology in Context
courses.description: Presents child and adolescent psychopathology and problems within a diagnostic framework. Topics address psychosocial issues for youth in family and cultural contexts. Students cannot receive credit for both BEHT 320 and FHS 329 .

courses.title: Individual Interventions in Ecological Contexts
courses.description: Introduces the concept of individual interventions within an ecological model. Provides knowledge and development of basic listening skills and how to apply these skills to individuals in diverse contexts.

courses.title: Group and Community Interventions
courses.description: Introduces the concept of group intervention within the context of group theory and community development. Provides knowledge and development of group interventions, including group facilitation skills and curriculum development.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research in Human Services
courses.description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to research methods commonly used in human services, prevention science, and social work. Students will learn how to formulate research questions; identify, interpret, and evaluate empirical research articles; and engage in scientific writing by preparing research reports.

courses.title: Human Services Professional Ethics
courses.description: This course focuses on professional ethics relevant to human services practitioners, with an emphasis on building skills required for ethical decision-making, including self-awareness, identification and integration of personal, professional, and legal values and standards, and evaluating scientific literature on prevention and intervention. Students cannot receive credit for both BEHT 412 and FHS 471 .

courses.title: Human Services in Practice
courses.description: Supervised practicum (internship) within a local school or community agency. Co-enrollment in FHS 473 required.

courses.title: Human Services in Practice Supervision
courses.description: Seminar providing group supervision related to practicum (internship) participation within a local school or community agency.

courses.title: Scientific Analysis in Human Services
courses.description: This course instills students with the knowledge and skills necessary to understand and perform basic quantitative and qualitative data analysis and to understand and demonstrate how data analysis is related to research design within the context of prevention science and the human services, broadly.

courses.title: Contemporary Issues in Public Health
courses.description: This course covers methods for assessing and addressing community health problems and promoting health equity.

courses.title: Child and Family Case Management
courses.description: This class provides students skills in case management and human-service-focused direct practice with children, adolescents, and families, including needs assessments, case conceptualization, service plan development, and direct service intervention.

courses.title: Adolescent and Adult Case Management
courses.description: This class provides students skills in case management and human service focused direct practice with adolescents and adults, including needs assessments, case conceptualization, service plan development, and direct service intervention.

courses.title: Survey of Real Estate
courses.description: Basics of buying, selling, and leasing real estate. Overview of real estate law, commercial and residential brokerage, real estate financing, and real estate administration.

courses.title: Personal Finance
courses.description: Overview of lifetime personal financial strategies. Topics include financial goals and building net worth, major purchasing decisions, credit use, tax planning, retirement, and estate planning.

courses.title: The Stock Market and Investing
courses.description: Investments and the stock market, securities and approaches to security selection, portfolio composition and structure.

courses.title: Economic Foundations of Competitive Analysis
courses.description: Analysis of market competition and its relation to product cost and pricing decisions by the firm. Students may receive credit for only one of EC 311 , FIN 311 , or FIN 311H .

courses.title: Economic Foundations of Competitive Analysis
courses.description: Analyzes the competitive structure of markets and industries. Focuses on the relationships among cost, pricing strategy and economic profit in competitive environments. Students may receive credit for only one of EC 311 , FIN 311 , or FIN 311H .

courses.title: Financial Management
courses.description: Corporate financial planning, selection among alternative investment opportunities, analysis of risk, funds acquisition, and long-term financing. Students cannot receive credit for both FIN 316 and FIN 316H .

courses.title: Financial Management
courses.description: Covers the fundamental tools and concepts of finance, including the evaluation of investment opportunities and the relation between risk and return Students cannot receive credit for both FIN 316 and FIN 316H .

courses.title: Financial Markets and Investments
courses.description: Financial markets and security investment decisions, analysis of risk and return, portfolio policies for individual and institutional investors, financial instruments.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Finance
courses.description: Covers the fundamental theories and tools of financial analysis. Topics include valuation, capital budgeting, risk and return, market efficiency, and financial policies. Sequence with FIN 613 .

courses.title: Managerial Economics
courses.description: Covers the fundamental theories and tools of economic and strategic analysis. Topics include demand and supply, pricing strategies, and perfect and imperfect competition. Sequence with FIN 612 .

courses.title: Quantitative Methods for Finance
courses.description: Mathematical and statistical tools fundamental to financial analysis; measuring returns and risk, probability models, statistical inference, regression analysis, all with applications to finance.

courses.title: Data Analysis in Finance
courses.description: Programming for financial data analysis using popular languages; Back testing and developing algorithm trading strategies.

courses.title: Corporate Finance and Valuation
courses.description: Application of financial principles to problems of valuation, capital budgeting, and financial policy.

courses.title: Advanced Topics in Corporate Finance
courses.description: Cases dealing with financial analysis, working-capital management, valuation, and firm investment and financing decisions.

courses.title: Fixed Income Securities
courses.description: Theoretical, empirical, and institutional aspects of fixed-income securities and their derivatives; application of these tools to managerial decisions in other contexts.

courses.title: Concepts of Investments
courses.description: Securities markets; risk-return characteristics of investment media; concepts of security analysis; investment and portfolio strategies of individual and institutional investors.

courses.title: Alternative Investments
courses.description: Covers alternative asset classes such as venture capital, private equity (LBO), commercial real estate, and cryptoassets. Focus on valuation methodologies, fund structures, and investment strategies.

courses.title: Voices of Africa
courses.description: Novels, music, dance, dress, paintings, films, and cartoons serve as a primary sources from which to learn about the diversity and vivacity of contemporary African peoples.

courses.title: Folklore and the Supernatural
courses.description: Introduces the study of beliefs about the supernatural by examining diverse approaches to the description and analysis of belief traditions and religious culture.

courses.title: Magic in the Middle Ages
courses.description: Examines how medieval culture defined magic and how the exploration of magic led to the beginnings of science. Analyzes the practices of medieval western Europe, particularly Britain.

courses.title: Introduction to Folklore
courses.description: The process and genres of traditional (i.e., folk) patterning; the relations between these forms of expression and other arts, especially English and American literature.

courses.title: Folklore and United States Popular Culture
courses.description: Explores the relationship between folklore and popular culture, with special emphasis on the analysis of legends, myths, icons, stereotypes, heroes, celebrities, rituals, and celebrations.

courses.title: Car Cultures
courses.description: Examines car customizing and tuning as forms of vernacular art; studies the environmental impacts of automobiles, the history of the industry, and the peculiarities of drivers' behavior. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Folklore and the Bible
courses.description: Studies readings of the Judeo-Christian Bible in connection with mythological and traditional contexts and meanings from ancient times to the present. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Folklore and Sexuality
courses.description: Examines intersections of folklore and sexuality as entry points for discussing social issues of sexual and gender identity, intolerance, and resistance. Offered alternate years.

courses.description: Examines nature of scholarly inquiry, research questions, and techniques. Historic orientation with emphasis on ideological development of folkloristics from its beginnings to the present.

courses.title: First-Year French
courses.description: Introduction to French stressing the development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills through a communicative approach. Sequence. Conducted in French.

courses.title: First-Year French
courses.description: Introduction to French stressing the development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills through a communicative approach. Sequence. Conducted in French.

courses.title: First-Year French
courses.description: Introduction to French stressing the development of listening, speaking, reading, and writing skills through a communicative approach. Sequence. Conducted in French.

courses.title: Intensive Beginning French
courses.description: Intensive study for experienced language learners; introduction to French culture. Sequence. Cannot be combined with FR 101 , FR 102 , FR 103 for more than 15 credits of first-year French.

courses.title: Intensive Beginning French
courses.description: Intensive study for experienced language learners; introduction to French culture. Cannot be combined with FR 101 , FR 102 , FR 103 for more than 15 credits of first-year French.

courses.title: Cultural Legacies of France
courses.description: This course explores and explodes cultural stereotypes while examining some of the social and cultural phenomena that many associate most strongly with France. Topics may include language and identity; love and romance; food and fashion; wars and revolutions; and cultural imperialism.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Intermediate French I
courses.description: Development of French language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Francophone world (France, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada, Caribbean, Lebanon, Magreb, Senegal, etc.). Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena. Sequence with FR 202 , FR 203 .

courses.title: Intermediate French II
courses.description: Development of French language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Francophone world (France, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada, Caribbean, Lebanon, Magreb, Senegal, etc.). Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena. Sequence with FR 201 , FR 203 .

courses.title: Intermediate French III
courses.description: Development of French language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Francophone world (France, Belgium, Switzerland, Canada, Caribbean, Lebanon, Magreb, Senegal, etc.). Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena. Sequence with FR 201 , FR 202 .

courses.title: La France contemporaine
courses.description: Training in language and culture of modern France using newspapers, short stories, poetry and film. Vocabulary enrichment activities. Conducted in French.

courses.title: Culture et langage Le monde francophone contemporain
courses.description: Training in language and cultures of the French-speaking world using literary texts, newspapers, short stories, poetry, films, websites, videos. Grammar review and vocabulary enrichment. Conducted in French.

courses.title: Oral Skills
courses.description: Practice in improving oral, comprehension, and listening skills in French. Communicative activities in class in addition to language laboratory work.

courses.title: French Survey of Francophone Literature
courses.description: Introduction to major authors and texts of the French-speaking world outside of France. Exploration of history and culture of former French colonies.

courses.title: Knights, Queens, and Rebels
courses.description: Introduction to major themes and ideas in French cultural and textual production before the 17th century.

courses.title: Monarchy, Liberty, Revolution
courses.description: Introduction to major themes and ideas in French literature from the 17th and 18th centuries through the reading of representative texts.

courses.title: Modernity and Creativity
courses.description: Representative literary works from the 19th-21st centuries with attention to textual analysis and connections to politics and society.

courses.title: French Poetry
courses.description: French and Francophone poems from the Middle Ages to the 21st century, literary movements, introduction to textual analysis and modern critical approaches.

courses.title: French Theater
courses.description: Explores important aspects of French theater. Reading plays from different periods. Emphasizes formal aspects and critical reading.

courses.title: French Narrative
courses.description: Covers important aspects of French and Francophone narrative. Reading texts from different periods. Emphasis on formal aspects and critical reading.

courses.title: French Film
courses.description: Focuses on the differences between American culture and French and Francophone cultures. Addresses a sensitive issue exemplified by the attitude of the international movie industry.

courses.title: The Natural Environment
courses.description: The earth's physical landscapes, vegetation patterns, weather, and climate; emphasis on the dynamic interactions among climate, landforms, vegetation, and soils.

courses.title: The Human Planet
courses.description: The spatial organization of humans and their activities on Earth’s surface. Cultural, political, and economic influences shaping places and their interconnections.

courses.title: Our Digital Earth
courses.description: Explores the emergence and use of the geospatial technologies that are shaping contemporary society and are pervasive in our everyday lives.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: World Regional Geography
courses.description: Introduction to the world's cultural regions. Study of the cultural and environmental factors that make different parts of the world distinct.

courses.title: Geography of Europe
courses.description: Physical and cultural processes that have shaped the rural and urban landscapes of Europe.

courses.title: Geography of the United States and Canada
courses.description: Historical and geographical analysis of the physical and human geography of the U.S. and Canada. Topics include physical regions, settlement patterns, economic development, and urbanization. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Geography of the Middle East and North Africa
courses.description: Physical and cultural processes that have shaped the rural and urban landscapes of the Middle East and North Africa.

courses.title: Geographies of Outdoor Adventure
courses.description: Introduces cultural geography by tracing the emergence and contemporary significance of outdoor adventure sports. Their global development since the late 1800s coincides with changing ideas about nature, wilderness, and the utility of play, and reflects modern social disparities and environmental challenges.

courses.title: The World and Big Data
courses.description: Explores technical foundations and social and economic applications of big data along the “5V” dimensions of volume, variety, velocity, veracity, and visualization.

courses.title: Climatology
courses.description: Energy and moisture in the atmosphere, atmospheric circulation, controls of regional and microclimates, applied climatology, climatic variations, past and future climates.

courses.title: Geomorphology
courses.description: Landforming processes with emphasis on mass movements, rivers, eolian, glacial, and coastal processes. Special fee.

courses.title: Biogeography
courses.description: The spatial patterns of biological diversity, and its causes, both in the present and in the past, and its relevance during a time of increasing human impact and climate change.

courses.title: Population and Environment
courses.description: Patterns of population growth over history and place, current policies and programs, and impacts and trends in United States and international contexts. Includes method and theory.

courses.title: Geography of Globalization
courses.description: Historical and geographical dimensions of globalization; emphasizes economic and social factors. Topics include multinationals, trade agreements, sustainability, global inequalities, and racial and gender divisions of labor.

courses.title: Society, Culture, and Place
courses.description: Examines ways in which geographical context reflects and shapes cultural and social processes. Importance of place and territory in human affairs.

courses.title: Watershed Science and Policy
courses.description: Physical and biological processes of watersheds; problems of land use, water quality, riparian zones, aquatic ecology; scientific basis of watershed management and policy. Special fee.

courses.title: Global Environmental Change
courses.description: Natural and human-induced changes in the Earth System and their impact on different environmental systems. Not available to those who have taken GEOG 143.

courses.title: Social Science Inquiry and Research
courses.description: Understanding scientific inquiry, the scientific method and learning to critique social science research. Readings and discussion focus on the questions, methods, conclusions and outcomes of research.

courses.title: Data Driven Cartography
courses.description: Introduction to concepts of cartography through representing, organizing, analyzing, and visually presenting geospatial information and data on maps. In this course we will explore different types of map design, how to represent different data types on maps, and how data impacts visual display. Sequence with GEOG 481 , GEOG 492 , GEOG 493 .

courses.title: Experimental Course:[Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Advanced Climatology: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics in climatology, including physical climatology, dynamic and synoptic climatology, and paleoclimatology.

courses.title: Advanced Biogeography: [Topic]
courses.description: Selected topics in biogeography including relation of plants and animals to their environment, historical changes in plant distribution, and palynological analysis. Special fee.

courses.title: Hydrology and Water Resources
courses.description: Emphasis on surface water including precipitation, evapotranspiration, surface runoff, and stream flow. Understanding and analysis of processes. Management for water supply and quality. Special fee.

courses.title: Fluvial Geomorphology
courses.description: Hydraulics and hydrology of stream channels; channel morphology and processes; drainage network development; fluvial deposits and landforms; field and analytical methods. Required field trips. Special fee.

courses.title: Long-Term Environmental Change
courses.description: Evolution of the physical landscape during the Quaternary period. Elements of paleoclimatology, paleoecology, and geomorphology. Required field trips. Special fee.

courses.title: Fire and Natural Disturbances
courses.description: Wildfire and other landscape disturbance processes, historical and current patterns of fire, use and management of fire. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Political Geography
courses.description: Spatial perspectives on global political patterns and processes. Relationship of political territories to resources, ethnic patterns, and ideological communities. Impact of political arrangements on identity, conflict, and peace.

courses.title: Urban Geography
courses.description: Urbanization throughout the world, the structure of urban settlements; cities as regional centers, physical places, and homes for people; geographic problems in major urban environments.

courses.title: Cultural Geography
courses.description: Patterns of culture as a force in human affairs. Dynamics of identity, place, and power. The creation of culture at different scales.

courses.title: Culture, Ethnicity, and Nationalism
courses.description: Relationship of ethnic groups and nationality to landscapes, perception, and cultural geographic phenomena. Distribution of ethnic and national groups. Junior standing required.

courses.title: Tourism and Development
courses.description: Tourism-related concepts and practices associated with tourism planning, development, marketing, and impacts in different geographic contexts.

courses.title: Environment and Development
courses.description: Critical analysis of development concepts. Economic activity and environmental impacts. Sustainable development. Development projects and landscapes in the industrializing world.

courses.title: Contemporary Food Systems
courses.description: Explores contemporary food systems at local, national, and global scales. Emphasis on the political economy and sociocultural dynamics linking agriculture, food industries, and consumption.

courses.title: GIScience I
courses.description: Introduction to geographic information science, geographic information systems (GIS), the current population survey (CPS), remote sensing, and cartography. Sequence with GEOG 482 , GEOG 491 .

courses.title: GIScience II
courses.description: Spatial data collection, spatial data models, database design, data editing, geographic information system (GIS) project management, and advanced topics in geographic information science. Sequence with GEOG 481 , GEOG 491 , GEOG 493 .

courses.title: Remote Sensing I
courses.description: Introduction to remote sensing science including its physical basis, instruments, platforms, data, processing methods, and applications. Sequence with GEOG 486 .

courses.title: Remote Sensing II
courses.description: The use of digital electromagnetic data for classification, mapping, and monitoring biologic, hydrologic, atmospheric, geologic, and human processes and environmental change. Sequence with GEOG 485 .

courses.title: Mapping with Drones
courses.description: This course introduces students to the understanding and applying the technologies, theories, and skills needed to acquire drone photography and convert this into maps useful in many professions. Applications include such subjects as resource management, physical environments, topographic mapping, vegetation, and urban characterization.

courses.title: GIScience: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced topics on geographic information systems science including spatial analysis and modeling, data visualization, cartography, volunteered geographic information, GIS programming.

courses.title: Advanced Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Automated processing of geospatial data using Python scripting language. Programming for spatial analysis such as 3-D and network modeling; processing of big data (satellite imagery, climate data, demographic information). Sequence with GEOG 481 , GEOG 482 .

courses.title: Web Mapping
courses.description: The web is full of maps, from providing context to local and international news to sharing research. We’ll explore interactive web mapping, including theories and principles of web mapping, fundamentals of cartography, and user interaction. Students will gather spatial data, develop a story or theme from their data, and produce interactive maps using front-end development.

courses.title: Advanced Cartography and Geo-Visualization
courses.description: This course explores advanced concepts in cartography, map design, geovisualization, and data visualization. Students will develop skills in GIS software and graphic design softwares to create a tailored final map design project with real-world data. Sequence with GEOG 393 .

courses.title: Spatial Analysis
courses.description: Introduction to a variety of spatial analysis techniques that can be used for understanding and modeling geographic phenomena.

courses.title: Geographic Data Analysis
courses.description: Analysis and display of geographical data by traditional data-analytical methods and by scientific-visualization approaches.

courses.title: Qualitative Methods in Geography
courses.description: Explores conceptual and practical dimensions of qualitative research. Includes linking theory and method; research question formulation; project design; ethics; data gathering, analysis, and presentation.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Advanced Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Socioeconomic analysis with geographic information systems (GIS) and the U.S. census, network modeling, 3-D models of natural and urban landscapes, web-based GIS and programming. Sequence with GEOG 581 , GEOG 582 .

courses.title: Theory and Practice of Geography I
courses.description: Introduction to professional practice in geography and the development of geographic concepts and theories from Ancient times through the mid-twentieth century. Sequence with GEOG 612 , GEOG 613 .

courses.title: Theory and Practice of Geography II
courses.description: The development of geographic concepts and theories from the mid-20th century to the present. Students learn to refine effective geographic research questions. Sequence with GEOG 611 and GEOG 613 .

courses.title: Research Design
courses.description: Examines main components of research design, including research questions, methodological approach, institutional review boards, funding programs, proposal writing, and application. Sequence with GEOG 611 and GEOG 612 .

courses.title: Progress in Physical Geography
courses.description: Recent developments in climatology, geomorphology, hydrology, and biogeography. Lectures, readings, and presentation of faculty and student works in progress.

courses.title: Progress in Human Geography
courses.description: Recent developments in cultural, economic, environmental and political geography. Lectures, readings, and presentation of faculty and student works in progress.

courses.title: Progress in Geographic Information Science
courses.description: Recent developments in cartography, GIS, remote sensing, data analysis, and visualization. Lectures, readings, and presentation of faculty and student works in progress.

courses.title: First-Year German
courses.description: Provides a thorough grammatical foundation and an elementary reading knowledge of German as well as an understanding of the spoken language. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 101 and GER 141 . Sequence.

courses.title: First-Year German
courses.description: Provides a thorough grammatical foundation and an elementary reading knowledge of German as well as an understanding of the spoken language. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 102 and GER 142 .

courses.title: First-Year German
courses.description: Provides a thorough grammatical foundation and an elementary reading knowledge of German as well as an understanding of the spoken language.

courses.title: German for Business I
courses.description: Introduction to German language and culture to start building meaningful relationships and personal connections with peers and potential business partners on different career paths. Coursework includes interpreting authentic German language sources (reading/listening/viewing) and producing language in presentational and interpersonal modes of communication (oral and written discussions, presentations) in familiar, everyday contexts. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 141 and GER 101 . Sequence with GER 142 .

courses.title: German for Business II
courses.description: Introduction to German language and culture to start building meaningful relationships and personal connections with peers and potential business partners on different career paths. Coursework includes interpreting authentic German language sources (reading/listening/viewing) and producing language in presentational and interpersonal modes of communication (oral and written discussions, presentations) in familiar, everyday contexts. Students cannot receive credit for both GER 142 and GER 102 . Sequence with GER 141 .

courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Second-Year German I
courses.description: Grammar and composition, reading selections from representative authors, conversation. Sequence.

courses.title: Second-Year German II
courses.description: Grammar and composition, reading selections from representative authors, conversation.

courses.title: Second-Year German III
courses.description: Grammar and composition, reading selections from representative authors, conversation.

courses.title: Existentialist Literature and Thought
courses.description: Survey of the existential tradition in German and Scandinavian literature, philosophy, drama, and film. Historical and conceptual developments are considered, from Kierkegaard to Kafka. Taught in English. Multilisted with SCAN 220M .

courses.title: Postwar Germany Nation Divided
courses.description: Introduction to literary and cultural movements of public dissent, including 1960s student revolutions, in postwar Germany. Conducted in English.

courses.title: Fascism and Culture
courses.description: Key debates in German culture, including the adequate representation of the Holocaust, literature in society, and the roles of ethnic and gender identities within the nation. Conducted in English.

courses.title: Germany: A Multicultural Society
courses.description: Examines the multiethnic complexities of German, Austrian, and/or Swiss societies through the writings of African, Turkish, or Jewish Germans. Period of focus varies. Conducted in English.

courses.title: The Culture of Money
courses.description: Explores ideas about money, value, and exchange in German-speaking cultures from selected moments in modern history through readings of literature, philosophy, and the arts.

courses.title: Sexuality
courses.description: Examines discourses on sexuality (e.g., sexual norms, gender roles, and divergences from them) in modern German, Austrian, and Swiss-German contexts through literature, essays, and films.

courses.title: War, Violence, Trauma
courses.description: Examines war, violence, and trauma in German and Austrian cultural history, during the modern period, through works of literature, thought, art, music, and film.

courses.title: Intermediate Language Training I
courses.description: Extensive practice in speaking and writing German; complex grammatical structures in writing.

courses.title: Intermediate Language Training II
courses.description: Extensive practice in speaking and writing German; complex grammatical structures in writing.

courses.title: Intermediate Language Training III
courses.description: Extensive practice in speaking and writing German; complex grammatical structures in writing. Option during 313 to take the Zertifikat Deutsch exam.

courses.title: Food, Culture, and Identity in Germany and Scandinavia
courses.description: Examines the relationships among food, culture, and identity in Germany and Scandinavia. Interdisciplinary readings, lectures, films, and discussions focus on literary and symbolic representations of food from production to consumption, and on images and depictions of cooking, eating, drinking, and feasting. Multilisted with SCAN 345M .

courses.title: Diversity in Germany
courses.description: Examines the social construction of identity in German literature and culture. Addresses topics of plural voices and tolerance in German-speaking cultures. Topics vary. Conducted in English.

courses.title: German Gender Studies
courses.description: Student oral presentations and written papers on such topics as men and women writers of German romanticism, mothers and daughters in German literature, comparison of men and women dramatists. No knowledge of German required; readings and discussions in English.

courses.title: German Cinema: History, Theory, Practice
courses.description: In-depth analysis of various facets of German cinema. Topics include film and the Third Reich, cinema and technology, German filmmakers in American exile, German New Wave. Conducted in English.

courses.title: German Fairy Tales
courses.description: The German fairy tale in historical and theoretical context, from the Brothers Grimm and Romantic Tales to adaptations in the 20th and 21st Centuries. Taught in English.

courses.title: Nature, Culture, and the Environment
courses.description: Introduction to the contributions German writers, philosophers, scientists, and artists have made to changing notions of nature and its supposed opposition toculture. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: German Literature Poetry, Plays, Prose
courses.description: Introduction to textual analysis--poetry, plays, and prose from 1800 to the present--in the context of major literary movements (romanticism, realism, modernism) and their social determinants. Focus on genre: poetry, plays, and prose.

courses.title: German Literature Literary Movements
courses.description: Introduction to textual analysis--poetry, plays, and prose from 1800 to the present--in the context of major literary movements (romanticism, realism, modernism) and their social determinants. Focus on literary movements.

courses.title: German Literature Interpretive Models
courses.description: Introduction to textual analysis--poetry, plays, and prose from 1800 to the present--in the context of major literary movements (romanticism, realism, modernism) and their social determinants. Focus on interpretive models.

courses.title: Modern and Contemporary in Politics and Society
courses.description: Examination of post-WWII cultural and social modernity. Most readings in Geman. Discussion, written assignments, and most reading assignments in German.

courses.title: Works, Authors, Industries in Film and Media
courses.description: Examines various aspects of German cinema and/or media in the context of socio-political history and aesthetics, including analysis of structure, composition, history, and artistry. Discussion, writing assignments, and most readings in German.

courses.title: Music and the Arts
courses.description: The course explores the history of German/Austrian/Swiss music and art and their connection to literary texts in German-speaking cultures. Discussion, writing assignments, and most reading assignments in German.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: New topics or approaches appropriate for third-year German proficiency level. Content varies; focus may be on various aspects of German language, literature, or culture and civilization.

courses.title: Translations-Transformations
courses.description: Presents the theory and practice of translation and other transformation media (e.g., the sister arts, literature into film).

courses.title: Gender, Class, Race, and Nation: [Topic]
courses.description: Part of 3-course graduate core series "Paradigms of Interpretation." Covers a range of socially embedded factors (gender, class, race, and/or nation) bearing on the interpretation of texts. Addresses ideas about identity and difference as they contribute to establishing nations, territories, borders, human hierarchies, and notions of the other. Students read texts in philosophy, literary theory, theories of race, gender, class, and/or nationality, in conjunction with German literary and artistic texts.

courses.title: Subject, Consciousness, Mind: [Topic]
courses.description: Part of a three-term series: "Paradigms of Interpretation." Deals with questions of consciousness, subjectivity, and mind in modernist philosophy and criticism. Focus is both on philosophical method and on methodology in/for the humanities and arts in first half of 20th century. Students will read texts in philosophy, psychoanalysis, literary theory, and theory of race and gender, in conjunction with German literary and artistic texts.

courses.title: Photography, Film, Video, Electronic Media: [Topic]
courses.description: Part of 3-term series "Mediations." With a focus on photography, film, video, and/or electronic media, this course traces the aesthetic and cultural significance of those media for understanding the ways that technologies of reproduction reconfigure the relation between media and literature, politics and aesthetics, mass culture and high art in German culture, aesthetics, and literature.

courses.title: Environment and Text: [Topic]
courses.description: Part of 3-term series "Mediations." With a focus on literature, painting, philosophy and critical theory, this course will examine how German writers, artists, philosophers, and theorists have processed often dramatic changes in the conception of nature in relation to human life and thought.

courses.title: Translations Transformations: [Topic]
courses.description: Already exists: Presents the theory and practice of translation and other transformation media (e.g., the sister arts, literature into film). Repeatable when topic changes. Part of 3-term series "Mediations."

courses.title: Introduction to Global Issues
courses.description: Survey of major political, economic, and cultural themes in international studies through in-class debates on key contemporary issues.

courses.title: Foundations for Intercultural Competence
courses.description: This course teaches practical skills and analytic frameworks that support safe, respectful, enlightening experiences of intercultural engagement.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Global Wellbeing
courses.description: Interdisciplinary introduction to human wellbeing, focusing on health, education, and the environment and the ways they impact wellbeing. Topics will be framed by the larger issue of whether or not something called "wellbeing" is a human universal, as well as how wellbeing can be improved.

courses.title: Perspectives on International Development
courses.description: Introduction to major ideologies, theories, historical processes, and contemporary challenges in international development. Galvan.

courses.title: Value Systems in Cross-Cultural Perspective
courses.description: Introduction to value systems of various cultures, focusing on how values relate to religion, forms of social organization, group affiliation, and patterns of conflict resolution.

courses.title: Globalization and the Global Economy
courses.description: Introduction to the study of globalization and the global economy, analyzing ways the global economy is structured and maintained through various political interventions and regimes. This course aims to provide tools for understanding and explaining these structures and their impacts.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Global Health and Development
courses.description: Introduction to major issues in global health, their causes and possible solutions, with a focus on the poor in developing countries.

courses.title: African Contemporary Issues and Concerns
courses.description: Introduces students to current challenges facing African peoples today. Extends survey of Africa courses, and prepares students for more advanced study regarding the African continent.

courses.title: Education and Development
courses.description: This course will introduce students to the foundational ideas in the field of international and comparative education, and help build a theoretical toolkit of the major approaches scholars utilize when analyzing education from a global perspective.

courses.title: International Cooperation and Conflict
courses.description: Utilizes case studies and selected themes to examine the root causes, stakeholder perspectives, and attempts to resolve international conflicts.

courses.title: International Human Rights
courses.description: Survey of human rights, examining diverse perspectives on the concept, practice, and implementation of human rights and human rights regimes.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.description: Special topics in international studies.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Recent topics include Africa: Development and Social Change.

courses.title: The Global Story of Race
courses.description: Working from a historical and cultural perspective, this course uses global case studies to explore how race came to be a key principle of social organization around the world through colonialism and extractive capitalism, and how it manifests today.

courses.title: Global Community Development
courses.description: Introduction to communitarian theory and local-level grass-roots development practices. Comparison across North-South divide of efforts to alleviate poverty, promote sustainability, and ensure mobilization and cohesion.

courses.title: Gender and International Development
courses.description: Analysis of gendered dynamics of changing roles, opportunities, and expectations as societies undergo social and environmental change associated with economic, social, cultural and political development.

courses.title: Aid to Developing Countries
courses.description: Examines the history and current dynamics of international bilateral and multilateral development assistance, the possibilities and constraints of aid, and other related issues.

courses.title: United Nations Intervention in Global Crises
courses.description: Explores the theory and practice of humanitarian aid, peace-building, and development during or after violent conflict. Focuses on work of international organizations in conflict areas or on issues of conflict.

courses.title: Global Food Security
courses.description: Explores explanations for, and solutions to, persistent inequities in food access. Considers the political, agricultural, economic and humanitarian aspects of the global food system.

courses.title: Cross-Cultural Communication
courses.description: Focuses on skills and insights needed by everyone working in cross-cultural settings. Considers values, development, education, politics, and environment as central to cross-cultural understanding. Theoretical as well as applied approach, with lectures, discussions and hands-on activities.

courses.title: Global Perspectives on Disability
courses.description: This class uses a human rights paradigm to examine issues facing people with disabilities throughout the world. Readings and discussions will emphasize cross-disability and cross-cultural approaches to gender and disability, international development and disability, inclusive educational models, and cross-cultural aspects of disability.

courses.title: South Asia: Development and Social Change
courses.description: Introduction to the vast social changes and development issues confronting the South Asian subcontinent.

courses.title: Europe Development and Social Change
courses.description: Through the lens of development studies, students (1) develop a fundamental knowledge and literacy regarding current European quality of life issues, (2) critically examine how these issues are viewed by various stakeholders, and (3) consider attempts to enhance human, national and regional development processes.

courses.title: Southeast Asia Today
courses.description: Introduction to mainland and island Southeast Asia, with a special focus on social changes that are occurring across the region and how change is affecting peoples and societies.

courses.title: Development and Social Change in Sub-Saharan Africa
courses.description: Introduces theoretical and practical aspects of development and social change in sub-Saharan Africa, with focus on key issues in African development during the post-colonial era.

courses.title: Development and Social Change in Latin America
courses.description: Explores development challenges, debt cycles, urban growth, neoliberalism, populism, socialism, gender, the environment, U.S.–Latin American relations, ecotourism, and drug geographies in the region.

courses.title: Bollywood's Lens on Indian Society
courses.description: Explores Indian society through film, focusing on critical social issues; depicted vs. the historical reality; and ongoing transformations of social orientations and values.

courses.title: Population Displacement and Global Health
courses.description: Explores health and mental health problems affecting displaced (migrant and refugee) communities and considers underdevelopment as a fundamental cause of displacement and health problems. Offered once per academic year.

courses.title: Global Reproductive Health
courses.description: Overview of issues in global reproductive health, including politics, economics, historical and cultural factors. Implications for international health and development programs reviewed. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: The Global Story of Race
courses.description: Working from a historical and cultural perspective, this course uses global case studies to explore how race came to be a key principle of social organization around the world through colonialism and extractive capitalism, and how it manifests today.

courses.title: Global Community Development
courses.description: Introduction to communitarian theory and local-level grass-roots development practices. Comparison across North-South divide of efforts to alleviate poverty, promote sustainability, and ensure mobilization and cohesion.

courses.title: Gender and International Development
courses.description: Analysis of gendered dynamics of changing roles, opportunities, and expectations as societies undergo social and environmental change associated with economic, social, cultural and political development.
