courses.title: Marketing Management
courses.description: Marketing Management addresses market analysis and segmentation, targeting, and positioning. Emphasis is on marketing strategies designed to deliver superior customer value and achieve organizational objectives.

courses.title: Marketing Research
courses.description: Marketing research as a tool for decision-making. Planning research projects; design, measurement, experimental and nonexperimental techniques, analysis and interpretation of data; reporting research results.

courses.title: Marketing Strategy
courses.description: Relationship between marketing and other functional areas of a business. Emphasis on case analysis as a means of acquiring both planning and operational skills.

courses.title: Strategic Brand Management
courses.description: This course will explore a range of issues related to the strategic management of brands. After building a fundamental understanding of the importance of brands and their core characteristics, we will focus on four key aspects of brand management – building/managing brands, leveraging brands, identifying brands / measuring their equity, and protecting brands.

courses.title: Theory and Research in Marketing Management
courses.description: Application of marketing concepts and of economics, management science, and behavioral science to the management of the product, price, promotion, and distribution variables.

courses.title: Theory and Research in Consumer Behavior
courses.description: The applicability of behavioral theories and methodologies to the understanding of the consumption process.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Materials in Technology I
courses.description: Fundamental principles necessary to understand structure-property relations in materials. Key concepts from physics, general chemistry, and mathematics are covered to instill core materials science subject matter and introduce knowledge of challenges in technology requiring innovation in modern materials. Included are thermodynamic concepts of structure from bonding to microstructure, and how to apply these interrelationships between structure and properties in technology relevant materials, diffusion processes, and gain competency in modern programming languages for data analysis. Sequence with MSTC 232 .

courses.title: Fundamentals of Materials in Technology II
courses.description: Continuation of fundamental principles necessary to understand structure-property relations in materials. In this second course (of the two-course sequence), the student will develop a fundamental understanding of defects in solids and how those, along with structure and bonding, govern, mechanical, thermal, electrical, optical, magnetic, and chemical properties of materials used in key technological applications such as computing, energy, and communications, and those used in structural and mechanical engineering. Sequence with MSTC 231 .

courses.title: Thermal Physics of Advanced Materials
courses.description: This course is the first one in a two-course sequence that teaches and applies a foundational understanding of thermodynamics, kinetics, and transport to understand how materials are formed and what governs the time evolution of materials processes. This course focuses on the thermodynamic properties of materials, i.e. how concepts of energy minimization determine the equilibrium phases and properties.

courses.title: Kinetics and Transport in Advanced Materials
courses.description: This course is the first one in a two-course sequence that teaches and applies a foundational understanding of thermodynamics, kinetics, and transport to understand how materials are formed and what governs the time evolution of materials processes. Begins with concepts of thermodynamically reversible and irreversible process and how those relate to entropy and entropy production. Transport by diffusion, rates of chemical reactions, and materials processes nucleation and growth and morphological evolution in solids are covered.

courses.title: Electronic, Optical and Magnetic Properties of Materials I
courses.description: In the first part of this two-course sequence, the student will study the significant role played by geometry and chemical composition in determining general material properties and explore how this structure conspires with quantum physics to govern the mechanical, thermal, and electrical properties of condensed matter, including the emergence of semiconducting behavior and superconductivity.

courses.title: Electronic, Optical and Magnetic Properties of Materials II
courses.description: In this course, the second part of a sequence, the student will extend their knowledge to magnetic, dielectric, and optical properties, and apply this knowledge to understand semiconductor devices (transistors, photovoltaics, diode lasers, etc.), nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR), spin-based quantum bits, and nanomaterials.

courses.title: Orientation to Music Education
courses.description: An orientation for first year music education majors, including an overview of music education philosophy and contemporary issues in music education. In addition, an exploration of career opportunities in music education and other fields will be covered.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Foundations of Music Education
courses.description: Professional orientation for prospective school music teachers; curricular, historical, philosophical, and social foundation of music education; ethical, professional, and social aspects of teaching; comprehensive field experience. Extra fee.

courses.title: Athletic Band: [Topic]
courses.description: Participation in an athletic band. Examples are Green Garter Band, Oregon Basketball Band, Oregon Marching Band, Yellow Garter Band.

courses.title: Teaching Laboratory I
courses.description: Practice in teaching using microteaching techniques and music education methods in a laboratory setting.

courses.title: Teaching Laboratory I
courses.description: Practice in teaching using microteaching techniques and music education methods in a laboratory setting.

courses.title: Teaching Laboratory I
courses.description: Practice in teaching using microteaching techniques and music education methods in a laboratory setting.

courses.description: Elementary instruction in pedagogy and performance of various instruments. Sections in violin and cello, low brass, high brass, percussion, flute and clarinet, saxophone, oboe and bassoon, and voice. Instrument rental fee.

courses.title: Band Methods
courses.description: Concerns of band teachers in secondary and elementary schools. Observations, procedures, and instructional materials; planning and teaching lessons for analysis and criticism. Admission to the MUP 300 level in primary instrument required.

courses.title: Elementary Music Methods
courses.description: This course is designed to prepare upper-division music education majors to teach elementary music in public schools. Students will become familiar with basic common pedagogical techniques used in the general music classroom. Lab fee.

courses.title: Secondary Choral Methods
courses.description: Secondary choral music curriculum, teaching methods, sight singing and music literacy, developing independent musicianship, philosophical and social foundation of vocal music education in the public schools.

courses.title: Contemporary Methods in Music Education
courses.description: This course is designed to assist upper-level and graduate music education students develop an understanding of Contemporary methods as commonly used in American public school general music K-9 grade classrooms and to apply these techniques to in-class and virtual teaching assignments.

courses.title: Music in Special Education
courses.description: Designed to assist senior and graduate music education majors and pedagogy majors in their awareness of the physical, intellectual, emotional, and social conditions of students with exceptionalities, so that they may be included and accommodated in music activities in the classroom.

courses.title: Music Classroom Management
courses.description: Techniques in classroom management; crises prevention and intervention; techniques for providing a safe and positive classroom environment; professional ethics and legal expectations.

courses.title: Music in School and Society
courses.description: Elementary and secondary school music programs, past and present, and their relationships to the communities they serve.

courses.title: Curricular Strategies in Music Education
courses.description: Procedures for developing music courses for today’s schools; determination of goals, content, instructional materials, and evaluative criteria; exploration of significant curriculum development projects in music education.

courses.title: Teaching Singing in the Classroom
courses.description: Methods for teaching group vocal technique in the classroom with emphasis on elementary, mid-level, and emerging adult voices. Concentration on development of the adolescent changing voice.

courses.title: Practicum in Music Education
courses.description: MUE 450 /550 provides scope, structure, supervision, and guidance to support Music Education students during two terms of Music Education practica in accordance with the Oregon Teacher Standards.

courses.title: Marching Band Methods
courses.description: Teaching methods for secondary school marching bands.

courses.title: String Methods
courses.description: Teaching methods for the beginning string class in elementary and middle schools. Development of technique sequences for string groups in secondary schools.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Violin and Viola Teaching
courses.description: Development of technique for instruction of beginning violin students, including philosophy, practice habits for young students, parent education, and pedagogical skills.

courses.title: Early-Intermediate Violin Repertoire and Technique
courses.description: This course provides an overview of early and intermediate repertoire, focusing specifically on sequentially-organized pieces that teachers can use to logically build violin technique.

courses.title: Pedagogy Methods: Violin and Viola
courses.description: Principles and techniques of violin and viola teaching selected from the pedagogical approaches of Flesch, Galamian, Dounis, Rolland.

courses.title: Advanced Violin/Viola Pedagogy
courses.description: This course will examine the major violin pedagogy schools with their pertinent somatic counterparts. Teaching practicums will be included.

courses.title: Somatics for String Players
courses.description: This course will examine somatic (body awareness) in string playing. Human anatomy, performance anxiety, proper breathing, and playing alignment will be addressed. Teaching practicums will be included.

courses.title: Community Music Institute Practicum
courses.description: This course serves as a repeatable teaching lab with students enrolled in the University of Oregon's Community Music Institute. Initial term includes observation of master teachers. Subsequent terms include guided teaching assignments commensurate with the university student's level of experience and expertise.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy I: Teaching Beginners
courses.description: In-depth study of beginning methods and materials for children and adults. Individual teaching experience. Offered alternating years.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy II Teaching Groups
courses.description: Methods and materials for group instruction of all ages and levels. Survey of learning theories and new technologies. Individual and group teaching experience. Offered alternating years.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy III Teaching Intermediate Levels
courses.description: Study of repertoire, technique, and teaching methods appropriate for intermediate-level piano students. Individual and master-class teaching experience. Offered alternating years.

courses.title: Teaching Laboratory II
courses.description: Practice in teaching using microteaching techniques and music education methods in a laboratory setting.

courses.title: Advanced Pedagogy: [Topic]
courses.description: Sections in piano and other topics.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Various advanced topics offered periodically according to student and faculty interest and availability.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: Various topics at a level above that available in the standard curriculum.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Band Methods
courses.description: Concerns of band teachers in secondary and elementary schools. Observations, procedures, and instructional materials; planning and teaching lessons for analysis and criticism. Admission to the MUP 300 level in primary instrument or voice required.

courses.title: Elementary Music Methods
courses.description: This course is designed to prepare upper-division music education majors to teach elementary music in public schools. Students will become familiar with basic common pedagogical techniques used in the general music classroom. Lab fee.

courses.title: Secondary Choral Methods
courses.description: Secondary choral music curriculum, teaching methods, sight singing and music literacy, developing independent musicianship, philosophical and social foundation of vocal music education in the public schools.

courses.title: Contemporary Methods in Music Education
courses.description: This course is designed to assist upper-level and graduate music education students develop an understanding of Contemporary methods as commonly used in American public school general music K-9 grade classrooms and to apply these techniques to in-class and virtual teaching assignments.

courses.title: Music in Special Education
courses.description: Designed to assist senior and graduate music education majors and pedagogy majors in their awareness of the physical, intellectual, emotional, and social conditions of students with exceptionalities, so that they may be included and accommodated in music activities in the classroom.

courses.title: Music Classroom Management
courses.description: Techniques in classroom management; crises prevention and intervention; techniques for providing a safe and positive classroom environment; professional ethics and legal expectations.

courses.title: Music in School and Society
courses.description: Elementary and secondary school music programs, past and present, and their relationships to the communities they serve.

courses.title: Curricular Strategies in Music Education
courses.description: Procedures for developing music courses for today’s schools; determination of goals, content, instructional materials, and evaluative criteria; exploration of significant curriculum development projects in music education.

courses.title: Teaching Singing in the Classroom
courses.description: Methods for teaching group vocal technique in the classroom with emphasis on elementary, mid-level, and emerging adult voices. Concentration on development of the adolescent changing voice.

courses.title: Practicum in Music Education
courses.description: MUE 450 /550 provides scope, structure, supervision, and guidance to support Music Education students during two terms of Music Education practica in accordance with the Oregon Teacher Standards.

courses.title: Marching Band Methods
courses.description: Teaching methods for secondary school marching bands.

courses.title: String Methods
courses.description: Teaching methods for the beginning string class in elementary and middle schools. Development of technique sequences for string groups in secondary schools.

courses.title: Fundamentals of Violin and Viola Teaching
courses.description: Development of technique for instruction of beginning violin students, including philosophy, practice habits for young students, parent education, and pedagogical skills.

courses.title: Early-Intermediate Violin Repertoire and Technique
courses.description: This course provides an overview of early and intermediate repertoire, focusing specifically on sequentially-organized pieces that teachers can use to logically build violin technique.

courses.title: Pedagogy Methods: Violin and Viola
courses.description: Principles and techniques of violin and viola teaching selected from the pedagogical approaches of Flesch, Galamian, Dounis, Rolland.

courses.title: Advanced Violin/Viola Pedagogy
courses.description: This course will examine the major violin pedagogy schools with their pertinent somatic counterparts. Teaching practicums will be included.

courses.title: Somatics for String Players
courses.description: This course will examine somatic (body awareness) in string playing. Human anatomy, performance anxiety, proper breathing, and playing alignment will be addressed. Teaching practicums will be included.

courses.title: Community Music Institute Practicum
courses.description: This course serves as a repeatable teaching lab with students enrolled in the University of Oregon's Community Music Institute. Initial term includes observation of master teachers. Subsequent terms include guided teaching assignments commensurate with the university student's level of experience and expertise.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy I: Teaching Beginners
courses.description: In-depth study of beginning methods and materials for children and adults. Individual teaching experience. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy II Teaching Groups
courses.description: Methods and materials for group instruction of all ages and levels. Survey of learning theories and new technologies. Individual and group teaching experience. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Piano Pedagogy III Teaching Intermediate Levels
courses.description: Study of repertoire, technique, and teaching methods appropriate for intermediate-level piano students. Individual and master-class teaching experience. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Advanced Pedagogy
courses.description: Sections in piano and other topics.

courses.title: Dissertation

courses.title: Quantitative Research in Music Education
courses.description: A focus on quantitative methodology and research in music education, including an introduction to descriptive and inferential statistics. Terminology, methods, and concepts relating to peer-reviewed research will be explored. Students will also have the opportunity to carry out a study using quantitative methods.

courses.title: Jazz Practicum
courses.description: Students will complete observations of experienced teachers in academic, ensemble, and studio settings, and will receive qualitative feedback from cooperating teachers through short, supervised teaching demonstrations.

courses.title: Pedagogy and Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: Teaching strategies and practical application. Topics include composition, conducting, ethnomusicology, jazz studies, music education, music history, music technology, music theory, performance practice, instrumental conducting, voice, keyboard, strings, woodwinds, brass, and percussion.

courses.title: College Music Teaching
courses.description: Developing knowledge, skills, and attitudes useful for teaching music; current principles of educational psychology at the college level, instructional techniques, tests and measurements.

courses.title: Psychology of Music
courses.description: Study of psychological, physiological, and neurological aspects of musical behavior and experience, including acoustics, human hearing, perception and cognition, development and expertise, affective response and preference, unusual abilities.

courses.title: History of Western Music Education
courses.description: A comprehensive overview of philosophical and historical foundations in music education from antiquity to the present day. It involves not only the study of music education from these perspectives, but general education and policy as well.

courses.title: Campus Athletic Band: [Topic]
courses.description: Participation in an athletic band. Examples are Green Garter Band, Oregon Basketball Band, Oregon Marching Band, Yellow Garter Band.

courses.title: Jazz Theory

courses.title: Functional Jazz Piano II

courses.title: Jazz Improvisation II

courses.description: History, biography, multiculturalism, and racism in modern jazz and free jazz to present. Includes Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, Miles Davis, John Coltrane, Ornette Coleman.

courses.title: Popular Piano and Musicianship II
courses.description: Continuing study of musicianship—integrated music theory, ear training, and piano—through piano instruction in popular music styles. Sequence with MUS 141 , MUS 143 .

courses.title: Popular Music Analysis
courses.description: Analysis of popular songs in diverse styles, focusing on form, harmony, timbre, rhythm, and lyrics.

courses.title: Popular Musics in Global Context
courses.description: Surveys the global popular music landscape of the 20th and 21st centuries, with an emphasis on identity and cultural mixture.

courses.title: US Popular Music 1800 to 1930
courses.description: This class examines the origins and development of popular music in the USA from its roots in the 19th century through the 1920s.

courses.title: US Popular Music 1930 to 1965
courses.description: This class examines the development of popular music in the USA from 1930 to 1965, including swing, blues, and the rise of rock ’n’ roll.

courses.title: US Popular Music 1965 to 2000
courses.description: This class examines the development of popular music in the USA from 1965-2000 by contrasting mainstream rock with various “alternative” genres.

courses.title: History of Music 800 to 1600
courses.description: Study of the history and evolution of music, principally Western art music, from the early Middle Ages through the Renaissance.

courses.title: Survey of Music History 1600 to 1800
courses.description: Study of the history and evolution of music, principally Western art music, from the early Middle Ages to the present.

courses.title: Survey of Music History 1800 to Present
courses.description: Key themes in the history, culture, aesthetics, and social context of Western art music from 1800 to the present.

courses.title: History of the Blues
courses.description: Traces blues music from its African and African American roots through its 20th-century history and its influence on the values of jazz, rhythm and blues, and country music.

courses.title: Music of the Woodstock Generation
courses.description: Examines the relationship between popular music and social upheavals in the United States during the 1960s.

courses.title: Analysis: [Topic]
courses.description: Techniques of analysis in various types of music.

courses.title: Composition II
courses.description: Composition and public performance of small works for piano, voice, and small ensembles.

courses.description: Composition and public performance of small works for piano, voice, and small ensembles.

courses.title: Composition II

courses.title: Music, Money, and the Law
courses.description: Explores theory and history of relationship between money and music, and corresponding laws that govern and shape that relationship. Topics include copyright, contract rights, media distribution technology, marketing, unions.

courses.title: The Music of Bach and Handel
courses.description: Compositions by Bach and Handel such as organ chorales, cantatas, oratorios, operas, and masses; cultural context in Germany, France, Italy, and England for the development of their styles.

courses.title: Music in World Cultures
courses.description: Explores the music of three world regions in their sociocultural context. Emphasis on listening skills, relationships between music and culture, aesthetics, styles, genres, music structures and forms, and participatory music making.

courses.title: Music of the Americas
courses.description: African American, Latin American, and Native American music in sociocultural context. Includes listening skills, music-culture relationship, aesthetics, styles, genres, music structures and forms, and participatory music making.

courses.title: Hip-Hop History, Culture, Aesthetics
courses.description: Examines the history and evolution of hip-hop and rap music in the late 20th and early 21st centuries.

courses.title: The Beatles and Their Times
courses.description: Presents and examines the music of the Beatles in the context of post–World War II English and United States cultures and 1960s Western youth cultures.

courses.title: Survey of African Music
courses.description: Students analyze musical expression—including traditional, neotraditional, and contemporary mass mediated popular music (Afro-pop)—in Africa and the diaspora.

courses.title: Campus Small Ensemble: [Topic]

courses.title: Campus Band: [Topic]
courses.description: Participation in Campus Band. No audition needed. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Campus Orchestra

courses.description: Participation in a choral ensemble. Gospel Choir, Gospel Singers, Gospel Ensemble. Audition required for Gospel Singers and Gospel Ensemble. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Film Music
courses.description: Film is one of the most culturally significant art forms of the 20th–21st centuries, and music has always played a crucial role in its production and experience. This class introduces students to the history and analysis of film music from silent cinema to the present.

courses.title: Introduction to Conducting
courses.description: Introduction to conducting with emphasis on the art and study of conducting, baton and left-hand technique, nonverbal (facial and body) communication, leadership, terminology, transpositions, and score reading.

courses.title: Collegium Musicum
courses.description: Study of music repertoire of the medieval, Renaissance, and baroque periods through rehearsals and extensive sight-reading; vocal and instrumental repertoire. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Oregon Electronic Device Orchestra
courses.description: Performance ensemble that uses data-driven musical instruments in combination with software and hardware to perform music and intermedia compositions.

courses.description: Participation in an orchestra. University Symphony Orchestra, Campus Orchestra. Ensemble fee.

courses.description: Participation in a choral ensemble. Chamber Choir, Concert Choir, Gospel Singers, Repertoire Singers, University Gospel Choir, University Gospel Ensemble, University Singers, Women's Choir. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Opera Workshop

courses.title: Post-Tonal Theory II
courses.description: Introduction to theory and analysis of post-tonal music. Concepts of pitch-class set analysis and practical applications. Sequence. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: The Collaborative Pianist
courses.description: Comprehensive study of techniques and literature for artistic ensemble performance by pianists. Includes chamber music, art song, opera arias, accompaniment, sight-reading, and orchestral reduction skills. Sequence.

courses.description: Comprehensive study of techniques and literature for artistic ensemble performance by pianists. Includes chamber music, art song, opera arias, accompaniment, sight-reading, and orchestral reduction skills. Sequence.

courses.title: The Collaborative Pianist
courses.description: Comprehensive study of techniques and literature for artistic ensemble performance by pianists. Includes chamber music, art song, opera arias, accompaniment, sight-reading, and orchestral reduction skills. Sequence.

courses.title: Cultures of Musical Celebrity
courses.description: Examines the cultural phenomenon of celebrity among musicians, composers, and audiences from antiquity to the present. Topics include cults, crowds, branding, shrines, and charisma.

courses.title: Tonal Analysis: Linear Prolongational Analysis
courses.description: Introduction to techniques of linear/prolongational analysis; exploration of connections between contrapuntal structures and small musical forms.

courses.title: Tonal Analysis: Form in Tonal Music
courses.description: Exploration of the analysis of form in music from the Baroque, Classical, and Romantic eras; covers Sonata Theory, form-functional theory, processual approaches to form, and the relationship between formal types and linear structures.

courses.title: Tonal Analysis: Analysis of Popular Music
courses.description: Exploration of the analysis of popular music, with repertoire from the 1960s to the present; discussion of methodologies adapted from traditional music theory, including form, harmony, meter, and rhythm, as well as more repertoire-specific topics, such as timbre, texture, and lyrics.

courses.title: Counterpoint
courses.description: Study of modal and tonal counterpoint through analysis and composition: 16th-century sacred polyphony.

courses.title: Counterpoint
courses.description: Study of modal and tonal counterpoint through analysis and composition: baroque imitative counterpoint.

courses.title: Counterpoint
courses.description: Study of modal and tonal counterpoint through analysis and composition: varies—typically devoted to more advanced fugal writing, 20th-century counterpoint, or other modal composition.

courses.title: World Music Ensemble: [Topic]
courses.description: Students engage the embodied practices and sociocultural contexts of diverse music-dance practices associated with the world music categories specified in the topic.

courses.title: Composers Forum
courses.description: Formulation of a two- or three-concert series of student compositions; sessions with visiting composers and UO performers and listening projects related to these residencies.

courses.title: Composition III
courses.description: Composition and public performance of works including large or chamber ensembles. Preparation of works for senior recital.

courses.title: Composition III
courses.description: Composition and public performance of works including large or chamber ensembles. Preparation of works for senior recital.

courses.title: Composition III
courses.description: Composition and public performance of works including large or chamber ensembles. Preparation of works for senior recital.

courses.title: Electronic Composition
courses.description: Develops an elementary understanding about how computers and software are used to process digital audio and create musical compositions. Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Digital Audio and Sound Design
courses.description: Examines concepts of digital audio representation, sampling, and processing; considers audio mixing, basic synthesis, and sound modification techniques and fundamentals of electroacoustic composition. Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Interactive Media Performance
courses.description: Examines concepts of interactive performance using MIDI, digital audio, and video processing, and considers issues related to designing performance algorithms in software. Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Sensor Music
courses.description: Examines the fundamental principles for microprocessors and sensor interface design within the context of musical performance, composition, and improvisation.

courses.title: Introduction to Ethnomusicology
courses.description: World musics studied in their social and cultural contexts. Compares the varied approaches, ideas, and methods of selected American and European researchers since 1980.

courses.title: Musical Instruments of the World
courses.description: Examines instruments of the world in their cultural contexts. Covers cross-cultural issues and focuses on particular geographic areas. Includes films, recordings, live demonstrations.

courses.title: Lyric Diction
courses.description: Analysis and International Phonetic Alphabet transcription of song and opera texts with emphasis on the singer's approach to performance. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Lyric Diction
courses.description: Analysis and International Phonetic Alphabet transcription of song and opera texts with emphasis on the singer's approach to performance. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Popular Musics in the African Diaspora
courses.description: Examines social and historical contexts of popular musics in the African diaspora from the 20th century forward. Geographic focus is North America, the Caribbean, and Africa.

courses.title: Popular Music Studies
courses.description: This seminar explores current research and foundational texts in the interdisciplinary field of popular music studies.

courses.title: Solo Vocal Music
courses.description: Solo songs with accompaniment; the lute air and Purcell; 19th-century art songs in Germany and France; 20th-century British, American, and Continental song literature; development of bases for artistic performance and sound critical judgment through study of text, voice, and accompaniment. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Solo Vocal Music
courses.description: Solo songs with accompaniment; the lute air and Purcell; 19th-century art songs in Germany and France; 20th-century British, American, and Continental song literature; development of bases for artistic performance and sound critical judgment through study of text, voice, and accompaniment. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: History of Electroacoustic Music
courses.description: Examines the development of aesthetic movements, styles, media, instruments, and performance practice related to electroacoustic music.

courses.title: Musical Performance Networks
courses.description: Examines various types of network architectures and data-processing and mapping strategies that can be applied to real-time musical outcomes.

courses.title: History of Opera I: 1600-1800
courses.description: A study of the history of opera from 1600-1800. The course focuses on four thematic pillars: intellectual movements that spurred the early development of opera; critical debates about the relationship between music, text, spoken drama, and dance; opera as a medium for politics; and opera's patrons, audiences, and developing industry as it moved between courts and public opera houses.

courses.title: History of Opera
courses.description: Critical study of the musical and dramatic content of operas forming the standard international repertoire, from Mozart to the present.

courses.title: Digital Audio Workstation Techniques I
courses.description: Explores the sequencing, editing, and routing of MIDI and digital audio using a computer. Basic Mac skills recommended. Series with MUS 477 and MUS 478 . Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Digital Audio Workstation Techniques II
courses.description: Explores the principles and techniques of audio mixing, sound design, and music production using a computer. Series with MUS 476 and MUS 478 . Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Digital Audio Workstation Techniques III
courses.description: Explores advanced techniques of mixing, the principles of mastering, and digital distribution using a computer. Series with MUS 476 and MUS 477 . Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Data Sonification
courses.description: Sonification uses non-speech sound to reveal new insights about data, insights that may be missed using visualizations and other graphic representations of data. The course explores developing audio applications for discovery and research and covers work in the fields of data sonification and auditory display.

courses.title: Audio Production Techniques I
courses.description: Hardware and software techniques for use in a music recording studio environment, including microphone usage, recording techniques, and digital production. Sequence with MUS 481 , MUS 482 . Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Audio Recording Techniques II
courses.description: Application of advanced recording techniques. Sequence with MUS 480 , MUS 482 . Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Audio Recording Techniques III
courses.description: Focuses on the production concepts and techniques necessary to produce a full-length, professional-quality compact disc. Sequence with MUS 480 , MUS 481 . Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Audio Effects Theory and Design
courses.description: Audio effects are common and useful tools used in the recording, mixing, and mastering of music and other sound, as well as in sound design. This course focuses on understanding, designing, and implementing audio effects, and using them for musical projects.

courses.title: Choral Conducting and Literature
courses.description: Choral conducting, gesture and communication, rehearsal technique, and choral literature appropriate for secondary school choral music programs (grades 6–12), community youth choirs, and collegiate ensembles.

courses.title: Instrumental Conducting
courses.description: Conducting techniques as applied to band and orchestral music with emphasis on various styles and periods of music; study of 20th-century rhythms and related conducting problems.

courses.title: Music and Emotion
courses.description: Introduction to the psychological and philosophical study of music and the emotions, with emphasis on cognitive, evolutionary, behavioral, and socio-cultural perspectives.

courses.title: Analog Recording Techniques
courses.description: Analog Recording using reel to reel tape machines provides students with a unique experience in not only sonic quality, but also artistic and technical decision-making due to the mechanical limitations presented by the analog format.

courses.title: Analysis of Rhythm and Meter
courses.description: This course focuses on theories and analytical methodologies for studying rhythm and meter, with focal repertoire drawn from European classical music, English- language popular music, and global vernacular and traditional repertoires. Topics covered include hypermeter and metrical levels, rhythmic consonance and dissonance, Afro-Cuban rhythmic patterns, and groove.

courses.title: Balinese Gamelan
courses.description: Pacific Rim Gamelan ensemble. Performance of original compositions and traditional music for gamelan. Limited to twelve performers. Three public performances a year.

courses.description: Projects in music history, analysis, theory, composition, performance, or related disciplines designed by the student in consultation with the instructor.

courses.description: Use of reference, research, and bibliographical sources in music. Sequence.

courses.title: Introduction to Musicology
courses.description: Introduces musicology and several of its subfields; includes current and recent arguments.

courses.title: Current Trends in Music Theory
courses.description: Surveys recent and developing trends in the discipline of music theory; includes discussion of writing and research methods.

courses.title: Bibliography in Instrumental Conducting
courses.description: Survey of research in conducting. Discussion of rehearsal strategies and psychology.

courses.title: Wind Repertoire
courses.description: Survey and analysis of music composed for large wind groups, from 1500 to the present. Sequence.

courses.title: Wind Repertoire
courses.description: Survey and analysis of music composed for large wind groups, from 1500 to the present. Sequence.

courses.title: Wind Repertoire
courses.description: Survey and analysis of music composed for large wind groups, from 1500 to the present. Sequence.

courses.title: Instrumental Conducting Laboratory
courses.description: Study, preparation, and conducting of works for instrumental ensembles in rehearsals and performances.

courses.title: Orchestral Music: Bach to Beethoven
courses.description: Survey of orchestral music from Bach to Beethoven. Sequence with MUS 626 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Orchestral Music: 1825 to Modern
courses.description: Survey of orchestral music from 1825 to the modern era. Sequence with MUS 625 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Survey of Choral Literature I
courses.description: Survey and analysis of choral repertoire from the Renaissance to the modern era with an emphasis on genres and text settings

courses.title: Survey of Choral Literature II
courses.description: Survey of sacred and secular choral music from 1450 to the present with an emphasis on an expanded view beyond the traditional canon

courses.title: Repertoire and Analysis
courses.description: Analytical interpretations of musical works in a context that focuses on repertoire rather than on particular analytical methodologies. The pieces studied vary each time the course is offered.

courses.title: History of Theory I
courses.description: Examination and evaluation of theories of music from ancient times to the 16th century, including Aristides Quintilianus, Boethius, Hucbald, Guido, Franco, Tinctoris, Ramis, and Aron. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: History of Theory II
courses.description: Examination and evaluation of theories of music from the 16th to 19th centuries, including Glarean, Zarlino, Descartes, Rameau, Tartini, Kirnberger, C. P. E. Bach, Fetis, Sechter, and Helmholtz. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: History of Theory III
courses.description: Theories of harmony and structure ranging from the mid-19th century to the present, including Hauptmann, Riemann, Schenker, Schoenberg, Hindemith, Babbitt, Forte, Lewin, Straus, and Lerdahl. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Advanced Post-Tonal Theory
courses.description: Analytic approaches to twelve-tone music.

courses.title: Advanced Composition Studies
courses.description: Studio instruction in composition.

courses.title: Advanced Composition Studies
courses.description: Studio instruction in composition.

courses.title: Advanced Composition Studies
courses.description: Studio instruction in composition.

courses.title: Notation of Medieval and Renaissance Music
courses.description: Representative examples of notational systems and practices in Western European polyphony from 900 to 1600. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Advanced Electronic Composition
courses.description: Develops an advanced understanding of computers and software and how they are used to process digital audio and create musical and mediacompositions. Repeatable with instructor’s consent. Laboratory fee.

courses.title: Piano Literature
courses.description: Advanced study of solo piano literature from Bach to the present. Sequence with MUS 650 , MUS 651 , MUS 652 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Piano Literature
courses.description: Advanced study of solo piano literature from Bach to the present. Sequence with MUS 650 , MUS 651 , MUS 652 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Piano Literature
courses.description: Advanced study of solo piano literature from Bach to the present. Sequence with MUS 650 , MUS 651 , MUS 652 . Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Music in the Middle Ages
courses.description: Sources of Western European music in classical antiquity and the Near East; sacred monophony, secular monophony; development of polyphony. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Music in the Renaissance
courses.description: The central Renaissance style in 15th-century France and Italy; high Renaissance music; late Renaissance music; developments in England and Germany; instrumental music; Renaissance music theory. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Music in the Baroque Era
courses.description: Musical genres in Italy, France, Germany, Austria, Britain, the Netherlands, Spain, Mexico, and South America in historical, social, political, and cultural contexts—early 17th century through Bach and Handel. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Music in the Classical Period
courses.description: Study of galant, Emfindsamer, and classical styles from c. 1730 to Boccherini, Haydn, and Mozart. Focus on instrumental and sacred music, and on opera before Mozart. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Music in the Romantic Era
courses.description: Virtuosic and lyric extremes in instrumental and vocal styles. Literary romanticism, descriptive music, and the Lied; opera in France and Italy; Wagner's music drama as Gesamtkunstwerk. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Music in the 20th Century
courses.description: Crisis of romanticism and tonality: transition of Debussy, Mahler, and others; new styles of Stravinsky, Schoenberg, Bartok; developments in the United States; implications of recent trends. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Graduate Seminar in Musicology: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced graduate seminars that revolve around a rotating selection of topics in musicology, emphasizing prominent research and approaches in the field.

courses.title: Seminar in Ethnomusicology: [Topic]
courses.description: Discussion-based seminar focused on history, methods, and emerging issues related to the discipline of ethnomusicology (the study of music in/as culture).

courses.title: Seminar: Music Theory: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced graduate seminars that revolve around a rotating selection of topics in music theory, emphasizing prominent research and approaches in the field.

courses.title: Campus Band: [Topic]
courses.description: Participation in Campus Band. No audition needed. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Campus Orchestra
courses.description: Participation in an orchestra. No audition needed. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Campus Chorus: [Topic]
courses.description: Participation in a choral ensemble. Gospel Choir, Gospel Singers, Gospel Ensemble. Audition required for Gospel Singers and Gospel Ensemble. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Historical Performance Practices I
courses.description: Introduction to theory and practice of sound production, rhetoric, pronunciation, instrumentation, pitch, temperament, and ornamentation in period vocal and instrumental solo and ensemble music, from the 12th through 16th centuries. Offered once every third year.

courses.title: Historical Performance Practices II
courses.description: Introduction to theory and practice of sound production, rhetoric, pronunciation, instrumentation, pitch, temperament, and ornamentation in period vocal and instrumental solo and ensemble music in the 17th and early 18th centuries. Offered once every third year.

courses.title: Historical Performance Practices III
courses.description: Introduction to theory and practice of sound production, rhetoric, pronunciation, instrumentation, pitch, temperament, and ornamentation in period vocal and instrumental solo and ensemble music in the late 18th and 19th centuries. Offered once every third year.

courses.title: Rhetoric and Music
courses.description: This class is designed as an in-depth study seminar of some important aspects of rhetoric as it applies to pre-World-War-One music.

courses.title: Musical Iconography
courses.description: In-depth study seminar of the interdisciplinary field that deals with visual representations (iconography) of all themes musical, from musical instruments, to musical notation, portraits of musicians, allegories, instruments as symbols and attributes, and concerts, from the late Middle Ages to the nineteenth century.

courses.title: Collegium Musicum
courses.description: Study of music repertoire of the medieval, Renaissance, and baroque periods through rehearsals and extensive sight-reading; vocal and instrumental repertoire. Ensemble fee.

courses.title: Oregon Electronic Device Orchestra
courses.description: Performance ensemble that uses data-driven musical instruments in combination with software and hardware to perform music and intermedia compositions.

courses.title: Chamber Ensemble: [Topic]
courses.description: Accompanying, Brass Choir, Brass Ensemble, Chamber Ensemble, Trombone Ensemble, Tuba and Euphonium Ensemble, Studio Guitar Ensemble, Jazz Guitar Ensemble, Latin Jazz Ensemble, Oregon Percussion Ensemble.

courses.description: Green Garter Band, Oregon Basketball Band, Oregon Marching Band, Oregon Wind Ensemble, UO Campus Band, UO Symphonic Band, Yellow Garter Band. Ensemble fee for Oregon Wind Ensemble, UO Symphonic Band, UO Campus Band.

courses.title: Business Analytics I
courses.description: Explores standard protocols for describing and modeling business information and processes; techniques for designing management information systems; criteria for analyzing firms' implementations of information technology. Students cannot receive credit for both OBA 311 and OBA 311H. Sophomore standing required.

courses.title: Business Analytics II
courses.description: Computer-aided business applications of hypothesis testing, simple linear regression. Introduction to multiple regression and nonparametric techniques. Blocked and completely randomized one- and two-factor experimental designs. Students cannot receive credit for both OBA 312 and OBA 312H . Sophomore standing required.

courses.title: Business Analytics II
courses.description: Computer-aided business applications of hypothesis testing, simple linear regression. Introduction to multiple regression and nonparametric techniques. Blocked and completely randomized one- and two-factor experimental designs. Students cannot receive credit for both OBA 312 and OBA 312H .

courses.title: Operations Management
courses.description: Concepts and applications of operations management. Use of information technology in operations. Topics include forecasting, quality, supply chain management, information systems in operations management, and planning and scheduling. Students cannot receive credit for both OBA 335 and OBA 335H. Sophomore standing required.

courses.title: Sports Analytics
courses.description: The course is intended for students interested in general management, operations, marketing or consulting in sports. The course leverages students’ core-course data management skills relating to spreadsheets and databases. Students will work on integrating those data management skills with statistics-based sports data-analytics methods, and thus be able to make rigorous data-driven decisions, as well as effectively communicate the rationale supporting those decisions.

courses.title: Introduction to Product Design
courses.description: This course is an introduction to the Product Design profession and its cultural relevance. Lectures, reading and projects convey theory (critical thinking), designers (history), design methods (CAD, drawing, building), and storytelling (documentation / presentation) to give a foundation in product innovation, creation, and portfolio generation.

courses.description: Focuses on perspective, line weight, construction with primary shapes, and shading in the creation of three-dimensional objects.

courses.title: Designers' Tools
courses.description: Fundamental construction methods for design. Develop and understanding material properties and the use of specific tools through the design, development and construction of two projects.

courses.title: Introduction to Design Studio
courses.description: Introduction to a studio based design course that combines theory and practice with a series of assignments and projects.

courses.title: Introduction to Design Studio II
courses.description: Integrate 2D and 3D communication and presentation skills to develop project-based design solutions.

courses.title: Design Drawing
courses.description: Introduces specific techniques in drawing and modeling objects and their spatial context; the demonstration and implementation of various media and types of drawing.

courses.title: Introduction to Computer Aided Design
courses.description: Introduction to computer-assisted design (CAD) in which students learn virtual design and physical manufacturing relationships and techniques.

courses.title: Design for Use
courses.description: Provides the basic theoretical underpinnings for considering the socio-cultural background and design of products. Lectures and readings present main issues; discussions complete conceptual principals.

courses.title: Objects and Impacts
courses.description: Explores how design influences and is influenced by materials and manufacturing processes. Lectures, readings, and discussions present sustainability, aesthetic, and functional aspects of product design.

courses.title: Design Process
courses.description: Introduces design processes, from theoretical to professional, using readings, guest lectures, and experimental new structures.

courses.description: Meshes virtual design and physical design as students work on projects using shop tools and computer-aided design and manufacturing software and equipment.

courses.title: Interaction Design
courses.description: This course will provide students with an understanding of the fundamentals of User Experience/User Interface design, including the design thinking process, interaction design, design principles, and prototyping. As part of this course, students will be immersed in user research, learning to identify and meet the needs of the users. Additionally, students will gain hands-on experience designing interfaces and prototyping through available digital design tool. After completing this course, students will be ready to create user-centric digital products with full understanding of User Experience/User Interface design

courses.title: Advanced Studio I
courses.description: Design studio focuses on personal questions that are explored through active design development. Questions may relate to issues of user interface, sustainability, or societal problems.

courses.title: Advanced Studio II
courses.description: Design studio focuses on global questions explored through active development. Questions may relate to issues of user interface, sustainability, or societal problems.

courses.title: Advanced Studio III
courses.description: Design studio focuses on corporate questions that are explored through active design development. Questions may relate to issues of user interface, sustainability, or societal problems.

courses.title: BFA Studio I
courses.description: Explores problems that stress design development through innovation and the responsibility to solve complex societal, functional, and aesthetic issues. Seminar component fosters theoretical, professional, and creative discussion.

courses.title: BFA Studio II
courses.description: Second course in series of interactive studios in which students engage in independent project-based learning. Sequence with PD 486 , PD 488 .

courses.title: BFA Studio III
courses.description: Third course in series of interactive studio in which students engage in independent project-based learning. Sequence with PD 486 , PD 487 .

courses.title: Philosophical Problems
courses.description: Introduction to philosophy based on classical and modern texts from Plato through the 21st century. Sample topics include free will, the mind-body problem, the existence of an external world.

courses.title: Ethics
courses.description: Study of moral theories and issues central to moral theory (such as justification of moral judgments and concepts of duty, goodness, and virtue) as well as theoretical engagement with pressing contemporary moral debates.

courses.title: Critical Reasoning
courses.description: Introduction to thinking and reasoning critically. How to recognize, analyze, criticize, and construct arguments.

courses.title: History of Western Philosophy
courses.description: The course is an introduction to some currents, seminal thinkers, and texts of the Western philosophical tradition from the Ancient Greeks to Medieval, Modern, and 19th and 20th Century Philosophy. The course includes both classical text and readings traditionally excluded from the canon.

courses.title: Human Nature
courses.description: Consideration of various physiological, cultural, psychological, and personal forces that characterize human beings, taking into account issues of class, gender, race, and sexual orientation.

courses.title: Ethics of Enterprise and Exchange
courses.description: Moral examination of business by considering the nature of enterprise and exchange. Topics include corporate and consumer responsibility, meaningful work, and leadership.

courses.title: Internet, Society, and Philosophy
courses.description: Introduction to philosophical problems of the Internet. Primary focus on social, political, and ethical issues with discussion of epistemological and metaphysical topics.

courses.title: Philosophy and Popular Culture
courses.description: Engages in critical philosophical reflection about and through popular culture, including movies, music, graphic novels, and sports.

courses.title: Ethics in the Life Sciences
courses.description: Focused on complex ethical issues in the life sciences, ranging from debates over human enhancement, the use of human cells in research, non-human animals in research, synthetic biology, genetically-modified organisms, and recent research in microbial biology.

courses.title: Love and Sex
courses.description: Philosophical study of love, relationships, marriage, sex, sexuality, sexual identity, and sexual representation.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Existentialism
courses.description: Basic ideas of the Christian and atheistic divisions of the existentialist movement; some attention to the philosophical situation that generated the existentialist rebellion.

courses.title: Philosophy and Cultural Diversity
courses.description: Philosophical investigation of the implications of cultural diversity for identity, knowledge, and community, from the perspectives of several American cultures.

courses.title: Sports Ethics
courses.description: Introduction to the nature of sport within the framework of the philosophical study of ethics and its implication for the athlete and spectator.

courses.title: Food Ethics
courses.description: Examination of a variety of issues relating to food production and consumption in light of virtue, utilitarian, deontological, pragmatist, and care ethics.

courses.title: Data Ethics
courses.description: This course explores central ethical challenges in data science and related fields of computational analysis. Offers both an overview of the major theoretical commitments of data ethics as well as engagement with applied contexts such as computer engineering, behavioral sciences, marketing, and surveillance.

courses.title: Introduction to Formal Logic
courses.description: Introduces formal logic, including both propositional (sentence) and predicate logic, including the use of truth trees.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Social and Political Philosophy I
courses.description: Survey of major political theorists (historical or contemporary) as well as central concepts of political philosophy such as justice, rights, liberalism, democracy, and equality.

courses.title: Social and Political Philosophy: [Topic]
courses.description: Focused study of particular theorists and contemporary debates central to the fields of social and political philosophy.

courses.title: Global Justice
courses.description: Introduction to philosophical problems of globalization and justice related to global poverty, citizenship, human rights, and issues of identity, multiculturalism, war,terrorism, environmentalism and health care.

courses.title: History of Ancient Philosophy
courses.description: Focuses primarily on Plato and Aristotle. Examines their roots in pre-Socratic philosophy and considers their relation to the Greek political and poetic traditions.

courses.title: History of Philosophy: Modern
courses.description: Survey of European philosophy through Hume, including the work of Descartes, Locke, and Spinoza.

courses.title: History of Philosophy: 19th Century
courses.description: Traces Kant's influence on such philosophers as Hegel, Nietzsche, and Marx.

courses.title: Introduction to Feminist Philosophy
courses.description: Introduces basic questions of philosophy through topics central to feminism.

courses.title: Philosophy of Religion
courses.description: Philosophical investigation of the nature of "religion" (e.g., the nature of the sacred, spirituality, and transcendence).

courses.title: Philosophy of the Arts
courses.description: Survey of classical and contemporary theories of art and aesthetic experience, with examples from various arts.

courses.title: Moral Theory
courses.description: Study of the most important traditional ethical theories; modern philosophical analysis of moral terms and statements.

courses.title: Philosophy in Literature
courses.description: Selective study of major philosophical ideas and attitudes expressed in the literature of Europe and America.

courses.title: Philosophy of Film
courses.description: Explores questions about the aesthetic dimensions of film, its relation to the other arts, and the treatment of philosophical questions in films.

courses.title: Medical Ethics
courses.description: Introduces theoretical tools and concrete case studies for formulating, analyzing, and evaluating ethical judgments raised by contemporary biomedical practice.

courses.title: Introduction to Philosophy of Science
courses.description: Examines theories of scientific practice, rationality, objectivity, values in science, and the role of science in society.

courses.title: Environmental Philosophy
courses.description: Considers the nature and morality of human relationships with the environment (e.g., the nature of value, the moral standing of nonhuman life).

courses.title: African Philosophy
courses.description: Survey of contemporary African philosophy with a focus on current debates (for example, critique of ethnophilosophy; relation between orality and writing; decolonization of knowledge).

courses.title: Introduction to Latin American Philosophy
courses.description: History of Latin American philosophy through the study of ideas, issues, problems, and forms of thinking in the work of key periods, movements, and authors.

courses.title: Critical Theory
courses.description: Examines the methodological, epistemological, moral, and political dimensions of critical theory. Prereq: one philosophy course. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Introduction to Philosophy of Law
courses.description: Introduces central problems in the law; examines the nature of legal reasoning.

courses.title: Place in the Cosmos
courses.description: Explores the relation between humans and the cosmos as a matter of place by comparing seminal texts in the history of philosophy. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Metaphysics
courses.description: Traditional issues in metaphysics selected from among such topics as substance, existence, time, causation, God, the nature of individuals, and the meaningfulness of metaphysics.

courses.title: Philosophy of Language

courses.description: This course covers classical and non-classical logics. A review of propositional and predicate logic will be followed by a consideration of “non-normal” logics of strict implication, conditional logics, many-valued logics, and first degree entailment. The course will consider philosophical issues raised by these diverse logics.

courses.description: Introduction to the wave nature of sound; hearing; musical instruments and scales; auditorium acoustics; and the transmission, storage, and reproduction of sound.

courses.title: Physics of Light, Color, and Vision
courses.description: Light and color, their nature, how they are produced, and how they are perceived and interpreted.

courses.title: Physics Behind the Internet
courses.description: How discoveries in 20th-century physics mesh to drive modern telecommunications. Topics include electron mobility in matter, the development of transistors and semiconductors, lasers, and optical fibers.

courses.title: Scientific Revolutions
courses.description: Surveys several major revolutions in our views of the natural and technological world, focusing on scientific concepts and methodological aspects. For nonscience majors. Multilisted with ERTH 156M .

courses.title: Physics of Energy and Environment
courses.description: Practical study of energy generation and environmental impact, including energy fundamentals, fossil fuel use, global warming, nuclear energy, and energy conservation.

courses.title: Solar and Other Renewable Energies
courses.description: Topics include photovoltaic cells, solar thermal power, passive solar heating, energy storage, geothermal energy, and wind energy.

courses.title: The Physics of Life
courses.description: Explores how physical laws guide the structure, function, and behavior of living organisms, and examines the physical properties of biological materials. Topics span microscropic and macroscopic scales.

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics for Everyone
courses.description: Introduction to quantum mechanics, a set of sometimes counterintuitive scientific principals describing atoms and light, along with the modern technologies it makes possible.

courses.title: Foundations of Physics I
courses.description: Newtonian mechanics; units and vectors; one-dimensional motion; Newton’s laws; work and energy; momentum and collisions. Sequence.

courses.title: Foundations of Physics I
courses.description: Vibrations and waves; oscillations; wave mechanics; dispersion; modes; introductory optics.

courses.title: Foundations of Physics I
courses.description: Electricity and magnetism; charge and electric field; electric potential; circuits; magnetic field; inductance.

courses.title: Foundations of Physics Laboratory
courses.description: Repeatable. Introduction to laboratory measurements, reports, instrumentation, and experimental techniques.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Foundations of Physics II
courses.description: Introduction to relativity and quantum physics with applications to atomic, solid-state, nuclear, and astro-particle systems

courses.title: Thermal Physics and Statistical Mechanics I
courses.description: Thermodynamic systems; first and second laws; kinetic theory of gases; entropy. Sequence.

courses.title: Thermal Physics and Statistical Mechanics II
courses.description: Thermal radiation; Maxell-Boltzmann statistics; Fermi and Bose gases; phase transitions. Sequence.

courses.title: Mathematical Methods
courses.description: Essential mathematical background for 400-level physics courses in classical mechanics, electricity and magnetism, and quantum mechanics. The emphasis is on the conceptual basis of the following topics and their connections, with an emphasis on physical applications, vector algebra and calculus. Dirac delta function, partial differential equations, linear algebra of function spaces, orthonormal bases, Dirac notation, special functions, standard and general Fourier analysis, matrices, eigenvalue equations, Fourier transforms.

courses.title: Physics Experimentation Data Analysis Laboratory
courses.description: Practical aspects of physics experimentation, including data acquisition, statistical analysis, and introduction to scientific programming, and use of Fourier methods for data analysis.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Supervised Tutoring
courses.description: A student, under faculty supervision, accepts responsibility for tutoring other students within the discipline.

courses.title: Mechanics
courses.description: Fundamental principles of Newtonian mechanics, conservation laws, small oscillations, planetary motion, systems of particles. Electromagnetic phenomena. Only nonmajors may earn graduate credit.

courses.title: Electricity and Magnetism I
courses.description: Fundamental principles of Newtonian mechanics, conservation laws, small oscillations, planetary motion, systems of particles. Electromagnetic phenomena.

courses.title: Electricity and Magnetism II
courses.description: Fundamental principles of Newtonian mechanics, conservation laws, small oscillations, planetary motion, systems of particles. Electromagnetic phenomena.

courses.title: Quantum Physics
courses.description: Planck's and de Broglie's postulates, the uncertainty principle, Bohr's model of the atom, the Schroedinger equation in one dimension, the harmonic oscillator, the hydrogen atom, molecules and solids, nuclei and elementary particles. Sequence.

courses.title: Quantum Physics
courses.description: Planck's and de Broglie's postulates, the uncertainty principle, Bohr's model of the atom, the Schroedinger equation in one dimension, the harmonic oscillator, the hydrogen atom, molecules and solids, nuclei and elementary particles. Sequence.

courses.title: Topics in Quantum Physics
courses.description: Perturbation theory, variational principle, time-dependent perturbation theory, elementary scattering theory.

courses.title: Partial Differential Equations: Fourier Analysis I
courses.description: Introduction to PDEs with a view towards applications in physics. Wave and heat equations, classical Fourier series on the circle, Bessel and Legendre series. Multilisted with MATH 421M .

courses.title: Electromagnetism
courses.description: Study of electromagnetic waves. Topics include Maxwell's equations, wave equation, plane waves, guided waves, antennas, and other related phenomena.

courses.title: Introduction to Space Physics
courses.description: Course explores the interaction of the solar wind with the Earth's magnetosphere using fundamental plasma physics supported and motivated by spacecraft observations. Students will gain an understanding of the physics governing the interaction building from single particle plasma motion to specific observation supported examples.

courses.title: Classical Optics
courses.description: Geometrical optics, polarization, interference, Frauenhofer and Fresnel diffraction.

courses.title: Modern Optics
courses.description: Special topics in modern applied optics such as Fourier optics, coherence theory, resonators and lasers, holography, and image processing.

courses.title: Analog Electronics
courses.description: Passive and active discrete components and circuits. General circuit concepts and theorems. Equivalent circuits and black box models. Integrated circuit operational amplifiers.

courses.title: Digital Electronics
courses.description: Digital electronics including digital logic, measurement, signal processing and control. Introduction to computer interfacing.

courses.title: Radiofrequency and Low-noise Measurements
courses.description: This course provides a hands-on introduction to radio-frequency technology, essential in communications, physics labs, and quantum computing. Students will learn radio-frequency device design, test equipment, and quantum measurement techniques through lectures and structured labs. In the second half, they will set up and program advanced radio-frequency test instruments used in quantum research, performing real-world measurements.

courses.title: Introduction to Biological Physics
courses.description: The application of physical principles to the study of the living world, especially at molecular and cellular scales. Topics include the mechanical properties of biomaterials, the sensory abilities of cells and organisms, and the dynamical properties of information processing networks.

courses.title: Computational Physics
courses.description: The goal of this course is to build a strong foundation in computational physics. With a particular emphasis on topics in modeling and data analysis for drawing physical insights from data in physics and astronomy, we will cover tools and techniques useful for conducting computationally demanding science.

courses.title: Design of Experiments
courses.description: Applies statistics to practical data analysis, data-based decision making, model building, and the design of experiments. Emphasizes factorial designs.

courses.title: General Relativity I
courses.description: This course will serve as an introduction to the concept of gravity as geometry. The course begins by motivating the need for General Relativity and culminate in the presentation of the Einstein equation, presenting some of the simplest solutions to the Einstein equation.

courses.title: The Physics Behind Quantum Computers
courses.description: Quantum computing is a new way of computing based on the rules of Quantum mechanics. We study what sort of computations can be performed by atoms, ions, and superconducting electric circuits.

courses.title: Research Project I
courses.description: For physics and other science majors, Physics Projects entails construction and use of apparatus, interfaces and computers to perform technically-sophisticated experiments, analyze and communicate results.

courses.title: Research Project II
courses.description: For physics and other science majors, Physics Projects entails construction and use of apparatus, interfaces and computers to perform technically-sophisticated experiments, analyze and communicate results.

courses.title: Research Project III
courses.description: For physics and other science majors, Physics Projects entails construction and use of apparatus, interfaces and computers to perform technically-sophisticated experiments, analyze and communicate results.

courses.title: Nanofabrication
courses.description: This course introduces students to the design and fabrication of micro- and nanoscale devices. The course will cover fundamental fabrication principles, including optical and electron-beam lithography, thin-film deposition, etching, and characterization. This framework will aid in understanding the processes used in manufacturing chip-based devices in semiconductor electronics, photonics, micro/nano-electromechanical systems, microwave electronics, microfluidics, and superconducting quantum circuits. In the hands-on lab component of this course, students will learn build, optimize and better understand these systems and fabrication processes through the statistical design and analysis of experiments (i.e. Design of Experiments, DOE).
