courses.title: Theoretical Mechanics
courses.description: Lagrangian and Hamiltonian mechanics, small oscillations, rigid bodies. Sequence.

courses.title: Theoretical Mechanics
courses.description: Lagrangian and Hamiltonian mechanics, small oscillations, rigid bodies. Sequence.

courses.title: Statistical Physics
courses.description: Thermodynamics, statistical mechanics, kinetic theory, application to gases, liquids, solids, atoms, molecules, and the structure of matter. Sequence.

courses.title: Statistical Physics
courses.description: Thermodynamics, statistical mechanics, kinetic theory, application to gases, liquids, solids, atoms, molecules, and the structure of matter. Sequence.

courses.title: Electromagnetic Theory
courses.description: Microscopic form of Maxwell's equations, derivation and solution of the wave equation, Lorentz covariant formulation, motion of charges in given fields, propagation and diffraction, radiation by given sources, coupled motion of sources and fields, the electromagnetic field in dense media.

courses.title: Electromagnetic Theory
courses.description: Microscopic form of Maxwell's equations, derivation and solution of the wave equation, Lorentz covariant formulation, motion of charges in given fields, propagation and diffraction, radiation by given sources, coupled motion of sources and fields, the electromagnetic field in dense media. Sequence.

courses.title: Physical Optics with Labs
courses.description: Fundamentals of applied geometric and wave optics theory, reinforced through homework assignments, and explored in experiments conducted with lasers and optical components. Sequence with PHYS 627 , PHYS 628.

courses.title: Optical Materials and Devices
courses.description: Principles of quantum mechanics and solid-state physics relating to material properties of optoelectronic devices with corresponding laboratories teaching how to operate and characterize these devices. Sequence with PHYS 626 , PHYS 628.

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics
courses.description: Review of fundamentals, central force problems, matrix mechanics. Sequence.

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics
courses.description: Approximation methods, scattering. Sequence.

courses.title: Quantum Mechanics
courses.description: Rotation symmetry, spin, identical particles. Sequence.

courses.title: Particle Physics I
courses.description: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: fundamentals; symmetries; quantum electrodynamics; R; quarks and leptons; chirality; flavor symmetry; mesons; baryons; form factors; deep inelastic scattering. Sequence.

courses.title: Particle Physics II
courses.description: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: quantum chromodynamics; parton distribution functions; hadron-hadron collisions; particle interactions in matter; collider detectors; experimental methodologies to analyze data; statistical thresholds and significance. Sequence with PHYS 661 , PHYS 663 .

courses.title: Particle Physics III
courses.description: Theory, phenomenology, and experimental basis of the standard model of particle physics: electroweak symmetry breaking; CKM mixing; Higgs couplings; early universe cosmology; Friedmann expansion; entropy; freeze-out; impact of neutrinos on cosmology; dark matter evidence and candidates. Sequence with PHYS 661 , PHYS 662 .

courses.title: Quantum Field Theory
courses.description: Canonical quantization, path integral formulation of quantum field theory, Feynman rules for perturbation theory, quantum electrodynamics, renormalization, gauge theory of the strong and electroweak interactions. Sequence with PHYS 665 , PHYS 666 .

courses.title: Quantum Field Theory II
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to apply the methodology established in QFT I to theories of charged fermions coupled to a photon. Then we will begin to explore QFT beyond leading order. Sequence with PHYSS 664, PHYS 666 .

courses.title: Quantum Field Theory III
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to understand QFT at loop level, and to extend the formalism to non-Abelian gauge bosons. In addition, we will cover a variety of special topics. This course is designed to be the last quarter of a full year sequence. Sequence with PHYS 664 , PHYS 665 .

courses.title: Solid State Physics
courses.description: Crystallography; thermal, electrical, optical, and magnetic properties of solids; band theory; metals, semiconductors, and insulators; defects in solids. Sequence.

courses.title: Solid State Physics
courses.description: Crystallography; thermal, electrical, optical, and magnetic properties of solids; band theory; metals, semiconductors, and insulators; defects in solids. Sequence.

courses.title: Theory of Condensed Matter
courses.description: Advanced topics include quantum and statistical description of many-particle systems, electronic structure, elementary excitations in solids and fluids, critical phenomena, statics and dynamics of soft condensed matter. Topics and emphasis vary.

courses.title: Semiconductor Device Physics
courses.description: Introduction to the theory behind semiconductors. Elementary theory of inorganic solids; electronic structures and transport properties. Basic theory of devices including diodes, transistors, mosfets, and optoelectronic devices. Offered only in summer. Sequence with PHYS 678M , PHYS 679M . Multilisted with CH 677M .

courses.title: Semiconductor Processing and Characterization Technology
courses.description: Introduction to the techniques required to make semiconductors and test their properties. Solid-state and surface chemistry of inorganic semiconductors as it pertains to microelectronic devices. Offered only in summer. Multilisted with CH 678M . Sequence with PHYS 677M , PHYS 679M .

courses.title: Device Processing and Characterization Laboratory
courses.description: Students use theory and techniques learned to design, fabricate, and test a device that performs a specific function, with an emphasis on wafer processing and device realization. Offered only in summer. Sequence with PHYS 677M , PHYS 678M . Multilisted with CH 679M .

courses.title: Cryogenic and Quantum Measurements
courses.description: This course introduces cryogenic and quantum measurements, emphasizing ultra-low-temperature physics and superconducting qubit characterization. Students gain hands-on experience with dilution cryostats, learning their operation, control, and measurement hardware. Through practical lab sessions, they conduct experiments on superconducting qubits, bridging theory with real-world quantum computing applications. Ideal for those in quantum technologies, condensed matter physics, or cryogenic engineering. Concurrent registration in PHYS 682 and PHYS 691 required.

courses.title: Optical Quantum Lab
courses.description: This hands-on laboratory course trains students in optical physics and experimental techniques central to quantum information science. Covering optical alignment, fiber optics, and acousto-optic modulation, students build a confocal microscope to image single spin qubits, such as atomic defects in diamond. They implement quantum state preparation, optical spin readout, single-qubit gates, and antibunching measurements. Advanced topics include Ramsey interferometry to study qubit dynamics. The course equips students with practical skills for careers in quantum engineering, photonics, and computing, providing a strong foundation for research or industry roles in the rapidly advancing quantum technology sector.

courses.title: Quantum Optics and Laser Physics
courses.description: Nonlinear optical processes and quantum statistical properties of light produced by such processes, laser theory, wave mixing processes, optical Bloch equations, field quantization, photon statistics, cooperative emissions. Sequence.

courses.title: Quantum Optics and Laser Physics
courses.description: Nonlinear optical processes and quantum statistical properties of light produced by such processes, laser theory, wave mixing processes, optical Bloch equations, field quantization, photon statistics, cooperative emissions. Sequence.

courses.title: Quantum Optics and Laser Physics
courses.description: Nonlinear optical processes and quantum statistical properties of light produced by such processes, laser theory, wave mixing processes, optical Bloch equations, field quantization, photon statistics, cooperative emissions. Sequence.

courses.title: Industry Projects in Quantum and Nanotechnology
courses.description: This hands-on course trains students in designing, building, and testing integrated quantum and nanotechnology devices. Working in teams, students take projects from concept to completion, developing plug-and-play systems through design and fabrication. Projects involve nanofabricating mechanical, photonic, electronic, or superconducting micro- and nanodevices using advanced tools like e-beam lithography, scanning electron microscopy, and atomic force microscopy. Students gain skills in technical communication, design, fabrication, measurement, simulation, analysis, and industry collaboration. By course end, they will have built a functional system, developed industry-relevant expertise, and gained experience in teamwork, innovation, and advanced nanofabrication techniques.

courses.title: First-Year Portuguese
courses.description: Introduction to Brazilian Portuguese language and culture, with emphasis on speaking, reading, writing, and listening comprehension skills. Sequence with PORT 102 , PORT 103 .

courses.title: First-Year Portuguese
courses.description: Introduction to Brazilian Portuguese language and culture, with emphasis on speaking, reading, writing, and listening comprehension skills. Sequence with PORT 101 , PORT 103 .

courses.title: First Year Portuguese
courses.description: Introduction to Brazilian Portuguese language and culture, with emphasis on speaking, reading, writing and listening comprehension skills. Sequence with PORT 101 , PORT 102 .

courses.title: Second Year Portuguese
courses.description: Development of Brazilian Portuguese speaking, reading, writing and comprehension; study of short literary and cultural materials. Sequence with PORT 202 , PORT 203 .

courses.title: Second-Year Portuguese
courses.description: Development of Brazilian Portuguese speaking, reading, writing, and comprehension; study of short literary and cultural materials. Sequence with PORT 201 , PORT 203 .

courses.title: Second-Year Portuguese
courses.description: Development of Brazilian Portuguese speaking, reading, writing, and comprehension; study of short literary and cultural materials. Sequence with PORT 201 , PORT 202 .

courses.title: Cultura e Lingua: Expressoes Artisticas
courses.description: Develops advanced language skills through the study of key representations in Brazilian art, literature, film, and music. Taught in Portuguese. Prereq: PORT 203 or equivalent. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Cultura e lingua: Brasil ontem e hoje
courses.description: Introduction to Brazilian history and culture. Topics include geography; indigenous, European, and African presence; trends of authoritarianism and democratization; issues in migration and urbanization; racial and ethnic identities; women’s roles. Taught in Portuguese.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]

courses.title: Advocacy and Social Change
courses.description: Explorations of how policy change occurs, how people and organizations create social and policy change, and how individuals can make a difference in their communities.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Introduction to Public Policy
courses.description: Overview of professional public service and the planning and management of public issues. Focuses on the goals of public services within their economic, social, and political contexts.

courses.title: Healthy Communities
courses.description: Historical relationships of public policy, planning, and public health; how public policies can promote health; relationship of planning and policies to inequalities in health outcomes.

courses.title: Introduction to City Planning
courses.description: Introduction to planning, using urban issues as lenses to explore transportation, housing, environment, and social equity as critical elements shaping where and how people live.

courses.title: Global Sustainable Cities and Landscapes
courses.description: The course focuses on Sustainable Cities and Landscapes (SCL), a key approach to achieving the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)—internationally recognized benchmarks for advancing global sustainability. It explores urbanization, with a particular emphasis on the Non-US Pacific Rim, and examines practices for creating sustainable cities worldwide. Topics include addressing the impacts of climate change on vulnerable communities, transforming water and energy systems, promoting biodiversity, enhancing placemaking, and fostering rural-urban co-development. Through hybrid teaching and methods such as case studies and peer learning, the course builds competencies in systems thinking, strategic action, collaboration, and integrated problem-solving, equipping students to critically evaluate sustainability.

courses.title: Arts and Human Values
courses.description: Addresses fundamental aesthetic theory and practice questions resulting from viewing art as a powerful communicator of social and cultural values. Values, rights, and responsibilities of the contemporary visual environment. Institutional structures advancing the arts and culture in society will be examined.

courses.title: Introduction to the Nonprofit Sector
courses.description: Overview of the nonprofit sector includes its origin, growth, oversight, and varied elements. Examines theory and research into the effectiveness of nonprofit strategies and structures.

courses.title: Inclusive Urbanism
courses.description: Investigates the relationship between social-economic inclusion and the physical form of cities.

courses.title: Community Leadership and Change
courses.description: Explores sustainable change at the community level by examining local systems and institutions: transportation, social influences, environment, housing, and the economy.

courses.title: Environmental Management
courses.description: Introduction to environmental management. Focuses on solutions to problems in managing population, pollution, and resources.

courses.title: Urban Transportation
courses.description: Presents a survey of transportation planning issues. Assignments use Oregon as a transportation laboratory and challenge students to synthesize planning and policy recommendations.

courses.title: Climate Change Policy
courses.description: Overview of climate-change policy; topics include cap and trade, carbon tax, fuel efficiency standards, biofuel standards, and renewable portfolio standards.

courses.title: Visual Design for Applied Practice
courses.description: Fundamentals of visual communications for the public and nonprofit organizations. Professional practice in creating effective visual media, and conveying qualitative and quantitative data.

courses.title: International Public Policy
courses.description: Introduces international dimensions of public policy making by comparing national systems and institutions of governance, public policy making processes, and public policy decisions. Investigates concepts, theories, and approaches to compare public policies in economic, welfare, educational, health, and environmental sectors.

courses.title: Global Sustainable Development and Policy
courses.description: Sustainable Development seeks to reconcile the economic, social, and environmental problems we face in a world with constrained resources. To have a broader perspective of what sustainability means, we examine problems through an interdisciplinary lens and investigate how these issues form and possible policy solutions.

courses.title: Internship and Professional Development
courses.description: Introduction to department’s internship program and career development. Overview of professionalism and effective communication, resume and cover letter writing, and interviewing and networking skills.

courses.title: Quantitative Methods
courses.description: Introduction to the use of quantitative techniques to answer planning, public policy and management related questions.

courses.title: Policy and Planning Analysis
courses.description: Applied problem-solving in the public policy and planning process. Examines the theoretical and methodological underpinnings of policy and planning analysis.

courses.title: Introduction to Public Law
courses.description: Administrative law, including introduction to legal research, for public administrators. Administrative procedures, implementation of policy through administrative law, judicial review, and practical applications in public agencies.

courses.title: Grant Proposal Writing
courses.description: Introduction to the process of preparing grant applications and material for funded research.

courses.title: Project Management
courses.description: Application of specific techniques that lead to projects being completed on time, within budget, and with appropriate quality.

courses.title: Strategic Planning for Management
courses.description: Process of strategic planning for communities, government agencies, and nonprofit organizations.

courses.title: Public Health Policy
courses.description: This course explores public health policies and how they intersect with social and environmental justice, including topics on cultural competency, natural disasters, criminal justice, and international cooperation.

courses.title: Urban Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Introduction to geographic information systems in areas of environmental, demographic, suitability, and transportation-related research.

courses.title: Transportation Issues in Planning: [Topic]
courses.description: Introduction to the social implications of various transportation-related policies and practices.

courses.title: Transportation Policy
courses.description: Provides a foundation in transportation policy and covers topics related local, state, and federal transportation policy. Course assignments challenge students to synthesize policy recommendations.

courses.title: Land-Use Planning and Policy
courses.description: Land use planning processes and policy tools that US governments employ to manage and control land development, especially those considered to support a community's efforts to pursue sustainable urbanization.

courses.title: Sustainable Urban Development
courses.description: Introduces issues revolving around cities as the nexus for environmental challenges, including land-use planning, transportation planning, community and neighborhood design, and green buildings.

courses.title: Natural Resource Policy
courses.description: Aspects of population and resource systems. Poses questions about population trends, policy, and optimum size; analyzes methods for determining resource availability and flows.

courses.title: Environmental Policy
courses.description: Overview of policies related to the environment: examines the design and effectiveness of specific policies.

courses.title: Green Cities
courses.description: Examines the history and future of the interface between urban growth and environmental concerns, and the technological, social, and political forces that continue to shape it.

courses.title: Socioeconomic Development Planning
courses.description: Planning for responsible economic and social development. Policy problems and issues in providing a stable economic base and social and economic well-being while avoiding environmental degradation.

courses.title: Collaboration
courses.description: Explores theory and practice of collaboration in public and nonprofit settings, including the role of organizations, agencies, and the public. Focuses in particular on developing and practicing collaboration skills and strategies.

courses.title: Public Participation in Diverse Communities
courses.description: Review of community engagement strategies and tools for encouraging public participation in low income and ethnically diverse communities.

courses.title: Nonprofit and Government Communications
courses.description: This course is designed to help students understand and learn the tools to implement intentional strategic communications that propel an organization forward in its quest to achieve its mission.

courses.title: Health Policy
courses.description: Introduction to the key health policy issues of access, cost, quality, and racial and ethnic disparities.

courses.title: Program Evaluation
courses.description: Introduction to the design and implementation of program evaluations.

courses.title: Lobbying and the Legislative Process
courses.description: This course introduces students to the legislative process and the role of lobbyists in legislative advocacy and policymaking.

courses.title: The Arts in Society
courses.description: Course examines the arts as they function in society. Anthropological, philosophical, sociological, and art educational orientations to art are examined. Implications for arts and cultural management are addressed.

courses.title: Cultural Policy
courses.description: Cultural Policy investigates political choice processes, governmental institutions, and the public policy cycle that shape the arts and culture sector.

courses.title: Creative Placemaking
courses.description: This course provides an overview of the relationship between the arts and community development.

courses.title: Cultural Programming
courses.description: Explore theory and practice related to arts and cultural programming in the public sector. Emphasis on inclusive and equitable cultural programming.

courses.title: Nonprofit Management
courses.description: How to manage nonprofit organizations for superior performance in a humane, responsive, and responsible manner. Distinctive characteristics of nonprofit organizations.

courses.title: Fundraising for Nonprofit Organizations
courses.description: Introduction to fundraising for nonprofit organizations. Annual giving, major gifts, planned giving, and campaigns.

courses.title: Volunteer Resource Management
courses.description: This course provides an overview of the role of volunteers in community organizations and serves as a comprehensive introduction to effective practices in volunteer resource management.

courses.title: Public and Nonprofit Financial Management
courses.description: Introduction to financial management for public agencies and nonprofit organizations. Topics include budget processes, financial statements, financial resource management (taxes, donations, grants), expenditure systems, and capital project analysis.

courses.title: Philanthropy and Grant Making
courses.description: History, economics, and practice of philanthropy and grant making in the United States. Students study philanthropy from a multidisciplinary perspective and finish the quarter by awarding a $15,000 grant to a nonprofit organization of their choice.

courses.title: Impact Philanthropy
courses.description: Introduction to impact philanthropy and related topics. Evidence-based philanthropy, venture philanthropy, impact investing, social impact bonds, impact measurement, and policy environment.

courses.title: Nonprofit Legal Issues
courses.description: This course introduces legal issues for administration of nonprofit organizations. Topics include formation and types of organizations, board of directors duties, regulation of nonprofits, risk management, employee and volunteer law, lobbying, charitable solicitation, and laws on discrimination and accommodation.

courses.title: Practice of Leadership and Change
courses.description: Examines the principles and practices of leadership and change in communities and organizations through discussions with community leaders and personal reflection.

courses.title: Advanced Urban Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Acquiring advanced skills of using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for community mapping and spatial analysis.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Trial courses are taught under these numbers. See the online class schedule for current titles.

courses.title: Introduction to Public Law
courses.description: Administrative law, including introduction to legal research, for public administrators. Administrative procedures, implementation of policy through administrative law, judicial review, and practical applications in public agencies.

courses.title: Grant Proposal Writing
courses.description: Introduction to the process of preparing grant applications and material for funded research.

courses.title: Project Management
courses.description: Application of specific techniques that lead to projects being completed on time, within budget, and with appropriate quality.

courses.title: Strategic Planning for Management
courses.description: Process of strategic planning for communities, government agencies, and nonprofit organizations.

courses.title: Public Health Policy
courses.description: This course explores public health policies and how they intersect with social and environmental justice, including topics on cultural competency, natural disasters, criminal justice, and international cooperation.

courses.title: Urban Geographic Information Systems
courses.description: Introduction to geographic information systems in areas of environmental, demographic, suitability, and transportation-related research.

courses.title: Transportation Issues in Planning: [Topic]
courses.description: Introduction to the social implications of various transportation-related policies and practices.

courses.title: Transportation Policy
courses.description: Provides a foundation in transportation policy and covers topics related local, state, and federal transportation policy. Course assignments challenge students to synthesize policy recommendations.

courses.title: Sustainable Urban Development
courses.description: Introduces issues evolving around cities as the nexus for environmental challenges, including land-use planning, transportation planning, community and neighborhood design, and green buildings.

courses.title: Natural Resource Policy
courses.description: Aspects of population and resource systems. Poses questions about population trends, policy, and optimum size; analyzes methods for determining resource availability and flows.

courses.title: Environmental Policy
courses.description: Overview of policies related to the environment: examines the design and effectiveness of specific policies.

courses.title: Socioeconomic Development Planning
courses.description: Planning for responsible economic and social development. Policy problems and issues in providing a stable economic base and social and economic well-being while avoiding environmental degradation.

courses.title: Collaboration
courses.description: Explores theory and practice of collaboration in public and nonprofit settings, including the role of organizations, agencies, and the public. Focuses in particular on developing and practicing collaboration skills and strategies.

courses.title: Public Participation in Diverse Communities
courses.description: Review of community engagement strategies and tools for encouraging public participation in low income and ethnically diverse communities.

courses.title: Nonprofit and Government Communications
courses.description: This course is designed to help students understand and learn the tools to implement intentional strategic communications that propel an organization forward in its quest to achieve its mission.

courses.title: Health Policy

courses.title: Lobbying and the Legislative Process
courses.description: This course introduces students to the legislative process and the role of lobbyists in legislative advocacy and policymaking.

courses.description: Course examines the arts as they function in society. Anthropological, philosophical, sociological, and art educational orientations to art are examined. Implications for arts and cultural management are addressed.

courses.title: Cultural Policy
courses.description: Cultural Policy investigates political choice processes, governmental institutions, and the public policy cycle that shape the arts and culture sector.

courses.description: This course provides an overview of the relationship between the arts and community development.

courses.title: Cultural Programming
courses.description: Explore theory and practice related to arts and cultural programming in the public sector. Emphasis on inclusive and equitable cultural programming.

courses.description: Introduction to fundraising for nonprofit organizations. Annual giving, major gifts, planned giving, and campaigns.

courses.title: Volunteer Resource Management
courses.description: This course provides an overview of the role of volunteers in community organizations and serves as a comprehensive introduction to effective practices in volunteer resource management.

courses.description: History, economics, and practice of philanthropy and grant making in the United States. Students study philanthropy from a multidisciplinary perspective and finish the quarter by awarding a $15,000 grant to a nonprofit organization of their choice.

courses.title: Impact Philanthropy
courses.description: Introduction to impact philanthropy and related topics. Evidence-based philanthropy, venture philanthropy, impact investing, social impact bonds, impact measurement, and policy environment.

courses.description: This course introduces legal issues for administration of nonprofit organizations. Topics include formation and types of organizations, board of directors duties, regulation of nonprofits, risk management, employee and volunteer law, lobbying, charitable solicitation, and laws on discrimination and accommodation.

courses.description: Acquiring advanced skills of using Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for community mapping and spatial analysis.

courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Dissertation
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Supervised work experience that offers students opportunities to explore and clarify career goals, apply academic learning, enhance and learn new skills, gain experience, and network with professionals.

courses.title: Special Problems: [Topic]
courses.description: Specialized inquiry into a particular topic by a student and discussed with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: A recent topic is Community Planning.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Introduction to Planning Practice
courses.description: Explores the concepts and functions of the planning process as they relate to the social, economic, political, and environmental aspects of communities and regions.

courses.title: Legal Issues in Planning
courses.description: Federal and state legal relationships, the role of the courts in reviewing public sector decision-making, sources of the law, issues in land-use regulation, and basic legal research skills.

courses.title: Planning Analysis I
courses.description: Data sources and methods of data collection including surveys; descriptive and multivariate analysis; computer applications; selected analytic models, population projections, cost-benefit analysis.

courses.title: Planning Theory and Ethics
courses.description: Logic of the planning process; the relationship of planning to the political process and to rational decision making in governance.

courses.title: Human Settlements
courses.description: Historical development of cities and the ways in which city and regional contexts influence economic, social, and political processes.

courses.title: Public Sector Theory
courses.description: Overview of the core concepts, theories, and practices that provide the foundation for the field of public policy and management.

courses.title: Planning and Management Research Skills
courses.description: Surveys research skills in planning and public administration. Applies research skills to applied projects in Community Planning Workshop and Nonprofit Consultancy.

courses.title: Professional Development
courses.description: Articulating preliminary career goals and mapping the necessary steps to accomplish these goals.

courses.title: Community Planning Workshop
courses.description: First in a two-term sequence of planning and problem-solving courses. Students working in teams conduct research and develop solutions to planning problems for a client community. Sequence with PPPM 626 .

courses.title: Community Planning Workshop
courses.description: Last in a two-term sequence of planning and problem-solving courses. Students working in teams conduct research and develop solutions to planning problems for a client community. Sequence with PPPM 625 .

courses.title: Public Sector Economics
courses.description: Reasons for governmental intervention and analysis of revenue sources available to governments. Includes discussion of various taxes, intergovernmental transfer policies, and user fees.

courses.title: Public Budget Administration
courses.description: Resource allocation through the budget process. Analysis of budget systems, service costing, and citizen participation in the budget process.

courses.title: Visual Design for Applied Practice
courses.description: Fosters skills including graphic representation of ideas, design software knowledge, and supportive critique of others’ work. Provides a strong graphic design software foundation.

courses.title: Public Management
courses.description: Theory and practice of public service managment; leadership and organizational capacity building, including key management activities for developing effective public service organizations.

courses.title: Public Policy Analysis
courses.description: Techniques in the policymaking process. Determining the impact of policies, comparing alternatives, determining the likelihood that a policy will be adopted and effectively implemented.

courses.title: MPA Policy Analysis Project
courses.description: Students team to produce a professionally oriented policy analysis memorandum and presentation on an assigned topic in a 48-hour period.

courses.title: MPA Capstone Applied Research Project I
courses.description: Team prepare applied research projects for client organizations using analytical and managerial skills to solve problems in public policy analysis or public management. Sequence with PPPM 639 .

courses.title: MPA Capstone Applied Research Project II
courses.description: Teams prepare applied research projects for client organizations using analytical and management skills to solve problems in public policy analysis or public management. Sequence with PPPM 638 .

courses.title: Land Use Planning and Policy
courses.description: Study spatial planning tools and processes that control and manage land developments in order to enhance the well-being of human societies and natural systems.

courses.title: Quantitative Methods
courses.description: Develops skills in quantitative analysis. Emphasizes selecting appropriate analysis procedures and properly interpreting and reporting results.

courses.title: Research Methods in Public Policy and Management
courses.description: Survey of research methods used in the analysis of public policy issues. Emphasis is on determining the appropriate methodology for a given research question.

courses.title: Professional Development II
courses.description: Pre-professional students practice the habits of professionals in the field. Build skills in metacognition and reflection, inclusion and diversity, and personal investment in life long learning and inquiry. Sequence with PPPM 623 .

courses.title: Managing Nonprofit Organizations
courses.description: Principles of effective management of nonprofit organizations. Governance, strategy, legal structure and standards, and volunteer administration.

courses.title: Public and Nonprofit Financial Management
courses.description: Financial management overview for public agencies and nonprofits, including budget processes, financial statements, resource management, expenditure systems, capital project analysis, and internal management control processes.

courses.title: Nonprofit 48-Hour Charrette
courses.description: Requires students to complete a management memo on an assigned topic. Work is completed within a 48-hour period, mimicking the high-stakes, deadline-intensive schedule characterizing professional work in the nonprofit sector.

courses.title: Nonprofit Board Governance
courses.description: Students serve on governing boards of nonprofit organizations for one year: fall, winter, and spring terms.

courses.title: Nonprofit Consultancy
courses.description: Student teams complete projects for nonprofit organizations, assessing organizational needs and capacity, evaluating alternative strategies, and recommending solutions for organizational success.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Dissertation
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Capstone Seminar I
courses.description: This is the first of a 3-course sequence to help students learn about prevention science and progress through the MEd/MS Prevention Science program and completion of their Capstone project. Students attend scientific presentations, complete prevention science readings, and write and present on course material.

courses.title: Capstone Seminar II
courses.description: This is the second of a 3-course sequence to help students learn about prevention science and progress through the MEd/MS Prevention Science program and Capstone project. Students conduct a literature review and develop analytical and writing skills required for their Capstone and other scientific manuscripts.

courses.title: Capstone Seminar III
courses.description: This is the third of a 3-course sequence to help students learn about prevention science and progress through the MEd/MS Prevention Science program and complete their Capstone project. Students complete an original empirical study and corresponding APA formatted 8 to 12-page scientific manuscript and presentation.

courses.title: Introduction to Prevention Science
courses.description: Overview of theory, research, and practice in prevention science and health promotion, including foundational concepts, translation of theory into intervention, methodology, and implementation.

courses.title: Contemporary Issues in Public Health
courses.description: This course introduces approaches, concepts, methods, and perspectives of epidemiology as applied to current public health issues and prevention science research and practice.

courses.title: Implementation Science
courses.description: Provides a framework for examining implementation science and its application to clinical and community-based research.

courses.title: Prevention and Policy
courses.description: Provide students with an understanding of how basic science is translated into evidence-based prevention programs and policy.

courses.title: Meta-Analysis I
courses.description: This course provides an introduction to systematic reviewing and meta-analysis methods for synthesizing results from multiple primary research studies in educational research. During this course, students will learn how to formulate research questions for a meta-analysis; conduct systematic literature searches; and synthesize effect sizes using basic meta-analytic techniques. Sequence with PREV 641.

courses.title: Thinking Like a Social Scientist
courses.description: Illustrates how the ways social scientists think—using quantitative, qualitative, and interpretive methods—help to sharpen thinking for many contexts and careers.

courses.title: Power, Politics, and Inequality
courses.description: Examines power and politics through the lens of inequality, focusing on the constant struggle between the haves and the have-nots.

courses.title: Introduction to Political Science
courses.description: Offers students the tools to think for themselves about politics: multiple ideological and analytical viewpoints on varying political arrangements around the world.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics vary from year to year.

courses.title: United States Politics
courses.description: Theoretical introduction to American institutions, political doctrines, and ideology as these affect the course of politics and public policy in the United States.

courses.title: Introduction to International Relations
courses.description: Introduction to theoretical and methodological tools for the analysis of world politics.

courses.title: Ethics, Identity, and Power
courses.description: This course is a gateway for those considering the "Ethics, Identity, and Society" track in the Political Science major. The course examines political power and ethical ideas about its use, with a focus on race, gender, religion, class, and other forms of social differentiation.

courses.title: Politics of Business
courses.description: Introduces the political science subfield of political economy, which studies the relationship between concepts like the market and the state, capitalism and democracy, politics and the economy. The American political economy is examined from both a historical and an international perspective.

courses.title: Legal Process An Introduction to the American Judiciary
courses.description: Overview of the United States legal system. Covers a range of sociolegal writing and provides a context for the legal system under which the U.S. operates.

courses.title: Introduction to Environmental Politics
courses.description: United States environmental policy and alternative environmental political futures.

courses.title: States' Rights and Wrongs
courses.description: Explores the division between U.S. federal, state and municipal power through the lens of our nation's most contentious and most critical political and policy debates. Examines how elected officials, judges and activists influence fights over federalism.

courses.title: Democracy, Dictators, and Development
courses.description: Examines key questions in political science like why some countries are rich while others are poor, why some countries are democratic and others are authoritarian, how these different political systems work, and which are best equipped to address ethno-nationalist conflict and economic development.

courses.title: United States Political Thought
courses.description: Development of United States political thought from the Revolution through the 20th century. Includes writings of Jefferson, Paine, Madison, Tocqueville.

courses.title: Political Ideologies
courses.description: Ideology is a more-or-less coherent lens through which a person views the world in order to process information and direct action. The course examines how the world looks different for those holding liberal, conservative, communist, fascist, or anarchist world views.

courses.title: Roots of Democracy
courses.description: Examines modern conceptions of democracy, citizenship, legality, virtue, equality, and political order, which grew out of ideas initially developed in ancient Greek city-states. Special attention given to ideas of active citizenship.

courses.title: Sovereignty and Revolution
courses.description: Examines contemporary conceptions of politics springing from European debates about the power of kings, law, and the people. Considers key textual moments in the process by which modern conceptions of democracy emerged.

courses.title: Shadows of Modernity
courses.description: Explores the distinct critiques of modernity offered by John Stuart Mill, Karl Marx, Friedrich Nietzsche, Michel Foucault, and Sigmund Freud. Concepts explored include liberalism, capitalism, power, morality, and the unconscious.

courses.title: Black Lives Matter and American Democracy
courses.description: Explores the ideological origins and contemporary politics of the Black Lives Matter movement. Modules include the demands of/on democracy, race and the criminal justice system, and the politics of protest.

courses.title: The Politics of the Body
courses.description: This course examines the politics of the body in the US. It will focus on scientific and biological definitions of identity and how that affects policies.

courses.title: International Organization
courses.description: Studies efforts by states to cooperate in an effort to avoid or resolve conflict in the realms of security, trade, human rights, and the environment.

courses.title: European Politics
courses.description: Overview of the formation and current dynamics of national politics in Western Europe.

courses.title: United States Foreign Policy I
courses.description: Basic concepts underlying the formulation and implementation of United States foreign policy; relationships between American society and foreign policy; the relationship of the U.S. to its international environment.

courses.title: Governments and Politics in Latin America
courses.description: Social, political, and economic developments in Latin America; causes and consequences of revolutions, democratization, economic politics; examples from Mexico, Brazil, Chile, Argentina, Guatemala, Venezuela. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: The Politics of Development
courses.description: Presents alternative perspectives on key north-south issues: trade, aid, foreign investment, debt, and the environment. Includes such institutions as the International Monetary Fund, World Bank, and World Trade Organization.

courses.title: International Political Economy
courses.description: Links between economics and politics in the international system. Basic concepts include power, dependence, inequality, imperialism, and development. EC 201Z , EC 202Z , or PS 205 recommended preparation.

courses.title: Politics of China
courses.description: Survey of the politics of the People's Republic of China. Emphasis on political sociology and group conflict: elites, ideology, social change, and organization.

courses.title: Southeast Asian Politics
courses.description: Surveys major themes in contemporary Southeast Asian politics, including nation-state building, economic development, democratization, and religious and ethnic politics.

courses.title: Terrorism and Weapons Proliferation
courses.description: Examines causes and control of terrorism, especially preventing terrorist use of weapons of mass destruction; theories and policies of nonproliferation and arms control.

courses.title: Women and Politics
courses.description: Examines the political role of women and questions of equality in the U.S. from historical and contemporary perspectives. Topics may include voting, welfare, reproductive rights, and representation.

courses.title: Mass Media and American Politics
courses.description: The role of the mass media in contemporary American politics; the effect of the media on such institutions as political parties, elections, and the presidency.

courses.title: Politics and Film
courses.description: Examines the political relevance of films and their role as a medium for illustrating, defending, and challenging political ideas.

courses.title: Democratic Dilemmas
courses.description: Core course for the Wayne Morse Scholars Program, exploring significant political and policy challenges confronting our representative democracy.

courses.title: Political Parties and Elections
courses.description: Overview of current developments in political parties and interest groups in the United States.

courses.title: Oregon Government and Politics
courses.description: Current political issues in Oregon with particular attention to political races and ballot measures before the Oregon electorate as well as the state's major political institutions.

courses.title: Politics, Science, and Climate Change
courses.description: How politics influences our understanding of the causes and impacts of climate change and the policies we adopt to address the global climate crisis.

courses.title: Gender in the Law
courses.description: Examines the role courts have played in framing and shaping policies where gender is a central feature with a focus on reproductive rights, pregnancy and abortion, domestic violence, rape, family issues, prostitution, and sexual harassment. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: United States Congress
courses.description: Study of Congress as an institution: congressional elections, the committee system, and the internal distribution of influence; relations with the president and the Supreme Court.

courses.title: Music and Politics
courses.description: In this class we will be asking questions about what is political about music of various musical styles and from diverse historical periods, exploring issues of cultural appropriation, issues of race, gender, and sexuality in music, and the politics of technology and music.

courses.title: Games in Politics
courses.description: Politics viewed as strategic interactions among politicians, voters, and countries; focuses on how to model these interactions using tools of game theory.

courses.title: Gender and Politics in Developing Countries
courses.description: Examines gender politics in the developing world and efforts to help women. Topics include female genital cutting, violence against women, economic development, microfinance, migration, trafficking. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Nuclear Politics of the Middle East
courses.description: History and current nuclear capabilities of all major Middle East states are evaluated. Nuclear strategy, arms control and important technological developments are examined. Theoretical models of proliferation, and debates of a nuclear "taboo" discussed. U.S. foreign policy options toward the Middle East critiqued.

courses.title: United States Social Movements and Political Change
courses.description: Causes and consequences of American social movements. Considers theoretical perspectives. Topics may include agrarian populism, labor movement, civil rights movement, the women's movement, and identity politics.

courses.title: Russian Politics
courses.description: The course traces Russian politics from a communist regime to its current state, investigates contemporary aspects of Russian politics, and examines Russia's vision of the world and its foreign policy.

courses.title: American Indian Politics
courses.description: Examination of the political and constitutional status of American Indian tribes in the United States, with some comparison to Canada. Focus on history, current policy, and potential futures.

courses.title: Non-Western Political Theory
courses.description: This course examines political ideas from outside of the "Western" tradition. It begins by comparing political ideas in classical China and India, and then turns to 19th and 20th century colonial experiences in China, India, and North Africa.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics vary from year to year.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Field Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Offerings vary from year to year, depending on student need and faculty interests.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Offerings vary from year to year, depending on student need and faculty interests.

courses.title: Honors Thesis Prospectus
courses.description: Prepares students for writing the senior honors thesis in political science. Provides guidance in framing a suitable topic, conducting preliminary research, and writing a prospectus.

courses.title: Marxism and Radical Thought
courses.description: Surveys utopian socialist thought, anarchism, Marxism, and Leninism. Central themes include the nature of radical theory, the role of the state, human nature and the new society.

courses.title: Causes and Prevention of War
courses.description: Surveys theories of causes of war; focuses on major theories of prevention; case studies from World War I, World War II, and other wars.

courses.title: Methods for Politics and Policy Analysis I
courses.description: Introduction to quantitative analysis, concepts and methods of empirical research, applied statistical data analysis in political science. Methods include descriptive statistics, bivariate correlation, and regression techniques.

courses.title: Methods for Politics and Policy Analysis II
courses.description: Survey of multivariate model building for political analysis. Multiple regression, discrete-variable techniques, recursive systems, and cross-level analysis. Application of these techniques to concrete political problems.

courses.title: Racial Politics in the United States
courses.description: Considers how race has interacted with political development in the U.S. from the New Deal to the present.

courses.title: Theories of International Politics
courses.description: Competing theories of international relations and strategies for testing the theories.

courses.title: Feminist Political Theories
courses.description: Examines the relationship between feminism, gender, and the state. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Political Economy of East Asia
courses.description: Examines the political economy of East Asia, with a focus on states, markets, and social classes during economic transformation. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: LGBT Rights in the Courts
courses.description: Analyzes the role of legal advocates and the courts in advancing and curtailing lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender rights with a focus on relationship recognition, parenting, employment, housing, military, education, and health care. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Civil Rights in Post-Warren Era
courses.description: Analyzes development in civil rights advocacy in the United States since the heyday of the Warren Court. Focuses primarily on developments in race, gender, disability, and sexuality. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: The United States Presidency
courses.description: An ambivalent view of the presidency as the key institution in the United States political system: source of great good but also of great harm.

courses.title: Constitutional Law
courses.description: Surveys how the U.S. Constitution works as a structure for government. Addresses how the federal courts interact within the U.S. system of government.

courses.title: Matters of Life and Death
courses.description: Examines the right to life from political, legal, and philosophical perspectives. Considers abortion, capital punishment, assisted suicide, just-war theory, and animal rights.

courses.title: Politics of the European Union
courses.description: Surveys the historical development and current workings of the European Union’s major institutions and policies. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: International Environmental Politics
courses.description: How nations solve international environmental problems. Explores major problems, processes, and current debates. Evaluates existing treaties through case studies.

courses.title: Globalization and Development
courses.description: What is globalization? What is development? Do forces of globalization impede or enable development? How do the changing rules and institutions of globalization affect efforts by developing countries to grow their economies? This course is designed to help students think through these questions systematically.

courses.title: U.S. Interventions in Developing Nations
courses.description: Examines theories of intervention: security, economic imperialism, humanitarian intervention, spreading democracy, domestic politics; over thirty-seven U.S. interventions since 1898 are surveyed.

courses.title: Introduction to Rational Choice
courses.description: Introduces the paradigm of rational choice and game theory that is of special significance to politics.

courses.title: United States Supreme Court
courses.description: The Supreme Court as a political body; the judicial role in the context of the economic, political, social, and psychological factors that influence the court's decisions.

courses.title: Civil Rights and Civil Liberties
courses.description: Overview of the role of rights in the United States legal system. Particular emphasis on the role of freedom and equality in a federal system.

courses.title: Politics of Everyday Life
courses.description: Examines how we try to influence each other's behaviors in the course of everyday life. Readings from several disciplines.

courses.title: United States Political Economy
courses.description: Examines United States political-economic institutions from a comparative and historical perspective. Topics include rise and fall of mass production, labor and the law, and regional development.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: Offerings vary from year to year, depending on student needs and faculty interests.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]
courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Offerings vary from year to year, depending on student needs and faculty interests.

courses.title: Marxism and Radical Thought
courses.description: Surveys utopian socialist thought, anarchism, Marxism, and Leninism. Central themes include the nature of radical theory, the role of the state, human nature and the new society.

courses.title: Causes and Prevention of War
courses.description: Surveys theories of causes of war; focuses on major theories of prevention; case studies from World War I, World War II, and other wars.

courses.title: Methods for Politics and Policy Analysis I
courses.description: Introduction to quantitative analysis, concepts and methods of empirical research, applied statistical data analysis in political science. Methods include descriptive statistics, bivariate correlation, and regression techniques.

courses.title: Methods for Politics and Policy Analysis II
courses.description: Survey of multivariate model building for political analysis. Multiple regression, discrete-variable techniques, recursive systems, and cross-level analysis. Application of these techniques to concrete political problems.

courses.title: Racial Politics in the United States
courses.description: Considers how race has interacted with political development in the U.S. from the New Deal to the present.

courses.title: Theories of International Politics
courses.description: Competing theories of international relations and strategies for testing the theories.

courses.title: Feminist Political Theories
courses.description: Examines the relationship between feminism, gender, and the state. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Political Economy of East Asia
courses.description: Examines the political economy of East Asia, with a focus on states, markets, and social classes during economic transformation. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Civil Rights in Post-Warren Era
courses.description: Analyzes development in civil rights advocacy in the United States since the heyday of the Warren Court. Focuses primarily on developments in race, gender, disability, and sexuality. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: The United States Presidency
courses.description: An ambivalent view of the presidency as the key institution in the United States political system: source of great good but also of great harm.

courses.title: Constitutional Law
courses.description: Surveys how the U.S. Constitution works as a structure for government. Addresses how the federal courts interact within the U.S. system of government.

courses.description: Introduces the paradigm of rational choice and game theory that is of special significance to politics. Myagkov.

courses.title: Civil Rights and Civil Liberties
