courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Qualitative Methods
courses.description: Survey of rationales for qualitative methods in the social sciences and the main conceptual and practical issues raised in qualitative research. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Interpretive Methods in Political Science
courses.description: This seminar trains graduate students in the philosophy, theory and practice of interpretive methods by engaging five methods: discourse analysis, genealogy, political ethnography, interpretive interviewing, and decoloniality. We will take some time with each approach, devoting a week or more to theory & method

courses.title: Research Design
courses.description: This course is designed to give students the practical skills necessary to craft a well-designed research project for any type of political science research question. In this way, it prepares students for writing a successful dissertation prospectus, as well as design a second-year paper.

courses.title: Teaching and Learning in Politics and Policy
courses.description: This course for beginning PhD students in Political Science and the School of Planning, Public Policy, and Management provides a forum to discuss teaching as as Graduate Employee and fundamental learning skills for new graduate students.

courses.title: Professionalization in Politics and Policy: [Topic]
courses.description: This course for graduate students in Political Science and PhD students the School of Planning, Public Policy, and Management develops skills necessary for future academic professionals. The specific skills taught are indicated by the course subtitle.

courses.title: American Political Institutions
courses.description: This is one of three required core seminars for PhD students who plan to take a comprehensive examination in American politics. We explore how political science approaches the study of institutional contributions to political and policy developments.

courses.title: United States Political Culture
courses.description: In this course we examine the different ways that scholars study the relationship between politics and culture, and then focus on the United States as our case.

courses.title: State of the Discipline
courses.description: Introduction to trends in the political science profession and to the faculty at the University of Oregon.

courses.title: Dissertation Prospectus
courses.description: This course is required for third-year Political Science doctoral students and will help them develop a detailed plan (prospectus) for researching and writing an acceptable dissertation in order to satisfy PhD requirements.

courses.title: Political Theory
courses.description: Survey of major works in the field of classical and contemporary political theory.

courses.title: International Relations
courses.description: Survey of major works in the field of international relations.

courses.title: Public Policy
courses.description: Survey of major works in the field of public policy.

courses.title: International Political Economy
courses.description: One of two required seminars for PhD students planning to take a comprehensive examination in international relations. Explores how politics and economics interact in shaping economic relations among states.

courses.title: States and State-Society Relations
courses.description: Examines how scholars have studied the state and its relationship to society. Students develop a comparative perspective of contentious politics ranging from peasant rebellions to revolutions to transnational social movements.

courses.title: Comparative Political Economy
courses.description: Doctoral-level survey of how political factors explain variations in national or regional trajectories of economic development, including industrialization, wealth, growth, and inequality.

courses.title: States and Regimes
courses.description: Introduction to the major issues, theories, concepts, and arguments about states and regimes in comparative politics. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Introduction to Psychology I
courses.description: Introduction to the science and application of psychology. Emphasis will be placed on psychological concepts, theories, and principles related to: Research Methods, Behavioral Neuroscience, Consciousness, Sensation/Perception, Learning, Memory, Thinking and Intelligence, and related topics.

courses.title: Introduction to Psychology II
courses.description: Introduction to the science and application of psychology. Emphasis will be placed on psychological concepts, theories, and principles related to: Personality, Social Psychology, Health and Well-Being, Motivation and Emotion, Disorders, Therapies, Lifespan Development, and related topics

courses.title: Scientific Thinking in Psychology
courses.description: Fundamentals in the empirical study of human behavior, including hypothesis formation, experiment design, behavioral data basics, and critical evaluation of scientific claims. Sequence with PSY 302 , PSY 303 . Students may not register for PSY 301 after completing PSY 303 .

courses.title: Statistical Methods in Psychology
courses.description: Probability and statistics applied in psychological research. Topics include descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, correlation, regression, and design of experiments. With laboratory. Sequence with PSY 301 , PSY 303 .

courses.title: Research Methods in Psychology
courses.description: Practical experience formulating a research question, conducting literature review, analyzing data, and communicating original research about human behavior. Sequence with PSY 301 , PSY 302 .

courses.title: Biopsychology
courses.description: Relationships between brain and endocrine activity and behavior. Topics include sensation, perception, sexual behavior, drug effects, eating, drinking, sleeping, dreaming, and learning.

courses.title: Cognition
courses.description: Major topics addressed in this class include perception, attention, memory, language, reasoning, and decision-making.

courses.title: Social Psychology
courses.description: Processes underlying social perception and social interaction. Topics include aggression, the self-concept, stereotyping and prejudice, conformity, persuasion, attraction, and helping.

courses.title: Personality
courses.description: Theory and methods for studying human traits, including personality tests and measures. Current research in personality. Studies of age, gender, culture, and relation to emotion and motivation.

courses.title: Developmental Psychology
courses.description: Survey of cognitive, social-emotional, and personality development in infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood.

courses.title: Psychopathology
courses.description: Major descriptive and theoretical approaches to etiological, developmental, and social factors in emotion and personality disorders. Includes assessment, diagnosis, treatment, and special topics.

courses.title: Music and the Brain
courses.description: Explores the neural correlates of our perception of tonality, harmony, melody, and rhythm and how these relate to neurobiology, brain damage, and cognitive neuroscience.

courses.title: Culture and Mental Health
courses.description: Role of culture in the definition and maintenance of mental health and the definition and treatment of mental illness.

courses.title: Psychology of Gender
courses.description: Critical analysis of evidence for sex differences, gender roles, and the effect of gender on traditional issues in psychology. Topics include parenthood, violence, and sexual orientation.

courses.title: Psychoactive Drugs
courses.description: Physiological and behavioral effects of psychoactive drugs such as alcohol, opiates, barbiturates, and excitants. The psychology of use and overuse; therapies for correcting drug problems.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Research: [Topic]
courses.description: Disciplined inquiry of a topic with varying techniques and assignments suited to the nature and conditions of the problem being investigated. Often pursued in relation to a dissertation or thesis.

courses.title: Thesis
courses.description: A written document resulting from study or research and submitted as a major requirement for a degree.

courses.title: Reading and Conference: [Topic]
courses.description: A particular selection of material read by a student and discussed in conference with a faculty member.

courses.title: Practicum: [Topic]
courses.description: A series of clinical experiences under academic supervision designed to integrate theory and principles with practice.

courses.title: Seminar: [Topic]
courses.description: A small group of students studying a subject with a faculty member. Although practices vary, students may do original research and exchange results through informal lectures, reports, and discussions.

courses.title: Laboratory Projects: [Topic]
courses.description: An experience in a lab setting in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content master.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: A presentation incorporating the knowledge and skills acquired from course work completed for a degree.

courses.title: Experimental Course: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Applied Data Analysis
courses.description: Intermediate-level practical data analysis and interpretation. Topics include experimental design, analysis of variance, multiple regression, exploratory data analysis. Extensive computer use. Honors only.

courses.title: Psychology and Law
courses.description: Introduction to topics of concern to both psychology and the law. Includes eyewitness identification, legal decision-making, criminal defenses, profiling, polygraphy, and mental-health law.

courses.title: Forensic Psychology
courses.description: This class focuses on the involvement of clinical psychologists with the legal system. The class will discuss the approaches, ethics, considerations, and measures utilized when clinical psychologists examine psycho-legal issues such as risk, custody, therapist-client confidentiality, and competency.

courses.title: Learning and Memory
courses.description: Processes underlying learning and memory, including evolution. Topics range from simple forms of behavior change to the acquisition, retention, forgetting, and retrieval of symbolic information.

courses.title: Human Performance
courses.description: Motor and intellectual capacities; analysis of the flow of information within the nervous system; applications of performance principles to human-machine systems.

courses.title: Perception
courses.description: Topics covered are color, size, shape, depth, distance, and movement. Examines the relationships between stimuli and perception, stimuli and the neural response, and the neural response and perception.

courses.title: Psychology and Evolution
courses.description: Evolutionary Psychology is the application of evolutionary theory to human cognition. Topics include: survival, mating, parenting, cooperation, aggression, status, and comparative cognition. We ground theory and criticism using empirical data from many levels of analysis.

courses.title: Brain Mechanisms of Behavior
courses.description: Organization of the mammalian brain. Structure and function of the neuronal systems underlying vision, perception, motivation, coordinated movement, sleep-wakefulness, learning and memory, and affective disorders.

courses.title: Cognitive Neuroscience
courses.description: Integrative neural mechanisms of normal and abnormal processes in systems (e.g., selective attention, language, memory, object recognition, and emotion).

courses.title: Hormones and Behavior
courses.description: Relationships among the brain, endocrine systems, and behavior. Developmental effects of hormones on the brain, puberty, sexuality, aggression, stress.

courses.title: Group Dynamics
courses.description: Topics in small-group dynamics, including decision-making, conflict, and changes over time in group structure and behavior.

courses.title: Decision-Making
courses.description: Psychological processes involved in judgment and decision-making. Normative theories of ideal behavior contrasted with descriptive analysis of actual behavior.

courses.title: Cultural Psychology
courses.description: Examines interdependence between mind and culture in substantive domains such as social cognition, motivation, emotion, and psychopathology. Cultural pluralism, collective identities, tolerance, and diversity considered.

courses.title: Stereotyping and Prejudice
courses.description: This course examines contemporary issues in stereotyping, prejudice, and discrimination, both from the perceiver's and the target's perspective. We will cover the phenomena and processes associated with one's beliefs about members of social groups (stereotypes), attitudes and evaluative responses toward group members (prejudice), and behaviors toward members of a social group based on their group membership (discrimination).

courses.title: Motivation and Emotion
courses.description: Adaptive human behavior; considers biological processes involved in emotions, how emotions interact with cognition, and social influences.

courses.title: Psychology of Trauma
courses.description: Cognitive, neuropsychological, developmental, social, and clinical approaches to understanding trauma. Includes analysis of childhood trauma, sexual assault, domestic violence, terrorism, combat, and natural disasters.

courses.title: Intimate Relationships
courses.description: Adult intimacy and love relationships. Clinical-counseling approaches: assessment, couple and family therapies, and evaluation. Models of relationship functioning and the empirical study of interpersonal relationships.

courses.title: Cognitive Development
courses.description: Intellectual development in children from infancy to adolescence with a focus on early childhood. Topics covered include perception, attention, memory, reasoning, conceptual structure, social cognition.

courses.title: Social Development
courses.description: Theoretical issues and empirical studies of social-emotional development. Topics may include attachment, temperament, moral development, family interaction, self-image, aggression, and sex-role development.

courses.title: Infancy
courses.description: Mechanisms and processes that underlie and promote rapid changes in physical, cognitive, and linguistic capabilities, from birth to 24 months. Covers innovative methodologies and cultural attitudes toward infants.

courses.title: Development and Psychopathology
courses.description: Biological and environmental factors that shape normal and abnormal development. Analysis of how family functioning affects psychopathology and resilience in children and adolescents.

courses.title: Honors in Psychology
courses.description: Reading and conference.

courses.title: Honors is Psychology
courses.description: Reading and conference.

courses.title: Honors in Psychology
courses.description: Reading and conference.

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Data Analysis I
courses.description: Introduction to probability, hypothesis testing, and analysis of variance with applications. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.

courses.title: Data Analysis II
courses.description: Multiple regression and advanced topics in analysis of variance. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.

courses.title: Data Analysis III
courses.description: Multivariate techniques including MANOVA, factor analysis, principal components. Includes training in the statistical analysis of data by computer. With laboratory.

courses.title: Community Needs Assessments
courses.description: Explores the formative steps in intervention design and implementation. Introduces key concepts and methods for needs assessments with agencies and individuals serving at-risk communities. Topics include building community partnership and using qualitative research methodology to empirically assess barriers to and facilitators of access to interventions.

courses.title: Implementation with Community and Cultural Perspectives
courses.description: This course explores essential tools for implementing effective (or evidence-based) programs with fidelity in community settings. Topics include community outreach, diversity science, partnership and co-creation, team science, and models of change.

courses.title: Substance Use and Addiction
courses.description: Exploration of brain mechanisms underlying motivated behaviors and dysfunctions that lead to addictive behaviors. Topics include neurobiological and psychological effects of addictive drugs, factors that contribute to addiction, societal impacts, the link between addiction and habits, and how this relates to behaviors like pathological gambling.

courses.title: Intervention Science
courses.description: Exploration of the development and evaluation of evidence-based treatments, the field of implementation science, and culturally competent intervention approaches. Students develop clinical intervention and case conceptualization skills while learning about infant, child, adolescent, adult, and couple focused evidence-based treatments for a variety of disorders.

courses.title: Clinical Psychobiology
courses.description: Research and theory from the neurosciences applied to clinical problems and biological therapies.

courses.title: Methods of Program Evaluation
courses.description: This course provides full-spectrum coverage of program evaluation. We will cover theory, model testing, experimental design and basic statistical theory and methods. Designed to provide students with tools and techniques they can apply to program development and evaluation within their home organizations.

courses.title: Translational Neuroscience in Early Childhood
courses.description: Introduction to key concepts and methods of translational neuroscience. Uses a multidisciplinary lens to examine environmental influences on early human development, from the prenatal period through early childhood. Reviews the evidence base for interventions that aim to mitigate risk factors in these critical developmental periods.

courses.title: Translational Neuroscience in Adolescence
courses.description: Examines neuroscience research on brain structure, function and neuroplasticity specific to adolescent development. Relates principles of adolescent brain development to evidence-based treatments. Topics include the effects of hormones on brain development, increased sensitivity to dopamine in reward seeking, and the neural basis of social cognition.

courses.title: Translational Neuroscience in Adulthood
courses.description: Examines neuroscience research on brain development and neuroplasticity in adulthood. Emphasizes bidirectional links between neurobiology and behavior and connections to evidence based psychosocial treatment common to aging adult populations. Topics include stress neurobiology, anxiety and depression, substance use, and cognitive function over the adult lifespan.

courses.title: Trauma Informed Interventions
courses.description: Seminar on the effects of adverse childhood experiences (ACES) and trauma on physical and mental health. Topics include stress-related psychopathology, culture- and trauma-informed clinical intervention, and socio-demographic and cultural factors related to adversity and stress.

courses.title: Capstone Research
courses.description: Faculty-supervised research credits for each quarter in which students conduct their capstone research project, which is individualized based on student interests, context and academic or career goals (minimum 6 terms; credits per term vary from 2-9 based on the term in the program; minimum 17 total credits). Project may be embedded in a community-based agency or organization. Small group advising allows for faculty-student advising on project enhanced by peer-to-peer advising/mentoring.

courses.title: Internship: [Topic]
courses.description: Professional practice in an organization that integrates concepts studied at the university with career-related work experience.

courses.description: A series of practical experiences on or off campus to understand principles or develop skills in performing selected tasks.

courses.title: World Religions: Asian Traditions
courses.description: Introduction to related religious traditions of Asia, including Hinduism, Buddhism, Daoism, Confucianism and Shinto. Readings in sacred texts and scholarlary literature. Lecture, discussion.

courses.title: World Religions: Near Eastern Traditions
courses.description: Introduction to the Abrahamic religions of Judaism, Christianity, Islam and to related traditions such as the Zoroastrian, Manichaean, Mandean, Baha'i. Lecture, discussion.

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Early Judaism
courses.description: In this course we will study the history, literature, and cultural and religious development of Judaism from the biblical period to the early rabbinic period (roughly until 500 CE). We will survey the foundational biblical heritage, with an emphasis on how Jews reinterpreted these traditions and lived among other cultures.

courses.title: Introduction to the Bible I
courses.description: The focus of this course is on the content and organization of the Hebrew scriptures (Old Testament)--highlighting the themes, streams of tradition, literary genres, etc., in the development of its ideas, ideals, and religious expression. This course provides an introduction to the scholarly methods and research tools used in biblical studies, specifically, and in religious studies as a whole.

courses.title: Introduction to the Bible II
courses.description: Examination of the written traditions of early Christianity with an emphasis on the New Testament.

courses.title: Introduction to Islam
courses.description: Islamic religious tradition, beginnings to present. Pre-Islamic Arabia, Prophet Muhammed, pillars of Islam, ethics and piety, Sunni-Shiite divide, reform and renewal movements.

courses.title: Chinese Religions
courses.description: Prehistoric roots of Chinese religion, Confucius and his followers, philosophical Taoism, Han Confucianism, religious Taoism, Chinese Buddhism, Neo-Confucianism, religion in China today.

courses.title: Japanese Religions
courses.description: Early Shinto and its developments, Japanese Buddhism, transformation of Taoism and Confucianism, medieval Shinto, religion in the Tokugawa period, Nationalistic Shinto, folk religion, new religions.

courses.title: Religions of India
courses.description: An introduction to the major religious traditions of the Indian subcontinent: Hinduism and Buddhism, and more briefly Sikhism and Jainism.

courses.title: Jesus and the Gospels
courses.description: This course focuses on the historical figure Jesus of Nazareth and the stories people have told about him in ancient literature and modern scholarship.

courses.title: History of Christianity I
courses.description: Course of Christian history in East and West; relations between spirituality, doctrine, and institutional forms. Covers the ancient period, from the Apostolic Fathers to the Islamic conquests (90–650).

courses.title: History of Christianity II
courses.description: Course of Christian history in East and West; relations between spirituality, doctrine, and institutional forms. Covers medieval Western Christianity, from the Germanic invasions to the Reformation (400–1500).

courses.title: History of Christianity III
courses.description: Course of Christian history in East and West; relations between spirituality, doctrine, and institutional forms. Covers modern Western Christianity, from the Reformation to the present (1500 to the present).

courses.title: History of Eastern Christianity I
courses.description: Byzantine Christianity from the founding of the Christian Roman Empire to the Fall of Constantinople in the 15th century.

courses.title: History of Eastern Christianity II
courses.description: The Eastern churches from the 15th century to the present.

courses.title: Introduction to the Qur'an
courses.description: An introduction to the nature of the Qur'an and the various ways it has been interpreted throughout history by both Muslims and non-Muslims.

courses.title: Dark Self, East and West
courses.description: Comparative examination of selfhood in Eastern and Western religious thought and cultural contexts. Focus on dark side or problematic dimensions of Buddhist, Christian, Daoist, Jewish, and other thought.

courses.title: Mysticism
courses.description: The experiential or mystical dimensions of the three major Abrahamic faiths. Exploration of the original writings of men and women from each spiritual tradition.

courses.title: War, Terrorism, and Religion
courses.description: Offers an examination of the theme of war, terrorism, and religion, focusing on cases of religiously motivated acts of violence in the contemporary era.

courses.title: Religion After Atheism
courses.description: Atheist critiques of religion, analyses of secularism from multiple disciplinary perspectives, and responses to atheism and secularism by modernist Christian, Muslim, and Buddhist writers.

courses.description: Survey of Russian literature from its origins to the present; emphasis on Pushkin, Gogol, Turgenev, Dostoevsky, Tolstoy, Chekhov, and contemporary works. Readings, lectures, and discussions in English.

courses.title: Russian Culture
courses.description: Comparative aesthetics and development of art, film, architecture, music, and literature in the context of Russian intellectual history. Readings, lectures, and discussions in English.

courses.title: Russian through Theater
courses.description: Combined elements of Russian language, literature, and culture learned through participation in a theater production. Credits vary with degree of involvement.

courses.title: Third-Year Russian I
courses.description: Intermediate-to-advanced Russian. Further development of basic skills, with special attention to reading comprehension, conversational competence, grammatical accuracy, and cultural sophistication.

courses.title: Dostoevsky
courses.description: Introduction to the novels and short stories of Dostoevsky. His literary, ethical, and political development. Readings and instruction in English.

courses.title: Russian Literature and Film
courses.description: Introduction to great works of 19th-century Russian literature and analysis of the cinematic adaptation of these works by Western filmmakers.

courses.title: Race in Russia and America
courses.description: While the racial conversation dominates American culture, few consider the history and significance of race beyond the U.S. This course examines race from a fresh, much less examined, perspective – that of Russia, Soviet Union, and post-Soviet world, in comparison to the US.

courses.title: Sports Business and Society
courses.description: This course provides an interdisciplinary treatment of the intersection of sports business and society. It reviews the broader sociology of sport and the critical role sport has played on issues of gender and race both domestically and globally.

courses.title: Sports Business and Society
courses.description: This course provides an interdisciplinary treatment of the intersection of sports business and society. It reviews the broader sociology of sport and the critical role sport has played on issues of gender and race both domestically and globally.

courses.title: The Business of the Olympic Games
courses.description: An introduction to the contemporary global business model of the Olympic Games including finance & economic impact, sales, sponsorship, media & technology, government relations, sustainability & regional impact, socio-political context, diversity & inclusion.

courses.title: Sports Marketing
courses.description: Essentials of effective sports marketing. Includes research, segmentation, product development, pricing, licensing, and communication channels such as advertising, sales promotion, and publicity.

courses.title: Sports Sponsorship
courses.description: Detailed consideration of the relationship between sports and corporate sponsorship programs. Focuses on alignment marketing, sponsor value, and sponsorship evaluation.

courses.title: Law and Sports Business
courses.description: Core legal principles across the sports business spectrum to improve risk and strategic management capabilities, competitive advantage, and critical negotiations.

courses.title: Financing Sports Business
courses.description: Revenue sources for sports organizations. Includes conventional sources (e.g., tax support, bonds, ticket, media, concession sales) and innovations (e.g, initial public offerings, seat licenses, naming rights).

courses.title: Sports Brand Management
courses.description: An integrative course that supports skills development for success in managing sports-related businesses and brands. It utilizes critical thinking, creative imagining and professional writing in developing capabilities used in businesses aligned with or in sports.

courses.title: Sports Product
courses.description: Examines the companies and organizations of the international sports product industry: manufacturing innovation, company management, branding, retail and wholesale.

courses.title: Marketing Sports Properties
courses.description: Examines essentials of effective sports marketing. Includes product or property development, legal aspects, segmentation, pricing, and communication channels (e.g., broadcast media).

courses.title: Sports Sponsorship Alliances
courses.description: Detailed consideration of the relation between sports, law, and corporate sponsorship programs. Focuses on alignment marketing issues, strategic communication through sponsorship, sponsor value, and sponsorship valuation.

courses.title: Legal Aspects of Sports Business
courses.description: Examines social responsibility and legal concepts in sports management including constitutional regulatory powers, individual participation rights, drug testing, antitrust, labor rights, intellectual property rights, sponsorships, product and event liability.

courses.title: Economic Aspects of Sports
courses.description: Comprehensive coverage of traditional and innovative revenue methods available to sports organizations from public and private sources. Detailed consideration of venue-based income sources (e.g., premium seating, permanent seat licenses).

courses.title: Existentialist Literature and Thought
courses.description: Survey of the existential tradition in German and Scandinavian literature, philosophy, drama, and film. Historical and conceptual developments are considered, from Kierkegaard to Kafka. Taught in English. Multilisted with GER 220M .

courses.title: Text and Interpretation
courses.description: Introduction to textual analysis; explores the relationship between experience, description, and identity through the reading and viewing of Scandinavian literature and film. Taught in English.

courses.title: Vikings through the Icelandic Sagas
courses.description: Introduction to the social, political, and cultural expressions of Viking society through the Sagas, the unique prose narratives of medieval Iceland. Conducted in English.

courses.title: Nordic Cinema
courses.description: Examines cinematic culture in the Nordic countries of Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, and Sweden. Includes works by Ingmar Bergman and the Danish group Dogma 95. Taught in English.

courses.title: History of Cinema
courses.description: A survey of Nordic cinema from the silent era to the present. Films will be viewed and analyzed within their aesthetic and historical contexts. Taught in English. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Directors, Movements, and Manifestos
courses.description: A directed study of specific directors, movements, and manifestos from the Nordic cinematic tradition. Taught in English. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Constructions versus Constrictions of Identity
courses.description: Explores the notion of regional, ethnic, gender, and class identity in Scandinavian texts and culture through folklore, fairy tales and Children's Literature. Conducted in English.

courses.title: Revisions of the Scandinavian Dream
courses.description: Examines development of Scandinavian countries from impoverished kingdoms on the European periphery to modern, multicultural welfare societies. Analyzes patterns in the arts, social and political structures, ecological issues. Taught in English.

courses.title: Norse Mythology
courses.description: Critical evaluation of the religious beliefs in Scandinavia from prehistory through the Viking Age. Taught in English. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Medieval Hero and Monster
courses.description: Study of medieval Scandinavian and Germanic literature addressing the remarkably fine line drawn between the heroes and monsters depicted. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Food, Culture, and Identity in Germany and Scandinavia
courses.description: Examines the relationships among food, culture, and identity in Germany and Scandinavia. Interdisciplinary readings, lectures, films, and discussions focus on literary and symbolic representations of food from production to consumption, and on images and depictions of cooking, eating, drinking, and feasting. Multilisted with GER 345M .

courses.title: Periods in Scandinavian Literature
courses.description: Possible topics are modern breakthrough and modernism in Scandinavian literature. Student discussion, oral presentations, and written papers. Readings and discussions in English.

courses.title: Scandinavian Women Writers
courses.description: Examines social issues, especially gender, in literature written by women from Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, and Sweden. Primary emphasis on 19th- and 20th-century texts.

courses.title: Genres in Scandinavian Literature
courses.description: Recent topics include short narrative fiction and Scandinavian drama. Student discussion, oral presentations, and written papers. Readings and discussions in English.

courses.title: Workshop: [Topic]

courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Introduction to Sociology
courses.description: Introduces the central concepts, theories, and methods that define the sociological approach to investigating the social forces that shape our lives. Topics may include social structure, culture, socialization, race, class, gender, sexuality, and inequality.

courses.title: Social Inequality
courses.description: Overview of social inequality, cross-culturally and within the United States. Examines relationship of social inequality based on social class, race, and gender to social change, social institutions, and self-identity.

courses.title: Introduction to Criminology
courses.description: This course is designed to give you a broad introduction to criminology as a discipline, including measurements and definitions of crime, the myriad societal & personal factors that explain crime, and criminal justice system processes and responses. We’ll also consider how criminology is woven into a range of careers in research, education, government, law, and non-profit organizations.

courses.title: American Society
courses.description: Selected aspects of American culture and institutions and the ways in which they are changing.

courses.title: Community, Environment, and Society
courses.description: Interrelationship of social and environmental factors in human communities, processes of community change, impact of environmental change on human communities.

courses.title: Social Theory
courses.description: Analysis of the major writers and ideas that have shaped contemporary sociology. Focus on recurrent concepts and issues that continue to challenge sociological inquiry.

courses.title: Research Methods
courses.description: The development of social research; the nature of scientific inquiry and basic methods and techniques; examination of representative sociological studies from the standpoint of methodology.

courses.title: Statistical Analysis in Sociology
courses.description: Construction and interpretation of tables and graphs, descriptive statistics, measures of association and contingency relationships, basic ideas of probability, and elementary statistical inference applied to nonexperimental research.

courses.title: Social Issues and Movements
courses.description: Contemporary social issues viewed in relation to the social structure of American society. Social movements and ideologies related to these issues.

courses.title: Sociology of the Mass Media
courses.description: Analysis of media events: advertisements, news broadcasts, documentaries, popular music, and television. Perspectives include content analysis, semiotics, functionalist and structuralist paradigms, and power system analysis.

courses.title: Self and Society
courses.description: How the thought, feeling, and behavior of individuals influence and are influenced by the actual, imagined, or implied presence of others.

courses.title: Sociology of the Family
courses.description: Introduction to and historical perspective of the family as a social institution and small-group association.

courses.title: Race and Ethnicity
courses.description: Examines the major racial and ethnic groups in the United States with special attention to social interactions and inequalities.

courses.title: Work and Occupations
courses.description: Characteristics of work and occupational careers in modern societies; relationships of those to family, the economy, bureaucracy, technology, and alienation.

courses.title: Sociology of Gender
courses.description: Position of women in contemporary society; women and work, politics, families, the economy; intersection of gender, race, and class; women's movements.

courses.title: Urban Sociology
courses.description: Examines the growth of cities; urban inequalities, politics, and social movements; built environment, ecology, and sustainability of cities and identity; global cities and immigration.

courses.title: Deviance, Social Control, and Crime
courses.description: Introduces the sociological concept of deviance, its relationship to the creation and violation of rules, laws, and other forms of social order with special attention to crime and the criminal justice system.

courses.title: Medical Sociology
courses.description: This course is designed as an introduction to the broad field of medical sociology and the sociology of health and illness.

courses.description: Descriptive and inferential statistics, including multiple regression.

courses.title: Sociological Research Methods
courses.description: Advanced statistical techniques focusing on generalized linear models.

courses.title: Issues in Environmental Sociology: [Topic]
courses.description: Analysis of selected topics in environmental sociology. Topics include environmental movement, impacts of technological change, environmental policy and the state, environmental values, attitudes, and behaviors.

courses.title: Issues in Urban Sociology: [Topic]
courses.description: Determinants and consequences of urbanization under different conditions; the city as a social and ecological system.

courses.title: Sociology of Race and Ethnicity: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced analysis of selected topics in sociology of race/ethnicity. Topics vary. Examples include Asian Americans, Latinos, mixed race, racial oppression, residential segregation, and the post-civil rights era.

courses.title: Issues in Sociology of Work: [Topic]
courses.description: Selected topics in sociology of work: occupational structures and careers, industrial democracy; technological change and work reform, politics of work.

courses.title: Issues in Sociology of Organizations: [Topic]
courses.description: Analysis of selected topics in the sociology of organizations. Topics include industrial sociology, organizational change; organizational democracy; corporate deviance; bureaucracy, power, and society.

courses.title: Social Stratification
courses.description: The interrelations among class, race, and sex. Historical origins and development of class and class systems including slavery.

courses.title: Issues of Migration: [Topic]
courses.description: Sociological analysis of migration, including dynamics of race and ethnicity, social structure, and social policy. Examines assimilation, marginalization, multiculturalism, postcolonialism, and social cohesion. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Issues in Sociology of Gender: [Topic]
courses.description: Advanced analysis of gender and social relations of power in contemporary society. Variable topics include Women and Health; Violence against Women.

courses.title: Feminist Theory
courses.description: Examines major sociological theories that elucidate the position of women and gender as part of the configuration of social relations of power in contemporary societies.

courses.title: Sex and Society
courses.description: Examines alternative sociological perspectives on sexual behavior, the social construction and regulation of sexuality, contemporary social and political issues pertaining to sexuality.

courses.title: Issues in Cultural Sociology: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics include the examination of sociological literature, research, and perspectives on a range of important themes core to cultural sociology, including production and consumption of culture, semiotics, community and networks, and popular culture.

courses.title: Issues in Science, Technology & Society: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics include the examination of sociological literature, research, and perspectives on a range of themes core to understanding science, technology and society, including the public understanding of science, science and social movements, and social inequalities in science.

courses.title: Political Sociology
courses.description: Analysis of political theory and behavior, social bases of power and policy determination, institutional interrelationships, intellectuals and ideologies, political trends and change, political participation and membership.

courses.title: Economic Sociology
courses.description: Applies the sociological perspective to basic economic phenomena such as markets, exchange, prices, money and rationality.

courses.title: Marxist Sociological Theory
courses.description: Basic concepts, theory, and social analysis in the works of Marx and Engels. Topics include dialectical and historical materialism, class, historical development, political economy, and imperialism.

courses.title: Issues in Deviance, Control, and Crime: [Topic]
courses.description: Topics vary. Examples are modern policing, juvenile delinquency, correction, emerging forms of social control.

courses.title: Overview of Sociological Methods
courses.description: Examines the research process--framing research questions, qualitative and quantitative design, relationships between methods and theory, deductive and inductive investigation logic, research ethics, sampling procedures, explanatory power.

courses.title: Advanced Sociological Methods: [Topic]
courses.description: Major methodological topics such as comparative, demographic, experimental, field, historical, and survey methods. Other possible topics include time-series analysis.

courses.title: Advanced Sociological Theory: [Topic]
courses.description: Major sociological theories such as modern functionalism, contemporary Marxism, phenomenology, postmodernism, feminist and organizational theory.

courses.title: Environment and Resource Issues: [Topic]
courses.description: Explores issues of environmental sociology and resource policy, including ecological crisis; environmental justice as it pertains to race, gender, class, and international inequality.

courses.title: Sociological Theory I
courses.description: Sociological theories of the 19th century (especially Marx, Weber, and Durkheim) and 20th century (e.g., modern functionalism, feminist, neo-Marxism, neo-Weberian, poststructuralist theories).

courses.title: Sociological Theory II
courses.description: Major themes and historical foundation of contemporary sociological theory.

courses.title: Teaching in the Social Sciences
courses.description: Prepares graduate students to teach their own classes. Covers pedagogy and develops practical skills. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Race and Ethnicity Issues: [Topic]
courses.description: Explores current reseach and theoretical debates, such as Chicano-Chicana and Latino-Latina studies, in the sociology of race and ethnicity.

courses.title: Issues in Sociology of Gender: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines sociological theories of gender, focusing on a particular substantive area such as health, work, family, or sexuality. Explores gender in relation to race, ethnicity, and class.

courses.title: Political and Economic Sociology Issues: [Topic]
courses.description: Examines the relationship between economic institutions and political processes. Sample topics include theories of modern capitalism, corporations and the state, development and underdevelopment, war and peace.

courses.title: First-Year Spanish
courses.description: Emphasis on the development of speaking, reading, and writing skills; introduction to Hispanic culture. Sequence. Conducted in Spanish.

courses.title: First-Year Spanish
courses.description: Emphasis on the development of speaking, reading, and writing skills; introduction to Hispanic culture. Sequence. Conducted in Spanish.

courses.title: First-Year Spanish
courses.description: Emphasis on the development of speaking, reading, and writing skills; introduction to Hispanic culture. Sequence. Conducted in Spanish.

courses.title: Intensive Beginning Spanish
courses.description: Intensive study for experienced language learners; introduction to Hispanic culture. Prereq: evidence of placement. Sequence. Conducted in Spanish. Cannot be combined with SPAN 101 , SPAN 102 , SPAN 103 for more than 15 credits of first-year Spanish.

courses.title: Intensive Beginning Spanish
courses.description: Intensive study for experienced language learners; introduction to Hispanic culture. Prereq: evidence of placement. Sequence. Conducted in Spanish. Cannot be combined with SPAN 101 , SPAN 102 , SPAN 103 for more than 15 credits of first-year Spanish.

courses.title: Hispanic and Latinx Cultures
courses.description: This course provides students with an overview of the cultures of the Hispanic/Latinx world (Spain, Latin America, US)

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Intermediate Spanish I
courses.description: Development of Spanish language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Spanish-speaking world. Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena.

courses.title: Intermediate Spanish II
courses.description: Development of Spanish language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Spanish-speaking world. Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena. Sequence with SPAN 201 , SPAN 203 .

courses.title: Intermediate Spanish III
courses.description: Development of Spanish language proficiency beyond beginning level through the study of the cultural products, practices, and perspectives of the Spanish-speaking world. Topics include history, geography, demographics, foodways, social movements, art, politics, and other cultural phenomena. Sequence with SPAN 201 , SPAN 202 .

courses.title: Latino Heritage I
courses.description: Designed for heritage learners—students who grew up with Spanish in their community and want to build communication skills in Spanish. Content focuses on personal experiences in U.S. Latinx communities. Cannot be combined with SPAN 201 , SPAN 202 , SPAN 203 for more than 15 credits of second-year Spanish. Sequence with SPAN 228 .

courses.title: Latino Heritage II
courses.description: Designed for heritage learners—students who grew up with Spanish in their community and want to continue developing communication skills in Spanish. Content focuses on personal experiences in U.S. Latinx communities. Cannot be combined with SPAN 201 , SPAN 202 , SPAN 203 for more than 15 credits of second-year Spanish. Sequence with SPAN 218 .

courses.title: Special Studies: [Topic]
courses.description: Experimental course - topic varies.

courses.title: Cultura y lengua: Identidades hispanas
courses.description: Develops advanced language skills through analysis of major historical influences in the cultures of Spanish-speaking regions: Spain, Latin America, and the United States. Taught in Spanish.

courses.title: Cultura y lengua: Expresiones artisticas
courses.description: Develops advanced language skills through the study of cultural products (e.g., art, literature, film, music) in Spanish-speaking societies. Taught in Spanish.

courses.title: Cultura y lengua Cambio social
courses.description: Develops advanced language skills through the investigation of major currents of change in modern Spanish-speaking societies; gender issues, technology, revolution and counter-revolution.Taught in Spanish.

courses.title: Oral Skills
courses.description: Practice in improving listening, comprehension, and oral skills in Spanish. Communicative activities in class in addition to language laboratory work.

courses.title: Culture and Language Bilingual Communities
courses.description: Designed for heritage learners—students who grew up with Spanish in their community and want to continue developing communication skills in Spanish. Explores socio-linguistic dynamics of communities in which Spanish is in contact with another language. Open to all students. Taught in Spanish.

courses.title: Advanced Writing in Spanish
courses.description: This requirement for the Spanish major provides additional language development for students early on in the major, emphasizing academic writing skills in Spanish.

courses.title: Spanish in the Media
courses.description: Designed for heritage learners. Examines the role of Spanish in various forms of media such as television, Internet, and literature. Students practice advanced writing skills necessary to participate in argumentative writing and close textual readings.

courses.title: Grammars of Spanish
courses.description: This course introduces students to the central themes of the linguistic structure of Spanish. We will use Critical Language Awareness (CLA) to study and practice elements of both the privileged normative varieties and minoritized varieties of Spanish. We also reflect on the social meaning of the variations we use in our daily lives.

courses.title: Introduction to Hispanic Linguistics
courses.description: Linguistic description of the Spanish language, including phonetics and phonology, morphology, syntax, history, and social and geographical variation.

courses.title: Languages in Contact
courses.description: An overview of languages in contact in the Spanish-speaking world. Students will learn theories of languages in contact, and how these languages behave in contact in their linguistic, social, political, and literary contexts. The language of instruction of this course is Spanish. All written work is completed in Spanish.

courses.title: Spanish Pronunciation and Phonetics
courses.description: Study of Spanish sounds, rhythms, and intonation; supervised pronunciation practice. Offered alternate years.

courses.title: Identities and Power
courses.description: This course investigates how individual and collective identities intersect with power, as expressed in cultural and linguistic contexts. The language of instruction of this course is Spanish. All written work is completed in Spanish.

courses.title: Literary Cultures
courses.description: This class introduces students to a variety of literary works from Latin American and Spain, mainly discussing the nineteenth and the twentieth centuries, with emphasis on their relationship to historical events and critical representation of Iberian and Latin American societies. All written work is completed in Spanish.

courses.title: Hispanic Cultures through Literature I
courses.description: Introduces students to a variety of texts written in the Hispanic world in their literary, artistic, and historical contexts, from 1100 to 1600.

courses.title: Hispanic Cultures through Literature II
courses.description: Introduces students to a variety of texts written in the Hispanic world in their literary, artistic, and historical contexts, from the 16th century to the Latin American independences.

courses.title: Hispanic Cultures through Literature III
courses.description: Introduces students to a variety of texts written in the Hispanic world in their literary, artistic, and historical contexts, from the revolutionary wars of the Nineteenth century to the Spanish Civil War.

courses.title: Hispanic Cultures through Literature IV
courses.description: Introduces students to a variety of texts written in the Hispanic world in their literary, artistic, and historical contexts, from the 20th century into the 21st.

courses.title: Latinx Culture and Society
courses.description: U.S. Latinx history, cultures, political movements, and human rights through transborder and bilingual cultural production (literature, film, visual arts and media).

courses.title: Introduction to Poetry
courses.description: Explores important aspects of Spanish and Latin American poetry; reading poems from different periods of Spanish and Spanish American literature. Emphasizes formal aspects and critical reading.

courses.title: Introduction to Theater
courses.description: Explores important aspects of Spanish theater; reading plays from different periods of Spanish and Spanish American literature. Emphasizes formal aspects and critical reading.

courses.title: Introduction to Narrative
courses.description: Explores important aspects of Spanish narrative; reading texts from different periods of Spanish and Spanish American literature. Emphasizes formal aspects and critical reading.

courses.title: Creative Writing in Spanish
courses.description: This course aims to expand the students’ creative capabilities in Spanish through writing exercises in poetry and fiction. Students will develop their literary sensitivity as well as their critical judgment and interpretative skills. Writing creatively diversifies the students' experience with the Spanish language.

courses.title: Literary Translation

courses.title: Sports Product Design and Business
courses.description: SPD 645 focuses on the fundamental business theories used to create, market, and sell sports products.

courses.title: Sports Product Materials and Manufacturing
courses.description: Explores the materials science, manufacturing, and sustainability theories applied in sports product design.

courses.title: Research Methodology and Innovation Process Studio
courses.description: Focuses on the design theories and methodologies used to design innovative sports products.

courses.title: Sports Product Design Studio I
courses.description: Explores the theories and creative problem-solving methods used to design solutions for sports soft goods. Theories of human thermoregulation, hydroprotection, support, aerodynamics, wearable technology, and kinematics.

courses.title: Sports Product Design Studio II
courses.description: Explores the theories and creative problem-solving methods used to design solutions for sports footwear. Mechanical theories of cushioning, stability, support, traction, and slipping-sliding.

courses.title: Sports Product Design Studio III
courses.description: Explores the theories and creative problem-solving methods used to design solutions for sports hard goods. Performance theories are considered to generate creative solutions.

courses.title: Innovative Project Strategy Development Studio
courses.description: First of a two-term capstone studio that examines the alignment of research, science, materials, and business theories to create an innovative sports product design opportunity.

courses.title: Collaborative Creation and Launch Studio
courses.description: Second of a two-term capstone studio that critically examines the alignment of design, materials, science, research, and business theories to create an innovative sports product design opportunity.

courses.description: An intensive experience, limited in scope and time, in which a group of students focus on skills development rather than content mastery.

courses.title: Terminal Project
courses.description: Recent topics include Special Education, Talented and Gifted.

courses.title: Foundations of Disability I
courses.description: Categorical and cross-categorical survey of information about exceptional children and youths. Topics include history, etiology, identification, classification, legislation, alternate program delivery systems.

courses.title: Diversity and Special Education
courses.description: Provides an overview of issues pertaining to diversity in special education. Topics include race, ethnicity, culture, and disability in a sociopolitical and historical context.

courses.title: Disrupting the School to Prison Pipeline
courses.description: Grounded in critical race theory and relying on research across different aspects of schooling and human development, students in this course will engage in rich discussions around practices that empower educators to disrupt the school-to-prison pipeline and bolster protective factors for youth, families, and communities.

courses.title: Applied Experience in Special Education
courses.description: This course is designed to provide practical experiences for students enrolled in the special education minor. Students engage with individuals with disabilities in early childhood settings, general or special education classrooms, or through community organizations.

courses.title: Special Education Mathematics Instruction
courses.description: Systematic instruction of mathematics skills for students with disabilities: assessment, planning, curriculum modification, diagnosis and remediation of persistent error patterns, evaluation.

courses.title: Behavior and Classroom Management
courses.description: Provides behavior management procedures for a variety of educational environments. Emphasizes functional assessment-based behavior support planning, classroom management, and principles of applied behavior analysis.

courses.title: Special Education Law
courses.description: This course provides students with knowledge of standards and ethics for professional practice, historical and current case law and legislation, and application of legal principles related to special education services in school settings.

courses.title: Introduction to Behavioral Disorders
courses.description: Introduces the characteristics and education of children and youth who have emotional and behavioral disorders.

courses.title: Advanced Behavior and Classroom Management
courses.description: Emphasizes functional assessment-based behavior support planning, individual education plans (IEPs), and effective behavior support systems for a variety of educational environments.

courses.title: Early Literacy for Diverse Learners
courses.description: Focuses on designing and evaluating instruction in the areas of reading and writing for preschool- to early elementary-aged students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 441 , SPED 442 , SPED 443 .

courses.title: Intermediate Literacy for Diverse Learners
courses.description: Focuses on designing and evaluating instruction in the areas of reading and writing for late elementary- to middle school-aged students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 440 , SPED 442 , SPED 443 .

courses.title: Adolescent Literacy for Diverse Learners
courses.description: Focuses on designing and evaluating instruction in the areas of reading and writing for middle school- and high school-aged students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 440 , SPED 441 , SPED 443 .

courses.title: Supporting Students with Low-Incidence Disability
courses.description: Provides skills on how to plan, coordinate, deliver, and evaluate evidence-based instruction for students with low-incidence disabilities. Sequence with SPED 440 , SPED 441 , SPED 442 .

courses.title: Principles of Applied Behavior Analysis
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with opportunities to learn and master basic behavior principles as a foundation to subsequent advanced courses. Students will master and build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis.

courses.title: Applied Behavior Analysis Assessment
courses.description: This course provides opportunities to build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis and the application of these principles to conduct functional behavior assessments including experimental functional analyses to determine the maintaining reinforcers for behavior and to plan for behavior change interventions. Students may enroll in either SPSY 671 or SPED 471 / SPED 571 , but cannot take both for credit.

courses.title: Behavior Change Group Settings
courses.description: This course provides opportunities to build fluency with the basic principles of applied behavior analysis and the application of these principles to develop, implement, and evaluate programs of behavior change in educational settings with a focus on positive behavior interventions and supports.

courses.title: Behavior Change Procedures 1
courses.description: The purpose of this course is to provide students with an overview of applied behavior analysis based behavior change systems and procedures that will include designing and implementing interventions based on fundamental elements of behavior change.

courses.title: Professional Practices: [Topic]
courses.description: Helps students critically assess their fieldwork and integrate fieldwork and course work in the wider context of the school experience.

courses.title: Early Intervention Practicum I
courses.description: Supervised field experience activities provide the experiential counterpart to instructive coursework and are a special emphasis of the Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education training program.

courses.title: Early Intervention Practicum II
courses.description: Supervised field experience activities provide the experiential counterpart to instructive coursework and are a special emphasis of the Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education (EI/ECSE) training program.

courses.title: Assessment in Special Education
courses.description: This course covers current concepts and issues related to assessment in special education and includes instruction and practice in administering standardized and curriculum-based assessments. Current issues in assessment such as assessing students from diverse backgrounds and response to intervention (RTI) will be covered.

courses.title: History of Special Education and Disability
courses.description: Historical context for contemporary issues in understanding and supporting the lives of people with disabilities and their families.

courses.title: Final Supervised Field Experience
courses.description: Repeatable.

courses.title: Grant Writing
courses.description: Provides structure and guidance in developing a grant proposal.

courses.title: Transition Planning and Instruction I
courses.description: Focuses on preparing youth for postsecondary education and training. Development of skills for using assessment-planning and content-instruction strategies to assist students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 634 .

courses.title: Transition Planning and Instruction II
courses.description: Focuses on strategies for improving employment and independent-living skills; using individualized assessment and planning strategies, curriculum and instructional strategies, and program delivery to assist students with disabilities. Sequence with SPED 633 .

courses.title: Student Teaching I K-12
courses.description: This course provides teacher candidates opportunities to apply evidence-based practices in Special Education and reading instruction. This course meets the requirements outlined by Teacher Standards and Practices Commission for the Reading Intervention endorsement. SPED 588 is taken concurrently.

courses.title: Student Teaching II K-12
courses.description: This course provides teacher candidates opportunities to apply knowledge of evidence-based practices in Special Education while teaching children/adults with disabilities. Teacher candidates are expected to independently generalize and apply foundational skills practiced during Student Teaching I and incorporate new skills. SPED 588 is taken concurrently.

courses.title: Student Teaching III K-12
courses.description: Provides practical experience in teaching students with disabilities in a public-school setting under the direction of cooperating teachers and university supervisors.

courses.title: Student Teaching K-12 Endorsement
courses.description: This course provides field-based practice for licensed teachers adding a Special Education endorsement, giving them opportunities to apply knowledge of evidence-based practices in Special Education while teaching children/adults with disabilities. Students take SPED 588 concurrently.

courses.title: Student Teaching Reading Endorsement
courses.description: This course provides field-based practice in reading instruction for licensed teachers adding a Reading Intervention endorsement. It provides opportunities to apply evidence-based practices in reading to design, deliver, and improve reading instruction for all students, including English learners and those with reading difficulties and dyslexia.

courses.title: Design of Instruction
courses.description: Design, development, and evaluation of instructional materials for children with disabilities. Emphasis on analysis and construction of instructional sequences for various learning tasks.

courses.title: Philosophy of Applied Behavior Analysis
courses.description: Introduces students to the philosophical foundations of radical behaviorism, classic and contemporary readings in behavior analysis. Students will be prepared to understand theoretical approaches to understanding behavior, and to interpret behavior in terms of the concepts and principles of behavior analysis.

courses.title: Experimental Research in Applied Behavior Analysis
courses.description: This online graduate level course introduces students to the experimental analysis of behavior. This course will cover basic research with humans and non-human animals that explores behavior analytic principles and mechanisms of learning and behavior.

courses.title: Experimental Research Behavior Analysis Lab
courses.description: This online graduate level course provides students who have taken the experimental analysis of behavior course opportunities to apply knowledge and interpret the results of simulated experiments. This course will model and then have students engage in their own simulated experiments.

courses.title: Ethics in Applied Behavior Analysis
courses.description: This course focuses on the Behavioral Analyst Certification Board Professional and Ethical Compliance Code. It provides a basic knowledge of the Ethical Code as well as different problem-solving frameworks to evaluate difficult situations that may arise during their practice.

courses.title: Single Case Research Design
courses.description: This course focuses on basic and complex single-case experimental design strategies and general procedures, as well as on issues related to clinician-researchers conducting and analyzing single-case research in applied settings.

courses.title: Behavior Change Procedures II
courses.description: This course provides students with an overview of applied behavior analysis based behavior change systems and procedures that will include designing and implementing interventions based on fundamental elements of behavior change. Emphasis is on decreasing, eliminating, and replacing behavior that is challenging to the context.

courses.title: Personnel Management
courses.description: This course focuses on the Behavioral Analyst Certification Board (BACB) Professional and Ethical Compliance Code related to personnel management. Emphasis is consultation strategies that board certified behavior analysts use to liaison with medical professionals, parents and other family members, and trainees in applied behavior analysis.

courses.title: Foundations in Early Childhood and Early Intervention
courses.description: Conceptual underpinnings and practical application of an approach to early intervention that links assessment, intervention, and evaluation.

courses.title: Early Intervention for Diverse Families
courses.description: Covers procedures for family assessment, intervention, and evaluation. Addresses adult communication and management strategies.

courses.title: Assessment and Evaluation
courses.description: Presents assessment and evaluation materials used in early intervention programs and provides methods for using these materials.

courses.title: Curriculum in Early Childhood and Early Intervention
courses.description: Presents curricular materials covering development from birth to six years. Discusses procedures for use and modification.

courses.title: Autism in Early Intervention
courses.description: Information is given regarding the etiology of autism, diagnostic/evaluation procedures and current evidence-based strategies for supporting young children with autism spectrum disorder and their families.

courses.title: Early Intervention Methods I
courses.description: Provide a foundation for understanding the components of a linked system of intervention in Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education, where practitioners conduct assessments, plan and implement intervention, assess their intervention, and monitor student progress. Sequence with SPED 688 , SPED 689 .

courses.title: Early Intervention Methods II
courses.description: The Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education Individualized Family Service Plan, Individualized Education Program Methods II course is the second of three Methods courses to be taken by EI/ECSE students across the year. Sequence with SPED 687 , SPED 689 .

courses.title: Early Intervention Methods III
courses.description: Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education (EI/ECSE) Methods III is the final course in the EI/ECSE Methods series. It is designed to address specific practices that students will use as professionals in EI/ECSE settings. Sequence with SPED 687 , SPED 688 .

courses.title: Professional Practices in Early Intervention I
courses.description: During this seminar students will focus on the structure and process of teaming within Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education systems. Additionally, students will participate in team meetings alongside their peers to reflect on and discuss issues related to their practicum experiences.

courses.title: Professional Practices in Early Intervention II
courses.description: During this seminar students will focus on Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education practices related to transition, intervention planning, implementation of interventions, self-assessment and reflection of practices, and monitoring child progress.

courses.title: Professional Practices in Early Intervention III
courses.description: During this seminar students will focus on Early Intervention/Early Childhood Special Education practices related to student teaching. The purpose of Professional Practices III is to support students in having a positive and productive student teaching experience.
